Get started → AI Expert Network
How It Works:

Step 2: Complete the 10 Core Modules Watch all 10 training modules end-to-end. These cover everything you need to succeed as an AI consultant on the platform. (We have tracking software that notifies us when you've completed the course.)

programs.title: How to Sell AI Solutions
programs.description: Sell pre-made AI solutions that can actually make money

lessons.title: 🎥 WATCH ME FIRST
lessons.description:
This video walks through my core principles when it comes to selling AI solutions in general.
And this is the link to the Miro board mentioned in the video:
Miro Explanation

lessons.title: 1) The Unorthodox Guide to Selling AI Solutions
lessons.description:
(& Creating Offers)
Mastering AI Sales Strategies: Practical Insights for Success 🚀
Foundational Sales Strategy: Avoid cookie-cutter outreach; instead, leverage your industry-specific experience and social proof to sell AI solutions. Free School communities, hackathons, and freelancing platforms like Upwork are great starting points for finding high-intent clients and collaborators.Creative Lead Generation: Explore non-traditional strategies like building Trojan Horse apps, offering high-value lead magnets (e.g., Chrome extensions or vibe-coded demos), and participating in niche-specific meetups or influencer collaborations to increase inbound interest and establish trust.Service & Pricing Structures: Begin with AI audits (free or paid), consulting retainers, and workshops. Bundle these into full-service offers (e.g., white-glove automation or MVP apps). Be mindful of pricing tiers, customer data quality, and potential churn after 2–3 months of retainer work.Business Models & Positioning: Focus on becoming a trusted problem-solver, not just an automation builder. Reposition yourself as a strategist, educator, or even AI recruiter to stand out in a commoditized space. Build modular, repeatable assets (e.g., starter kits, mockups, reusable workflows) for scalability.Financial Laddering & Profit Focus: Avoid glorifying revenue without understanding costs. Mix income sources (audits, workshops, MRR, etc.) to reach revenue goals like $15K/month, but always track profit margins. Aim for sustainable growth by delegating smartly and protecting your reputation with quality work.

lessons.title: 2) How to Conduct an AI Audit (Part 1)
lessons.description:
How to Conduct An AI Audit (that actually wins business) - PART 1
Four-Step AI Audit Framework: The process starts with deep research on the company using tools like Claude, Perplexity, and Plexi Labs, followed by creating tailored workflows in advance to wow the client and differentiate from competitors.Lead Generation Strategy: The AI audit can serve as a Trojan horse—offered for free or low-cost to build trust and potentially convert into long-term paid work. Honesty about experience and underpricing experimental work is emphasized as good business karma.Audit Form & Prompting Structure: A form collects detailed inputs about a company’s operations, tech stack, and pain points. This info feeds into customized system prompts (e.g., automation consultant personas) to auto-generate pain point analyses, workflow suggestions, and automation roadmaps.Prebuilt Workflow Examples: The creator demonstrates how to use prompt tools to generate workflows using platforms like n8n and make.com. These workflows can be presented during client calls as ready-made solutions, even before charging.Looking Impressive with Automation: By combining research, prompt-generated workflows, and a presentation deck (like Gamma), you appear highly competent and indispensable—setting yourself apart in a saturated AI space. Part 2 will cover deliverables.

lessons.title: 3) How to Conduct an AI Audit (Part 2)
4) Effective Pricing Strategies for AI Slns 💰
lessons.description:
How to Price AI Solutions the Data-Driven way (real numbers)
AI Audits: Start free to gain experience and build trust. Paid audits can range from $600 to $3,000, with an average around $1,400 depending on reputation and deliverables like decks and workflows.Custom GPTs & ChatGPT Projects: These range from $250 to $4,000, with higher pricing justified by adding custom actions or integrating automation tools like N8N or Make. Prior experience and delivery confidence are key.Consulting Rates: Started at $50/hour, now up to $500/hour. Lower prices help solve the “cold start problem.” Retainers last around 2–3 months, with typical churn due to early value saturation.Custom Apps & Automations: Custom-coded apps can reach $500K+ if production-grade, secure, and client-trusted. Automations (e.g., via N8N) can be priced per flow or bundled—value-based pricing tied to business impact is encouraged.Workshops & Arbitrage: Workshops command $2,500–$25,000 depending on experience and brand authority. Some AI services (like voice agents) are becoming commoditized, but early movers benefit from short-term pricing arbitrage.

lessons.title: 5) Your First Dollar in AI
lessons.description: This is an intro to an AI course where the instructor lays out a simple promise: if you actually apply what's taught over the next few hours, you'll be equipped to make your first dollars online and build a real AI business. The main caveat is that watching alone won't cut it. You have to take action, because no matter how many times you rewatch the content, nothing changes if nothing changes. The goal is to keep things concise and give you everything you need to get started.

lessons.title: 6) The Real Freedom This Business Solves
lessons.description: The speaker explains that this course is designed for people who want time, location, and financial freedom rather than traditional job stability. They describe wanting full control over their schedule, working without weekends, and being able to earn income from anywhere in the world with just a computer and internet. While money alone is not positioned as happiness, it is framed as a powerful tool that makes every problem easier to handle. The course is aimed at entrepreneurs willing to set aside ego, learn from those who have already succeeded, and focus on building a practical, high income skill instead of chasing billion dollar dreams. The core message is about building your own life and business on your terms rather than continuing to build someone else’s.

lessons.title: 7) Why AI Consulting Is the Smart Entry Point
lessons.description: The speaker explains that AI consulting follows the same pattern as past technology waves, where new platforms created massive opportunities for businesses that helped others adopt them, from the internet to mobile apps to social media. AI is framed as early, unsaturated, and full of obvious gaps, with many businesses still failing to respond to customers outside normal hours or using any form of automation at all. This creates a clear opportunity to provide immediate value without heavy competition. The model is also attractive because risk is spread out rather than controlled by a single platform or company, making it more resilient than dropshipping or ad based businesses. Ultimately, the appeal of AI consulting is that success depends mainly on execution and skill, not on gatekeepers or external platforms shutting you down.

lessons.title: 8) Why AI Chatbots Fix Broken Websites
lessons.description: The speaker explains that most business websites overwhelm visitors with information while still failing to answer the one question a person actually cares about, especially given that users spend barely a minute on a site. Long forms and cluttered pages push people away instead of helping them, which creates a clear gap in how businesses communicate with potential customers. The solution presented is an AI chatbot trained on everything about the business that allows visitors to ask direct questions and get personalized answers instantly. This approach delivers one to one service at all hours without the cost of hiring staff and helps businesses make a meaningful leap forward in how they serve customers. The speaker emphasizes that while AI chatbots may seem common online, most real businesses still do not use them, making this a practical and scalable opportunity.

lessons.title: 9) Trust Comes Before Big AI Deals
lessons.description: The speaker explains that the foundation of a successful AI agency is trust, not chasing high ticket sales too early. Selling services is different from selling physical products because clients must believe you will deliver results, and that belief only comes from repeated proof over time. The recommended approach is to start with simple, low cost solutions that are easy for business owners to say yes to, use those early wins to build credibility, and spend as much time as possible working closely with clients. As trust grows, larger and more complex problems naturally surface, creating opportunities for higher value retainers and long term relationships. The core idea is that patience and consistency outperform aggressive selling, and most people fail because they try to skip the trust building phase entirely.

lessons.title: 10) How I Built an AI Agency From Scratch
lessons.description: The speaker explains that this course shares the exact model they used to scale an AI agency to over a million dollars a year, stressing that it is based on real experience rather than theory. They recount their path from heavy debt, exhausting jobs, and multiple failed businesses to early success on Amazon, followed by a sudden collapse when their account was shut down, which taught them the danger of relying on a single platform. That failure became the motivation to find a more resilient business model, leading them to AI services that required no inventory, avoided platform dependency, and could be duplicated quickly. Starting with small monthly payments for AI chatbots, they built recurring revenue, partnered with the right people, and ultimately gained the freedom to work when and where they want. The story is meant to show that while the journey is difficult and full of setbacks, the underlying approach is simple, achievable, and focused on building a life that does not revolve around weekends or escape.

lessons.title: 11) Why Picking a Niche Makes Everything Easier
lessons.description: The speaker explains that choosing a specific niche is critical because it allows knowledge, conversations, and solutions to compound over time instead of starting from zero with every new client. By focusing on one industry, the same problems, language, and needs show up repeatedly, which improves sales confidence, delivery quality, and pricing power. Specialization also makes it possible to charge more for the same underlying solution simply by tailoring it to a defined audience. When choosing a niche, the key criteria are whether the industry has money, whether decision makers are accessible, and whether you can realistically solve a problem for them. The message closes with a strong emphasis on consistency, patience, and commitment, urging people to stop overthinking, pick a niche, stick with it for at least a year, and let progress compound over time.

lessons.title: 12) The Simple Tech Stack You Actually Need
lessons.description: The speaker walks through the practical setup needed to get started, emphasizing simplicity and speed over perfection. On the business side, the essentials are basic payment tools like Stripe and Wise, a simple one page website with a domain, and optionally a YouTube channel, while stressing that branding, logos, and website design matter far less than most people think. On the product side, the core tools are OpenAI as the intelligence layer and an automation platform like n8n to connect the AI to a usable front end. For outreach and sales, the focus is on choosing a lightweight CRM approach, either starting lean with Google Sheets and a phone tool or upgrading later to a full system like GoHighLevel once traction exists. The overall message is to avoid overengineering early, understand how the pieces fit together, and only invest time and money where it directly supports getting clients and delivering results.

lessons.title: 13) How to Prep for Your First Sales Call
lessons.description: The speaker explains what to do once a prospect books a meeting, focusing on reducing no shows and making a strong first impression. The process starts with sending both an email invite and a confirmation text that asks for a clear yes, which filters out people who are not serious. Before the call, the key move is to go above and beyond by building a free demo AI chatbot specifically for the prospect’s website, even if they have not paid yet. This preparation shows effort, builds trust, and immediately demonstrates value in a tangible way. The overall goal is to arrive on the call fully prepared, differentiated from competitors, and positioned as someone who takes the client’s business seriously.

lessons.title: 14) Turning Booked Calls Into Real Opportunities
lessons.description: The speaker explains how to handle the moment a prospect books a call, focusing on reducing no shows and setting up a strong demo. The process starts with sending both an email invite and a confirmation text that asks for a clear yes, which filters out people who are not serious and increases attendance. Before the call, the key move is to prepare a personalized demo by building a simple AI chatbot trained on the prospect’s website, even if they have not paid yet. This extra effort shows initiative, builds trust quickly, and makes the value of the service obvious during the call. The goal is to arrive prepared, professional, and clearly differentiated by showing real work instead of just talking about possibilities.

lessons.title: 15) Delivering, Upselling, and Scaling Your Agency
lessons.description: The speaker closes the course by explaining how to deliver results after a client says yes and how to turn that delivery into long term growth. Contracts are positioned as secondary to trust and speed, with the real priority being fast delivery by duplicating workflows, training the AI on the client’s business, testing it, and getting it live quickly. After delivery, the focus shifts to recurring check ins, improving performance, and proactively helping clients with related problems, even small ones, to deepen the relationship. These wins lead to testimonials, which lower risk for future prospects and allow higher pricing over time. The final takeaway is simple: finish the course, pick a niche, build an agent, start outreach, and focus on execution rather than overthinking the next step.

lessons.title: 16) A Simple System to Manage Cold Outreach
lessons.description: The speaker explains how to stay organized while doing high volume cold calling by using a simple Google Sheets system instead of overcomplicated tools. Each contact is color coded to track outcomes, with yellow for no answer, red for not interested, blue for callbacks, and green for booked meetings, making it easy to see what needs follow up at a glance. The daily workflow is to call through a list of 75 businesses, double dial each number, update their status, then add another 75 leads for the next day. This repeatable process, done consistently Monday through Friday, keeps momentum high and prevents leads from falling through the cracks. The emphasis is on discipline, simplicity, and showing up daily rather than trying to optimize too early.

lessons.title: 17) How to Build a Cold Call List That Converts
lessons.description: The speaker breaks down a practical outreach system focused on cold calling as the fastest way to get feedback, improve sales skills, and land early clients. The process starts with using Google Maps to find businesses in a chosen niche, ideally with moderate review counts, then qualifying them by checking their website, traffic estimates, and whether they lack basic engagement tools like chat or texting. The Better Business Bureau is used as a shortcut to identify business owners and bypass gatekeepers by calling with a specific name. Each qualified business is added to a simple call list with notes on traffic, website gaps, and call status. The emphasis is on consistency and volume, building a list of around 75 businesses per day, and focusing on real conversations rather than overthinking channels or tools.

lessons.title: 18) The Anatomy of a Winning Cold Call
lessons.description: The speaker breaks down how to approach cold calls with clarity, honesty, and a single objective, which is booking the meeting rather than selling the product. Because cold calls interrupt people unexpectedly, the key is to ask permission early, be upfront that it is a cold call, and keep the conversation short and respectful. Instead of explaining features or technology, the focus should be on outcomes like saving time or making more money and creating enough curiosity to justify a follow up demo. The call works best when it feels human, avoids buzzwords, and clearly guides the conversation toward scheduling a meeting as soon as possible. With repetition, confidence grows, and the process becomes a simple, repeatable way to open real sales conversations.

lessons.title: 19) How to Run a Calm, Effective Sales Call
lessons.description: The speaker explains that a successful sales call is less about pitching and more about listening, understanding, and guiding the conversation naturally. Instead of scripted selling or forced small talk, the focus should be on asking good questions, matching the prospect’s level of knowledge, and positioning yourself as a helpful consultant rather than a salesperson. By discussing outcomes in plain language, showing the product in action, and involving the prospect in shaping the solution, trust is built without pressure. Because the initial offer is low risk, the seller can stay relaxed and detached from the outcome, which actually increases confidence and credibility. The overall goal is to build a relationship, deliver value early, and create space for long term collaboration rather than forcing a single sale.

lessons.title: 20) Building Your First AI Agent Step by Step
lessons.description: The speaker walks through the practical process of building an AI chatbot using n8n, OpenAI, and a simple chat widget, showing how all the pieces connect from backend to frontend. They explain how to import a ready made workflow, connect it to an OpenAI model with an API key, and configure the agent’s behavior using a clear prompting framework that defines context, actions, rules, output style, and safety. The agent is trained by uploading business data like scraped website content, allowing it to answer questions, capture leads, and route customer service requests appropriately. The workflow is designed to be duplicated easily for new clients by swapping prompts, data, and notification details. Finally, the chatbot is deployed through a front end widget that can be embedded on any website, demonstrating how quickly a functional, client ready AI agent can be built and customized.

lessons.title: Meeting Transcript to LinkedIn Post
lessons.description: An automation service that captures transcripts from Firefly meetings and transforms them into LinkedIn post drafts. This process not only helps in generating content quickly after client meetings but also ensures that the posts reflect the insights shared during those discussions.

lessons.title: How to Sell AI Solutions Masterclass
lessons.description:
Presented by Cleve - 20+ Years Sales Experience
---
The CALM Framework
- Curious - Lead with questions, not pitches. Understand the client's real issue
- Authentic - Drop the robotic scripts. Be yourself
- Listening - Sales is not manipulative when you're genuinely helping. View sales as service
- Close with ease - Focus on where you lose sales (discovery), not just the close
---
Discovery Calls: Where 80% of Sales Happen
Key insight: If you're doing all the talking, you're guessing what they need. If they're talking, they're telling you what they need.
Surface Questions vs. Root Questions
Surface (Low Value):
- Budget questions
- Basic rapport
- General pain points
Root (High Value):
- "What's the cost of inaction today?"
- "Who else is impacted by this bottleneck?"
- "Why now? Why is this urgent?"
Hidden Pain Points
- Clients often don't know their actual problem (they see smoke, not the fire)
- Some play cards close to their chest (been burned before, or already have a competitor in mind)
- Ask: "What are you really trying to achieve?" and "What's it actually costing you?"
---
Funnel Stages & How to Identify Them
Top of Funnel (Problem Aware)
- Questions to ask: "What made you reach out now?" / "What happens if you don't solve this in 6 months?"
- Your role: Teacher - validate pain, educate on root causes
Middle of Funnel (Solution Seeking)
- Questions to ask: "Why do you think Method X is the right fit?" / "What metrics would you like to see change?"
- Your role: Strategic Advisor - differentiate yourself, sell the transformation
Bottom of Funnel (Decision Ready)
- Questions to ask: "What happens if we delay the start by 30 days?"
- Your role: Implementation Partner - handle logistics, make it easy
---
Reading the Room: 5 Energy Signals
1. Voice - Getting quieter? Getting excited? The "lean in"?
2. Language Patterns
- "I should..." = External, non-committal
- "I need to..." = Motivated
- "I'll try..." = Possibly could
- "I will..." = Commitment
3. Story vs. Solution Mode - Are they sharing emotion or just logistics?
4. Resistance Patterns - "What if...", "I've tried...", "That's great, but..."
5. Enrollment Language - "If I do this...", "When I get access..." = STOP TALKING, they've sold themselves
---
Three Cognitive Biases That Drive Buying
1. Loss Aversion - Pain of losing > pleasure of gaining
- Reframe: "Every week you wait is another $5K client slipping through"
2. Social Proof - They want to know others have done it
- Reframe: "This is the same roadblock my client hit last month..."
3. Sunk Cost - How much more will they waste trying alone?
- Reframe: "You've already invested X hours figuring this out..."
---
The Three-Step Closing Sequence
1. Recap the solution - Mirror back what you heard using their words
2. Tie to their goals - "This will help you achieve X"
3. Assumptive close - "When would you like to get started? I have Tuesday or Thursday next week"
Never ask: "What do you think? Should we pursue this?" - You just handed them all the power back.
---
Critical Rules
- Always Book a Meeting from a Meeting (BAMFAM) - Give 2 specific options, not "when works for you?"
- "Let me think about it" with no next step = You're getting ghosted
- Clients who negotiate hard on price = Your worst clients - Those who sign without haggling become your best advocates
- Build rapport in 2-3 minutes max - Then move to substance
- Mirror their energy - High energy person? Match it. Internal processor? Slow down
---
Biggest Mistakes People Make
1. Talking too much instead of listening
2. Selling features instead of transformation
3. Leaving calls without defined next steps
4. Giving pricing before establishing value
5. Chasing every lead instead of qualifying
---
When Asked for Price Too Early
Response: "I could tell you a million dollars right now, but I need to understand what's happening so I can give you an accurate price. Some clients spend $40, some spend $40,000 - it depends on what you need."
---
Platform Strategy by Funnel Stage
- YouTube - Middle + Bottom (trust building, tutorials)
- LinkedIn - Middle + Bottom (thought leadership)
- Email (your list) - Middle + Bottom (nurturing)
- TikTok/Instagram - Top of Funnel only

programs.title: What AI Software I Use
programs.description: The exact AI tools, software, and services I use to run Prompt Advisers.

lessons.title: AWS
lessons.description:
I use AWS for all our cloud infrastructure. The AI Expert Network runs on ECS and various other services.
The security angle matters for client work. We access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, which means sensitive data stays within our AWS environment with proper encryption and compliance. When we're working on client projects with sensitive data, we connect Claude Code through our AWS infrastructure rather than going directly to Anthropic's API.
I also used to work at Amazon, so I know this stack well. I know where the gotchas are and how to debug things when they break. That familiarity has value.

lessons.title: Claude Code
lessons.description:
My go-to AI programming CLI. Anthropic is a focused company - they didn't build image models, they didn't chase video models, they picked one lane and went deep on it. That discipline shows in the product.
They keep shipping and they keep listening to the dev and non-dev community. That feedback loop is tight, and it's hard to lose when you're that responsive.
Claude Code has replaced my need for ChatGPT entirely. I cancelled that subscription. The quality of output for coding tasks is better, and running it in my terminal means I'm not switching between browser tabs and my IDE. Everything lives where I'm already working.

lessons.title: Claude AI (Web)
lessons.description:
The fallback for quick things when I'm away from my setup.
If I'm on my phone or on a computer without my dev environment and I need to quickly ask Claude something, I'll use the web interface. It's not my primary way of working - Claude Code is - but for quick and dirty stuff on the go, it works.

lessons.title: Cursor
lessons.description:
My favourite IDE right now.
I can run Claude Code directly in the terminal. I can run the Claude Code VS Code extension inside it. For $20/month I get access to other AI models like Gemini if I need them.
I'm aware of Windsurf and I'm keeping tabs on it, but Cursor has more tenure. More users have stress-tested it. More edge cases have been found and fixed. I stick with products that have more shots on goal.
I stay away from shiny new tools no matter how good they seem on the surface. They haven't been truly tested yet. Early reports of certain AI CLIs destroying people's C drives or hitting undocumented rate limits - that's the kind of thing that only surfaces after real usage at scale. I'd rather wait.

lessons.title: DemoPro
lessons.description:
The pointing and highlighting tool I use in my YouTube videos.
It lets me point directly at what I'm talking about instead of describing where something is on screen. Small thing, but it makes tutorials significantly easier to follow.

lessons.title: Gemini + AI Studio
lessons.description:
I pay for Gemini Pro so I can upload videos. I use the Gemini APIs extensively and AI Studio is genuinely underrated as a tool for experimentation.
Google has done a better job than OpenAI at generalizing for the mass market. And unlike OpenAI, they don't rent infrastructure - they own their TPUs, GPUs, and data centers. That's a structural advantage that compounds over time.
After a lot of testing, I've landed on this:
- Gemini = excellent at front-end work
- Claude = excellent at back-end work
I use both for what they're best at.
Gemini also has developer-friendly rate limits and a 1M+ token context window without too much degradation. I use it to upload Loom videos and generate SOPs from them - it watches what I do and documents it. That alone has been worth the subscription.

lessons.title: Firecrawl
lessons.description:
One of the few APIs I pay for without second-guessing it.
It handles the messy reality of modern web scraping - JavaScript-rendered pages, dynamic content, all the stuff that breaks basic scrapers. Their Agent endpoint is particularly useful for complex multi-page scraping tasks.
If you're building anything that needs to pull data from the web, this is the one I'd recommend.

lessons.title: Make.com
lessons.description:
I still have some workflows running on Make. It's reliable and the interface is cleaner than n8n - more visually intuitive.
We're migrating things to n8n for the flexibility and cost savings, but I won't pretend Make is bad. It's a solid product.
If you're non-technical and want something that looks intuitive, Make works well.

lessons.title: MCP Servers + Extensions
lessons.description:
I'm pretty particular about using as few MCP servers as possible with Claude Code. I like to keep it vanilla. The more MCPs you add, the more potential errors that pop up for no reason. Keep it simple.
My go-tos:
- Supabase MCP - Direct database access from Claude Code. Create tables, run queries, deploy edge functions without leaving the terminal.
- Vercel MCP - For deployment workflows. Sometimes I'll run this through Cursor instead of Claude Code because it's more reliable there.
- Firecrawl MCP - Web scraping and search directly from Claude Code.
- Docker MCP - From the Docker MCP catalog, runs locally. Integrates really well into Cursor.
Chrome MCP - I used to use this, but the latest version of Claude Code has browser capabilities built-in now, so I don't need it anymore.
I've also built my own MCP servers when I needed something specific. Built a Perplexity MCP before, but ended up switching to one from the Docker MCP catalog that runs locally.
On using MCPs in Cursor:
Sometimes if I want to go back and forth with something like Vercel, I'll use Cursor with Claude Sonnet 4 or even Haiku on the side. Hooking up an MCP server to Cursor is basically a one-click operation. It's doable in Claude Code too, but I find it more reliable in Cursor for certain tasks.
Extensions
The philosophy: keep Claude Code as vanilla as possible. Fewer moving parts, fewer random errors.
I keep this minimal too.
- Claude Code for VS Code extension - Runs inside Cursor. Useful for quick inline stuff while I have the full Claude Code terminal running for bigger tasks.
- Google Colab extension - For machine learning work where I have notebooks running online. Lets me run and interact with those notebooks from my local environment instead of switching to the browser.
That's it. I don't load up on extensions. Same philosophy as MCPs - fewer moving parts, fewer problems.

lessons.title: n8n
lessons.description:
If you're going to use visual workflow automation, n8n is my pick over Make and Zapier.
It's cheaper at scale, especially if you self-host. It's more flexible - custom code nodes, complex branching, self-hosting for data privacy. The community is responsive and actually ships based on feedback.
I'll be honest though: I'm questioning how much automation should live in n8n versus just being code. Claude Code can write a script that does the same thing, and sometimes that script is simpler to debug and maintain than a visual workflow. Worth thinking about for your own use case.

lessons.title: OpenRouter
lessons.description:
A unified API for accessing multiple AI models through one interface.
I use it for testing - running the same prompt against different models to see which performs best for a specific task. Useful when you want to validate that you're using the right model for the job, and useful for cost optimization when a smaller model might be sufficient.

lessons.title: Screen Studio
lessons.description:
What I use to record YouTube videos.
Auto-zoom on clicks, good audio processing, makes screen recordings look professional without much effort. If you're creating tutorial content, this handles a lot of the polish automatically.

lessons.title: Splashtop Business
lessons.description:
Remote desktop access to my laptop.
Claude Code web isn't where I need it to be for serious work yet. So when I'm away from my desk and need to run something in Claude Code properly, I remote into my laptop through Splashtop and use the full terminal experience.
Not glamorous, but it solves a real problem.

lessons.title: Supabase
lessons.description:
My go-to database for MVPs and internal products.
RAG has never been something I struggled with because Supabase makes it straightforward. Basic vector search, hybrid RAG, whatever you need - the primitives are there and they compose well.
The real value is the Supabase MCP combined with Claude Code. I can be in my terminal and Claude Code can directly modify tables, create functions, spin up edge functions, and query data. All in one conversation without leaving the terminal.
Cost is about $10 per project instance. For Postgres, auth, storage, edge functions, and vector search - that's reasonable for what you get.

lessons.title: Wispr Flow
lessons.description:
Dictation software that has genuinely changed how I work.
Context matters when working with AI. The more context you provide, the better the output. Typing is slow - maybe 60-80 words per minute. Speaking is 150+ words per minute without trying.
I use Wispr Flow for vibe coding, writing first drafts, documentation, emails. It lets me give richer context faster. If you do knowledge work and you're not using dictation, you're leaving speed on the table.
I have an affiliate link because I actually use this daily and stand by it.

programs.title: Everything ClaudeClaw
programs.description: Find everything related to the community ClaudeClaw build.

lessons.title: Link to Github Repo + Read Me
lessons.description:
This is the latest version of the repo:
https://github.com/earlyaidopters/claudeclaw

programs.title: Claude Code Zero-to-Hero
programs.description: Learning Claude Code for non-technical and technical folks

lessons.title: 1) Getting Started & Environment Setup
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-01-getting-started
Alright so this is where it all starts. We're gonna get Claude Code installed, set up your environment, and make sure everything is runnin' smooth. The big thing I teach here is what I call the "brilliant but blind" principle  - basically Claude is insanely capable, but it's only as good as the context you give it. So we cover how to structure your project, manage your API keys safely, and set things up so Claude actually understands what you're workin' with.
What You'll Learn:
How to install and verify Claude Code on your machineWhy project structure matters (and how it affects Claude's output)Managing API keys the right way so you don't accidentally expose themSetting up your first project with the proper folder structureThe mental model behind how Claude Code actually thinks
Key Takeaway: Think of Claude Code like having a senior dev on your team  - but you gotta give it the right context first. This module shows you exactly how to do that.

lessons.title: 2) Slash Commands
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-02-commands
Commands are one of the biggest game changers in Claude Code. Instead of typing out the same instructions every single time, you save them as a slash command and reuse them whenever you want. I'm talkin' one-click workflows that you build once and use forever. This module teaches you how to create, organize, and use custom commands from scratch.
What You'll Learn:
What slash commands are and why they're a huge time-saverHow to create your own custom commands from scratchThe template pattern that keeps your results consistentHow to install commands globally so they work across all your projects9 production-ready commands are included in the repo so you can start usin' them right awayKey Takeaway: Once you start using commands, you're never gonna go back to manually typing out the same prompts over and over. Trust me on this one.

lessons.title: 3) The Core Workflow
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-03-core-workflow
This is honestly the most important module in the entire course. You're gonna learn the 5-phase workflow that takes Claude Code from "just a chatbot" to a legit production-ready development partner: Plan → Build → Validate → Review → Commit. Whether you're fixin' a quick bug or buildin' out a full feature, this workflow keeps everything organized and your code quality high.
What You'll Learn:
The 5-phase workflow: Plan, Build, Validate, Review, CommitHow to use spec files to track what you're building and whyThree workflow recipes depending on the situation: Quick Fix, Standard Feature, and Full WorkflowVisual verification with Playwright (Claude can actually see your app!)How the specs folder system works for tracking your progress
Key Takeaway: This workflow is the difference between vaguely askin' Claude for help and consistently shipping quality work. It's how you go from amateur to professional use of Claude Code.

lessons.title: 4) MCPs (Model Context Protocol)
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-04-mcp
MCPs (Model Context Protocol servers) are what give Claude Code superpowers. They let Claude interact with databases, browsers, APIs, and external services  - way beyond just reading and writing files. This module covers what MCPs are, how to install them, how to evaluate whether they're safe to use, and you'll even build your own simple ones
What You'll Learn:
What MCPs are and the three capabilities they bring to the table (Tools, Resources, Prompts)How to install and configure MCPs for your projectsSecurity evaluation: how to tell if an MCP is legit and safeThree custom MCPs included in the repo: a memory system, a prompt library, and a work journalRecommended MCPs for common use cases like Playwright, Supabase, Notion, and SlackKey Takeaway: MCPs are how you take Claude Code from a code assistant to a full-on automation platform. Think of them as plugins that let Claude talk to pretty much anything.

lessons.title: 5) Skills
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-05-skills
Skills are the next level up from commands. Commands are single-file prompts, but skills are full multi-file packages that combine instructions, templates, and even MCPs into seriously powerful, reusable workflows. This module shows you how to use existing skills and create your own  - we're talkin' generating documents, extracting data, automating complex stuff, all that.
What You'll Learn:
The difference between commands and skills (and when to use each one)How skills are actually structured under the hood5 production-ready skills included: File Factory (creates PPTX, DOCX, XLSX, PDF), Video Transcript Extractor, Time Travel (git checkpoints), MCP Builder, and Skill CreatorA showcase folder with 63 example outputs so you can see what these skills actually produceAccess to 21,000+ community-built skills you can plug in
Key Takeaway: Skills let you package up complex workflows into something anyone can trigger with a single command. They're how you scale your Claude Code setup across a team.

lessons.title: 6) Sub-Agents
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-06-subagents
Subagents are where things get really cool. You can have Claude spin up specialized workers for specific tasks  - a code reviewer, a test runner, a doc generator  - all runnin' independently without losing track of the main work. This module teaches you the "one agent, one prompt, one purpose" principle and shows you how to build custom agents for your own workflow.
What You'll Learn:
What subagents are and the "one agent, one prompt, one purpose" ruleWhen you should delegate to a subagent vs. just do the work directlyHow to create custom agents tailored to your specific needs3 agents included in the repo: Code Reviewer, Test Runner, and Doc GeneratorHow to track what your subagents are doing with logging hooks
Key Takeaway: Subagents are like having a team of specialists on call. Instead of one agent tryin' to do everything, you delegate tasks to purpose-built agents that each do one thing really well.

lessons.title: 7) Security Essentials with Hooks
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-07-security
Alright so if you're gonna give an AI access to your codebase, you need guardrails. This module covers hooks  - automated scripts that run before or after Claude takes actions  - and how to use them to protect sensitive files, prevent accidental deletions, and catch exposed credentials. You'll set up three protection tiers that keep your projects safe without slowin' you down.
What You'll Learn:
What hooks are and how they protect your work (PreToolUse and PostToolUse)Three protection tiers: Zero Access, Read Only, and No DeleteHow to install security hooks at both global and project levelsWhat to do if credentials get exposed (detection and response)A complete Security Audit skill with quick checks and full audits
Key Takeaway: Security isn't optional  - it's table stakes. The beauty of these hooks is they run automatically in the background, so you get protection without even having to think about it.

lessons.title: 8) GitHub Integration and Mobile Workflows
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-08-github-integration
This one is honestly one of the coolest modules. You're gonna connect Claude Code directly to GitHub so you can create an issue from your phone, have Claude implement the solution, and then review the pull request when you're back at your desk. It's a mobile-first workflow that lets you be productive from literally anywhere.
What You'll Learn:
GitHub CLI setup and authenticationInstalling Claude's official GitHub AppUsing @claude in issues to kick off automated workUsing @claude in pull requests for automated code reviewsThe full mobile workflow: Issue → Claude Implements → PR → Review → Merge
Key Takeaway: Create an issue from your phone, tag @claude, and come back to a finished pull request. Nothing deploys without your approval though  - you're always in control.

lessons.title: 9) Validation and Visual Verification
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-09-validation
Building features is only half the job  - you gotta make sure they actually work. This module covers runnin' tests across any framework, capturing screenshots so Claude can visually verify what your app looks like, and creating retrospectives to learn from every session. You'll also set up automated validation hooks that run tests every time code changes.
What You'll Learn:
Running tests across frameworks (npm, pytest, vitest, bun, all that)Capturing and comparing screenshots for visual verificationCreating session retrospectives to continuously improveAutomated validation hooks that test your code on every changeTwo demo apps included so you can practice everything hands-on
Key Takeaway: Trust but verify. Visual verification means Claude doesn't just write code  - it actually confirms the output looks right. And retrospectives mean you're gettin' better with every single session.

lessons.title: 10) Tips, Tricks, and Power User Shortcuts
lessons.description:
GitHub Repo: https://github.com/AllCytes/cc-fundamentals-10-tips-tricks
The final module is a collection of power-user techniques that make everything from the previous 9 modules faster and smoother. Keyboard shortcuts, context management, CLI aliases, communication patterns, and some creative uses for Claude Code that go way beyond just writing code.
What You'll Learn:
Essential keyboard shortcuts (Esc Esc to rewind, Ctrl+R to search history, Ctrl+S to stash)Context management  - how to keep Claude focused during long sessionsCLI aliases and launch options so you can fire things up fasterCommunication techniques that get you better results from ClaudeUsing Claude Code beyond just coding: writing, research, data analysis, and more
Key Takeaway: These are the habits that separate someone who uses Claude Code from someone who's truly fluent in it. Small optimizations that compound big time over time.

lessons.title: Capstone Project - The Legendary Live Build
lessons.description:
Associated Github Repo: Github
This is a production-ready project template that has everything from the course baked in. Clone this for any new project and you'll start with the core workflow commands, security hooks, Playwright integration, and proper folder structure all set up and ready to go.
The bonus module is a live build where you watch a real project get built from scratch using everything from the course. No shortcuts, no pre-written code  - just the raw process includin' mistakes, debugging, and real-time problem solving. This is what it actually looks like to build something real with Claude Code.
What You'll See:
- Starting from an empty project and setting up the full structure
- The Plan → Build → Validate → Review → Commit workflow in real-time
- Commands, MCPs, and skills all workin' together on a real project
- How to handle errors and debug when things don't go as planned
- The complete start-to-finish process of shipping a working app
Key Takeaway: Watching someone build something real  - includin' the messiness  - is honestly more valuable than any polished tutorial. This is the unfiltered reality of building with Claude Code.

lessons.title: The AI Strategy Factory 🏭
lessons.description:
Imagine you could put the name of your/another company, a few details here and there, and get an entire set of files, roadmaps, and even a full report and powerpoint on what the 2026 AI strategy should be?
Well, imagine no more.
I spent around 4.5 hours putting this resource together, building on a decent number of tricks I've already showed you related to Claude Code and generating assets at scale.
🔗 GitHub Repo: https://github.com/promptadvisers/ai-strategy-factory
Clone it. Customize it. Use it to look like a legend.
What I built:
Two text boxes. Company name. Company description. Click submit. Get 15 markdown files, 2 PowerPoints, and 2 Word documents—a complete AI strategy deliverable suite generated automatically.
The deliverable list:
Markdown files (internal knowledge):
- Technology inventory and data infrastructure assessment
- Pain point matrix by department
- AI maturity model and readiness assessment
- 30/60/90/180/360-day implementation roadmap
- Quick wins list
- Vendor comparisons and build vs buy frameworks
- License consolidation recommendations
- ROI calculator and cost analysis framework
- Data governance and acceptable use policy template
- Prompt library starter kit
- Glossary of AI terms
- Department-specific use case library
- Change management and training playbook
PowerPoint files (polished delivery):
- AI strategy presentation deck
- Current state vs future state diagrams (rendered as actual images using Mermaid)
Word documents (formal reports):
- Final AI strategy report
- Statement of work template
The process I went through:
Phase 1 - Consulting Knowledge Synthesis
I downloaded playbooks from McKinsey, BCG, KPMG, OpenAI, and IBM—all the free guides these firms publish to show they know their stuff. Problem is, these PDFs are massive. 28 pages here, 46 pages there. Can't just dump them all into Claude Code without blowing the context window.
So I built a lazy agentic RAG system. Used Gemini 2.5 Flash (cheap, massive context window) to go through each PDF in 10,000 token chunks. For each chunk, I ran a synthesis prompt that extracted only the valuable nuggets—removing the buzzwords, the fluff, the repeated frameworks. End result: compressed cheat sheets of every consulting guide that I can feed into Claude Code without worrying about token limits.
Phase 2 - Skill Extraction
I needed Claude Code to know how to create DocX files, PowerPoints, Excel files, and PDFs. Instead of manually teaching it, I went straight to Anthropic's GitHub repo where they publish the exact Python functions Claude uses to generate these files. Pulled the skills directly from the source, had Claude learn from them, and documented the whole process in a skills reference file.
Phase 3 - Perplexity API Integration
Here's what makes this temporal and not cookie-cutter. I integrated the Perplexity API so that when you run this system, it does live research on the company. If you're running this in January 2026, it searches as of January 2026. If n8n gets replaced by something better, the system will know. If Claude Code gets superseded, the recommendations update.
I set it up with different research depths—quick mode for basic company intel, comprehensive mode for deep research. You choose at runtime so you control cost.
Phase 4 - The Factory Architecture
Built a phased implementation plan where Claude Code:
1. Takes your two inputs (company name + description)
2. Runs Perplexity research to understand the company, industry, competitors
3. Synthesizes findings with the consulting knowledge base
4. Generates all 19 deliverables in organized folders
5. Renders Mermaid diagrams as actual PNG images
6. Packages everything for download
Each phase checks off completion before moving to the next. If the context window maxes out (which happened to me twice during the build), you can spin up a new session and pick up where you left off using the to-do list it maintains.
Phase 5 - React Frontend
Simple interface. Company name field. Description text box. Research depth toggle. Submit button. All generated assets appear on screen with download links.
The technical reality:
This took multiple Claude Code sessions. I hit the context window limit. I had API errors. The Perplexity model name was wrong in the docs. Gemini chunking broke on one file. I'm showing you all of that in the long-form video because building in public means showing the mess, not just the result.
But here's the thing—all these assets are just words. They take fewer tokens than building a complex web app because you're not generating hundreds of lines of JavaScript and Python. You're asking AI to write organized documentation. Even on the $20 Claude plan, you can get away with a lot of this if you spread out your requests.
Why this matters:
Whether you're doing this for your own business, for yourself, or for someone else on a paid basis—this system lets you crank out strategy documentation that would take days to produce manually.
The whole point: minimum input, maximum professional output. You look like a legend. The client gets a comprehensive deliverable package. Everyone wins.

lessons.title: Document Polisher Claude Skill 📜
lessons.description:
I spent a couple hours creating a custom Claude Code skill that applies world-class brand styling to any document you generate
The Economist, McKinsey, Stripe
Pick a style, and it formats your docx accordingly.
Video attached to this post walking through the full build (about 20 minutes)
🔗 GitHub Repo: Claude Skills Document Polisher
→ Clone it
→ Swap the brands
→ Make it yours
What I built:
A skill that presents you with 10 brand options when invoked
Pick a number, and it applies that brand's colors, fonts, spacing, and heading structure to your document
The brand menu:
Editorial style: The Economist
Consulting style: McKinsey, Deloitte, KPMG
Tech style: Stripe, Apple, IBM
Design style: Notion, Linear, Figma
The process I went through:
Phase 1: Building the Firecrawl MCP Server 🔥
Remember the Firecrawl brand extract trick from a couple weeks ago?
I turned that into a proper MCP server so I could use it inside Claude Code whenever I want.
Used the /mcp slash command, fed it the API documentation for Firecrawl's branding endpoint, and it generated a working MCP server in one shot
Three tools: Extract Branding, Scrape with Branding, and Compare Branding.
Phase 2: Cloning and Augmenting the Skill 🧞‍♂️
Cloned Anthropic's skills repo into .claude (important: has to be inside .claude or Claude Code won't find it).
Told it to create a new skill called Document Polisher, based on the existing docx creator.
Then I had it come up with 10 brands with strong visual identity and use the Firecrawl MCP to extract the branding from each one
Colors, fonts, typography
All pulled auto-magically
Phase 3: Testing and Fixing ⚙️
This part took the most back and forth.
Some issues I ran into:
Firecrawl only allows 5 concurrent requests, so it had to queue them up.
Generic homepages don't always show the brand well, so I had to find specific blog posts from each site.
Your local Microsoft Word might not have premium fonts installed, so it defaults to Calibri even if the file has the right font specified.
Had to tell Claude Code to update the actual skill file, not just fix things in context.
The takeaway:
→ You can take any existing Claude Code skill and augment it
→ The docx skill becomes a document polisher.
→ The pptx skill could become a deck polisher.
Feed it your client's website, extract their branding, and generate documents that look like theirs.
Zero code written, just API documentation provided and an API key.
Enjoy 🫶

lessons.title: The e-Book Generator System 📕
lessons.description:
If you check out the sample I attached to this post, you'll see it looks pretty polished!
🔗 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗼: e-Book Generator System
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝘁:
A Claude Code skill that generates polished e-books (DocX to PDF) with AI-generated cover images using 𝗚𝗲𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗶 𝟯'𝘀 𝗡𝗮𝗻𝗼 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗮. Full-bleed cover art, proper text positioning, the whole thing.
Use it for lead magnets, team resources, client deliverables, whatever.
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀:
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟭 - Reverse Engineering
I grabbed a McKinsey e-book and asked Claude Code: "Can you make something like this?" First few versions looked... rough. High school report energy. So I kept iterating ("make it prettier, make it prettier") until we got somewhere decent.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟮 - Adding Image Generation
The body content was fine. The cover page was the problem. So I wired up Gemini 3's Nano Banana API to generate portrait-sized cover images. Abstract, textbook-style visuals that relate to the topic without being too literal.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟯 - Positioning and Polish
First attempt: image and text were clashing. Asked it to make the image bleed over the whole page, superimpose the title with a backdrop so it's readable. Worked in one shot. Sometimes things just click.
𝗢𝗻𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜 𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲𝗱:
My prompts are getting lazier. Not because I don't care, but because these models are finally good enough to read between the lines. Two years ago, I was selling five-page scientific prompts. Now I can say "make it prettier" and it knows what I mean.
We're getting closer to the world I've always wanted: vague request in, polished output out.
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗯𝗶𝘁𝘀:
- Claude Code for DocX/PDF generation (skills I've already shown you)
- Gemini 3 Nano Banana for cover image generation
- YOLO mode in the Cursor extension (video shows how to set this up)
- Compact command to manage context when it gets heavy
𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀:
Designrr charges monthly for something you can now do yourself
Create branded e-books for your website, your clients, your lead funnels, whatever you need.
Could even combine this with the AI Strategy Factory.
Take those 19 deliverables, package the best ones into a polished e-book with your logo and brand colors, and now you've got something you can actually hand to a client or post on your site.
Pettiness → productivity
Enjoy. 🎁

lessons.title: The n8n Workflow Architect System 🧠
lessons.description:
Before you build anything, you should probably ask: "Should I even be using n8n for this?"
It's no secret I've been using Claude Code heavily. You've seen me use it for almost everything
BUT there are times when n8n is genuinely the better choice. The problem is knowing when.
So I built a system that helps you decide before you write a single node (and to help all the current hackathoners)
It marries the open-source n8n MCP + the out-of-the-box new skills + MY skill I designed for design thinking
🔗 GitHub Repo: n8n Powerhouse Repo
What I built:
A Claude Code skill layer that sits on top of the open source n8n MCP. It acts as a thought partner in plan mode, helping you decide:
- Should you use n8n, Claude Code, or standard Python?
- If n8n, what's the best design structure?
- What's the path of least resistance and why?
The process:
Phase 1: Setting Up the n8n MCP
The open source n8n MCP has gotten way better in the last month. 39 tools, free, connects to your n8n Cloud (or self-hosted instance). One-line command to install it. No more messing with .mcp.json files.
Phase 2: Adding the Existing Skills
The MCP repo now includes skills files for code nodes, design patterns, validation, and more. These help Claude Code understand how to use n8n properly. Cloned the repo, added to the skills directory, done.
Phase 3: Building the Workflow Architect
This is the layer I added. I talked for 20 minutes about my mental model for when to use n8n vs code, then had Claude format it into a skill. It covers:
- The authentication problem (OAuth is annoying in pure code)
- Processing limits and long-running workflows
- The hybrid approach (using n8n as infrastructure within Claude Code)
- When visual workflows make sense for non-technical maintainers
Phase 4: Wiring It All Together
Created a proper .claude folder structure with a claude.md file that understands how all the pieces connect. New sessions automatically know which skills to invoke.
The decision framework:
Quick version from the skill:
- Does it involve OAuth? → Probably n8n
- Will non-developers maintain it? → Probably n8n
- Does it need to wait days or weeks? → Probably n8n
- Is it a one-off script? → Probably Claude Code
You can change this logic. This is my opinion based on the workflows I've seen. Yours might differ.
Why this matters:
Instead of jumping straight into building and realizing halfway through that you picked the wrong tool, you now have a planning step. Go into plan mode, describe your business stack (Shopify, Zoho, Stripe, whatever), and let it recommend the approach before you commit.
In the video, I tested it with an e-commerce workflow. It correctly identified that all four integrations should use n8n because of managed auth and non-technical maintainers.
Then it built the Shopify webhook workflow using the MCP.
Enjoy. 🎁

lessons.title: The Ultimate MegaRAG System 📚
lessons.description:
This is the last Claude Code system of 2025, and it's probably the best
I think I accidentally rebuilt two SaaS products. Oops 🤷🏽‍♂️
A few months ago, I showed you Ragie, a plug and play RAG as a service tool. Then there's Vectorize, which blew up this year. Both charge monthly. Both are black boxes. You can't control how they chunk. You can't see what's happening under the hood.
So I built my own. And I might have gone a little overboard.
𝗩𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗼 attached showing exactly how I built this over 4-5 days of late nights (12am to 4am, burning the midnight candle). You'll watch me struggle, debug, and figure it out in real time.
Click to the right of the original video when you open this post.
🔗 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗼: MegaRag
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝘁:
A multimodal RAG system that handles any file type. Drag and drop MP4s, MP3s, PNGs, PDFs, DocX files, text files, all at the same time. It processes them, chunks them, extracts entities, maps relationships, builds knowledge graphs, and lets you query everything through five different search modes.
Think Vectorize, but you own it. Think Ragie, but no black box (and monthly fee).
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁:
File Support:
- MP4 videos (frame by frame text extraction)
- MP3 audio (transcription and chunking)
- PNG/images (Gemini vision describes and chunks)
- PDF, DocX, text files (Dockling inspired parsing)
Five Querying Modes:
- Mix: The full picture. Chunks + entities + relationships
- Hybrid: Entity search combined with relationship search
- Local: Find specific entities (people, companies, places)
- Global: Explore connections (how is X related to Y)
- Naive: Classic semantic similarity search
The Interface:
- Chat with persistent history and citations
- Source references with relevance ranking
- Data explorer with chunk viewer
- Entity browser across all documents
- Knowledge graph visualization (basic, but functional)
- Admin panel with dashboard and stats
- Dark mode (obviously)
The API:
- Document upload endpoint
- Query endpoint with all five modes
- Chat endpoint
- Document listing endpoint
- Ready for n8n, Claude Code workflows, or your own MCP server
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀:
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟬 - The Lazy Genius Move
This is the sauce. I cloned four open source repos into my Claude Code environment: Dockling, LightRAG, RAG Anything, and Video RAG. I didn't use them directly. I wanted Claude Code to draw inspiration from what already works and combine the best ideas into something new.
Then I ran /init and said: "Create a claude.md with your understanding of all these repos."
Now Claude had a comprehensive mental model of multimodal RAG before we wrote a single line of code.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟭 - Supabase Setup
I set up the Supabase MCP. It's one of the few MCPs I still use. My old reliable. $10/month, handles everything: edge functions, queries, tables, PGVector for embeddings. All the dirty work.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟮 - File Processing Lanes
Each file type gets its own processing lane:
Lane 1 (Text): File read → token chunking → stored. Done.
Lane 2 (Documents): PDF/DocX → Dockling parser → text blocks + tables → Gemini describes tables → chunking → embeddings.
Lane 3 (Images): Gemini Vision describes the image → chunk the description → embeddings.
Lane 4 (Video): Upload to Gemini File API → wait for processing → frame by frame extraction → chunking.
Lane 5 (Audio): Gemini File API → transcription → chunking.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟯 - Entity Extraction and Knowledge Graphs
Every chunk gets analyzed for entities (people, places, things, concepts). Entities get categorized under subjects. Relationships get mapped between entities. This is what powers the advanced search modes.
My knowledge graph visualization is basic. You could make it way better. But it works and shows you how everything connects.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟰 - The Five Query Modes
Naive mode: Generate embedding → search chunks → return top 5. Fast, but shallow.
Mix mode (default): Generate embedding → search chunks → search entities → search relationships → find related chunks → deduplicate → return with citations. Slower, but comprehensive.
The other three modes fall somewhere in between depending on what you need.
𝗣𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟱 - Chat Interface and Admin Panel
Built chat with persistent history, source citations with relevance scores, and clickable references so you can trace exactly where each answer came from.
Admin panel gives you document stats, entity distribution, recent uploads, and the knowledge graph explorer.
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸:
All you need is Supabase and a Gemini API key. That's it.
- Frontend: NextJS, React, TypeScript
- Backend: NextJS, Supabase, Dockling (inspiration), FastAPI
- Embeddings: Gemini 2.0 (cheap and works)
- Storage: Supabase storage + Postgres with PGVector
- Processing: Gemini File API for video/audio
I tried to reduce how many things you need to sign up for. Two services. Done.
𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀:
I'm going to say something that might be controversial. The way I see 2026 and forward, I don't see many use cases where you should use no-code tools for RAG anymore.
RAG changes so much. RAG, if you want to make it world class, takes many layers. Whether it's Claude Code or Windsurf or Cursor or whatever IDE, I don't think RAG should be done in no-code workflows anymore. And I'm gonna stand by that statement.
This flexibility of building such a sophisticated system would take you ages in n8n. It took me 4-5 days of late nights. But it was doable and I didn't actually write any code myself. I entered a couple API keys in the database secrets, but that's about it.
𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗱𝗼 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀:
- White label it for clients
- Add a preprocessing pipeline
- Turn it into an MCP server
- Plug the API into n8n or Claude Code workflows
- Build on the knowledge graph visualization
- Make it from good to awesome
Is this perfect? No. I haven't battle tested this in production to the point where I can say I just killed a multi-billion dollar industry. But I made a dent. And you can take what I built and find all the bugs and make it infinitely better.

lessons.title: Notebook LM Re-Imagined 📓
lessons.description:
I rebuilt Google's NotebookLM from scratch using Claude Code - but made it actually useful for power users.
What it does:
- RAG-powered chat with your documents (PDFs, DOCX, websites, YouTube)
- Multi-person podcast generation (deep dive, brief, debate modes)
- Video explainers via Alibaba's Wan 2.5 (10x cheaper than Veo)
- Deep research mode using Gemini's research API
- Study materials: flashcards, quizzes, study guides, FAQs
- Slide decks, reports, data tables - all exportable
- Full REST API with 50 endpoints - use it from n8n, Zapier, anywhere
The key difference: Everything is API-first. The web UI is just another client. You own the infrastructure.
Tech stack:
- Supabase (database, auth, storage)
- Vercel (hosting)
- Gemini API (RAG, TTS, research)
- FastAPI backend + Next.js frontend
In the repo you'll find:
- Complete codebase (frontend + backend)

- 28 architecture diagrams
- 8 prompt templates for each build phase
GitHub: NotebookLM Reimagined
Watch the full build walkthrough on my channel if you want to see how it all comes together.

lessons.title: The Complete Beginner’s Guide to Claude Code
Claude Code Masterclass: Be A One Person Company
1) Building & Deploying A Website In One Shot
lessons.description:
This 60-minute video walks through building an AI consultancy website from absolute zero using Claude Code, showing the unfiltered reality of what happens when you build without the safety nets of platforms like Lovable or Bolt - including every error, debugging session, and moment of frustration that typically gets edited out of tutorials.
Foundation Setup (First 30 minutes):
- Setting up MCP servers: Playwright for automated browser testing, Exa for search capabilities, and RefTools for documentation lookup
- Troubleshooting authentication issues with GitHub and API keys
- Validating that each MCP tool is actually accessible and running
- Creating detailed planning documents in markdown format that Claude Code can reference throughout the build
- Learning why "claude dangerously-skip-permissions" can save time but also cause problems
The Build Process and Debugging Nightmare:
- Initial prompt included specific technical implementations suggested by Grok that turned out to be incompatible (particularly with Framer Motion and CSS animations)
- 40-minute debugging session where the website appeared completely broken
- Dealing with caching issues that showed old versions of the site instead of current builds
- Port conflicts between localhost:3000 and localhost:3001 causing confusion between different project instances
- CSS classes that were hiding content instead of displaying it properly
Problem-Solving Techniques Demonstrated:
- Using Playwright to automatically screenshot and validate the build
- Leveraging Cursor as a co-pilot to diagnose issues when Claude Code gets stuck
- Recognizing when cached versions are interfering with current development
- Identifying that the root cause was an incompatibility in the initial plan rather than the execution
- Using both "ask mode" and "agent mode" strategically to control Claude's behavior
Deployment and Final Steps:
- Pushing the completed website to GitHub with proper commit history
- Deploying through Vercel (with comparison to Netlify, Render, and DigitalOcean options)
- Encountering and fixing deployment dependency errors
- Achieving a live website that's fully owned and customizable without any platform limitations
Key Lesson: While the journey is messier than one-click solutions, the destination offers complete control and understanding of your codebase.

lessons.title: 2) From Lovable to Claude Code (Bridge Strategy)
lessons.description:
This 45-minute video demonstrates the bridge strategy: using vibes coding tools (Lovable) for rapid prototyping, then transitioning to Claude Code for complex functionality.
The build: Neo-brutalist AI chatbot connected to Alex Hormozi's book content via N8N webhook.
Phase 1 - Lovable (0-40%): Built UI with sample questions, connected Supabase for message storage, integrated N8N webhook with proper timeout handling.
Phase 2 - Claude Code (40-100%): Cloned GitHub repo, added GPT-4o-mini for conversation context, implemented thread management, created UI specialist agent to fix scrolling issues.
Key technical points: Webhook timeout solutions, two-way GitHub sync between Lovable and Claude Code, creating project-based agents, proper commit practices.
Main lesson: Mix tools strategically. If N8N handles authentication in 5 minutes vs an hour in Claude Code, use N8N. Own your stack by choosing the right tool for each job.
Result: Fully functional chatbot with conversation persistence, context-aware responses, and custom UI - all exportable and owned.

lessons.title: 3) Bldg. an AI Workflow Planner feat Output Styles
lessons.description:
Today we're building something genuinely useful - an AI-powered workflow planner that helps you design automations BEFORE you build them (all associated files used attached 📂)
Plus, I'm showing you how to use Claude Code's Output Styles feature to make responses actually readable for non-developers
What you'll watch: 35 minutes of building a thought partner for automation planning, including:
- Pre-loading documentation as .md files for context
- Setting up GPT-5 Nano API integration
- Creating an interactive chat interface with Mermaid diagrams
- Using Output Styles to translate tech jargon to plain English
- Real-time testing and iteration to fix UI issues
The concept: Instead of jumping straight into building automations, this tool helps you:
- Brainstorm what you're trying to automate
- Get pushed back with clarifying questions
- Generate visual workflow diagrams
- Understand WHY the workflow is designed that way
Key technical implementation:
- Initialize with documentation: /init command reads .md files
- Maintain conversation context across API calls
- Render Mermaid diagrams inline with chat
- Download diagrams as PNG/JPEG
- Custom output style for beginner-friendly explanations
The workflow design principles file includes:
- Best practices for n8n workflows (although can be used for any tool if you provide it the logic)
- How to structure complex automations
- When to use conditional logic vs linear flows
- Error handling strategies
Why this matters: Everyone will have access to text-to-workflow tools soon enough - the differentiator isn't building automations - it's knowing WHAT to build and HOW to structure it properly
This tool makes you a better automation architect
Files used (attached below):
- workflow_tips.md - Design principles for automations
- how_to_call_gpt5_nano.md - API documentation
- Full source code of final app
Pro tip: Do your homework upfront. Loading documentation as .md files before starting means Claude Code already knows what you need, making one-shot builds actually possible

lessons.title: 4) Bldg. on the Workflow Planner (Major Updates)
lessons.description:
This is the marathon session. 1 hour 16 minutes of real development to you, but 4 hours behind the scenes - the kind where nothing works until suddenly everything does
If you want to see what building with Claude Code actually looks like when things go wrong, this is it
I published this repo for all of you to clone and build on: Github Repo
What you'll watch: Building a complete workflow planning tool with JSON export, including:
- 4+ hours of actual development compressed into 76 minutes
- Implementing Claude 4 Sonnet for JSON generation
- Adding Web Speech API for voice input
- SELF-HEALING Mermaid diagrams and JSON validation
- API failures, and how to fix them
- The moment everything finally clicks (finally)
The build journey:
0:00-40:00 - Planning phase (yes, 40 minutes of planning)
- Setting up Supabase and Vercel MCPs
- Creating documentation files for APIs
- Recycling voice implementation from previous projects
- Configuring environment variables
40:00-60:00 - The struggle phase
- Voice button looking "ugly as sin" and not working
- API request failures despite correct keys
- CORS policy blocking Claude API calls
- Console debugging to find the real errors
60:00-76:00 - The breakthrough
- Finally parsing API responses correctly
- Self-healing diagrams working silently
- JSON export with "Let's Build It" button
- Clean repository and production deployment
Critical lessons learned:
- Log EVERYTHING to console for debugging
- Planning for 40 minutes saves 4 hours of debugging
- Don't let AI dictate to other AI (classes/functions)

- Commit frequently - you'll need those checkpoints
Final features delivered:

- First-person explanations of workflow logic

Repository structure (cleaned):
