- Organized: Proper file structure for cloning

This is more of a documentary than a tutorial 🤣 enjoy

lessons.title: 5) Adding Supabase with MCP Servers
lessons.description:
(The Right Way)15 minutes to add a complete database backend to our workflow planner using Supabase MCP servers.
What you'll watch: Clean database integration without the usual pain, including:
- Proper MCP.json configuration for Supabase
- Setting up access tokens (not project-specific)
- Creating 6 interconnected tables in one command
- Testing data flow from frontend to database
- Protecting credentials with .gitignore
Critical setup steps shown:
- Adding Supabase to MCP.json with --access-token flag
- Creating access tokens in Supabase account settings
- Adding mcp.json to .gitignore BEFORE committing
- Verifying MCP connection shows actual functions
The schema we built:
- Sessions table - tracking user sessions
- Initial prompts - first user input storage
- Voice inputs - Web Speech API transcripts
- Conversations - full chat history
- Mermaid diagrams - workflow visualizations
- User actions - button clicks and interactions
Key technical wins:
- MCP server creates tables directly (no SQL copy-paste)
- Automatic frontend-backend connection
- Self-testing with SQL query validation
- Real-time data verification in Supabase dashboard
The game-changing difference:
Traditional way: Generate SQL → Copy → Paste in Supabase → Test → Repeat
MCP way: Tell Claude what you want → Done
Testing methodology:
- Submit text prompt → Check database
- Use voice input → Verify transcript storage
- Generate diagram → Confirm Mermaid code saved
- Full conversation → See complete history
Common pitfalls avoided:
- Don't trust "Supabase connected" without function list
- Always test in incognito after database changes
- Hard refresh breaks Loom recording (learned that one)
- Some MCP commands work locally but not on server
What's not covered (future videos):
- Authentication with Clerk
- Session-based history loading
- Google auth (too painful with Supabase)
The money quote: "This is like a cheat code... imagine going through and saying, oh, I want to change one column, so now it spits out a new SQL script, and then you have to go back into Supabase... paste the SQL code every single time."
Pro tips delivered:
- Create access token first, copy immediately
- Add #mcp section to .gitignore
- Use claude mcp list to verify functions
- Watch for "success: true" in console
This is what MCP servers were built for - turning a 30-minute manual process into a 2-minute automated one. No SQL knowledge required, just tell Claude what tables you need.
Repository updates included, ready for cloning.

lessons.title: 6) Running Claude Code Inside Replit Agent 🫨
lessons.description:
This is the craziest Claude Code hack you'll see this week, guaranteed.
Claude Code fully running inside Replit Agent, without using Cursor or even a local editor.
We spun up a web app scaffold in Replit, opened the shell, and installed Claude Code right there. No GitHub commit required, no duplicate subscriptions.
Editor-free Claude integration that actually controls the live Replit project, including:
- Creating a functional “Skool clone” app scaffold in Replit Agent
- Installing Claude Code via npm directly in the Replit shell
- Using device-code auth to sign in cleanly
- Running claude dangerously to bypass restrictions
- Verifying Claude Code can analyze and rewrite the repo on the fly
The scaffold we built:
- Web application shell (community-style platform)
- Live code preview generated by Replit
- Claude Code analyzing repo structure
- Full theme/UI pass applied via Claude prompts
Key technical wins:
- No local editor, Cursor, or VS Code needed
- Full access to Claude Code in a hosted environment
- Ability to stack Claude Code on top of Replit infra (DB, deploys, previews)
- Credit-efficient: Replit tokens only spent when using their native services
- CLI superpowers inside a browser-only dev flow
Enjoy the nugget 🌝

lessons.title: 7) Making Your Apps Beautiful with Aura & 21st.dev
lessons.description:
Your cheat code to stop building ugly apps.
21 minutes to transform Claude Code projects from developer-bland to designer-beautiful using three different approaches.
What you'll watch: Three methods to instantly upgrade your UI, including:
- Aura.build for full website templates
- 21st.dev for component-level design
- 21st.dev MCP server for AI-powered mockups (teaser for tomorrow)
Method 1: Aura.build (Full Website Templates)
- Browse trending components with animations
- Copy entire codebase (886 lines)
- The 32K token limit problem and solution
- Export CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS=64000
Critical lesson learned:
Starting from scratch with template code: 10 minutes
Retrofitting existing website: 1+ hour of pain
Always start with the design template, THEN modify.
Method 2: 21st.dev (Component Library)
- Glass morphism buttons
- Liquid glass effects
- Copy prompt feature for Claude Code
- Targeting specific elements
The token limit fix:
When Claude says "response exceeded 32,000 output token maximum"
Run: export CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS=64000
This doubles your output capacity to 64K tokens
Pro workflow discovered:
1. Take template code from Aura.build
2. Implement as-is first (no modifications)
3. Then change colors/content in plan mode
4. Result: Clean diffs, fewer hallucinations
Component targeting with 21st.dev:
- Copy prompt from 21st.dev
- Add context: "Apply to the get consultation button"
- Queue multiple component updates
- Watch the magic happen
Common pitfalls shown:
- Don't delete and rebuild (causes hallucinations)
- Template has 886 lines? Increase token limit first
- Missing 30% of screen? Auto-layout issues
- Orbital animation disappeared? Start fresh
Tomorrow's preview: 21st.dev MCP server
- See 6-7 mockups instantly
- Choose your favorite
- Auto-implement in Claude Code
Result: From basic consultant website to glass-morphism beauty in under 20 minutes (when you do it right).

lessons.title: 8) 21st.dev Magic MCP - AI-Powered UI in Minutes
lessons.description:
This handy super-trick applies anywhere that can take an MCP server.
10-ish minutes to go from ugly to beautiful using the 21st.dev Magic MCP server - the closest thing to having a designer in your terminal.
What you'll watch: Live UI generation with AI-powered mockups, including:
- Installing and configuring the Magic MCP
- Using Component Builder for multiple variants
- Component Inspiration for quick previews
- Real-time iteration without code copying
The Magic MCP tools explained:
- Component Builder: Creates 5-6 UI variants you can preview
- Logo Search: Adds company logos automatically
- Component Inspiration: Fetches designs from 21st.dev
- Component Refiner: Improves existing UI components
Setup process shown:
- Copy install command from 21st.dev/mcp
- Includes API key automatically
- Restart terminal session after install
- Verify with: claude mcp list
The workflow difference:
Yesterday: Copy code → Paste → Debug → Cry
Today: Ask for redesign → Preview 5 variants → Pick one → Apply
Live demo results:
- Started with "ugly" floating ideas app
- Generated 5 completely different designs
- Each variant viewable in browser
- "Open in MCP" applies changes instantly
Common issues addressed:
- First redirect might fail (retry works)
- Framework conflicts possible (but rare)
- Some variants might miss context (iterate)
Why this matters:
No more copying code from design sites. No more token limits. Just describe what you want, preview options, and apply.

lessons.title: 9) Building Sleek Analytics Dashboards in Minutes
lessons.description:
Only 15 minutes showing how to build what used to take weeks with analytics providers, now possible in an hour with Claude Code
Here's the Github Repo: Github Link
What you'll watch: Building a production-ready analytics dashboard, including:
- Live CSV data connection (no hard-coded values)
- Drag-and-drop file upload with instant refresh
- Interactive filtering and slice-and-dice capabilities
- Glassmorphic UI that actually looks professional
- Using Polars instead of Pandas (10x faster)
The secret sauce: Polars
While everyone uses Pandas (2018 tech), Polars is the modern alternative:
- Handles 1-5 million rows easily
- Instant filtering (no caching needed)
- Built for production, not tutorials
Key features delivered:
- 6 chart types (line, bar, pie, scatter, area, heatmap)
- Real-time KPI cards with metrics
- Multi-page data tables with sorting
- Min/max price range filtering
- Region and channel filtering
- Export capabilities
The conversation log trick:
"Before we get started, keep track of this entire conversation in a .md file"
This prevents context window loss and creates documentation automatically.
Critical prompt elements:
- "Focus on real data from CSV with no hard-coded values"
- "Use Flask backend with Polars for high performance"
- "Glassmorphic UI with skeleton loading"
- "Interactive slice and dice filtering"
The trauma that shaped the prompt:
First attempt: 3.5 hours of failure (using wrong Sonnet model)
Second attempt: 1 hour to working dashboard
Lesson: Ground rules prevent AI from using fake/hard-coded data
Framework prescriptions that work:
- Backend: Flask (lightweight, Replit default)
- Data: Polars (not Pandas)
- Frontend: React + TypeScript
- Charts: Recharts
- Styling: Tailwind with glassmorphism
Business opportunity identified:
- Tableau licenses: $70/user/month
- Power BI: Requires learning DAX language
- Your solution: Custom, instant, beautiful
- Perfect for SMBs who need "their" dashboard
Next steps to build on this:
- Add data validation wizard
- Create portable data engineer agent
- Build analytics suite for specific industries
- Sell to businesses tired of Power BI/Tableau
Files provided:
- conversation_log.md (full build history)
- requirements.md (one-shot prompt)
- Sample CSV data
- GitHub repo (public, ready to clone)

lessons.title: 10) Building Custom GPTs in Claude Code
lessons.description:
19 minutes showing how to build custom GPTs using just markdown files - no flaky MCP servers, no complex setups, just clean, portable knowledge systems
What you'll watch: Creating a slide generation system, including:

Knowledge base creation via web search:

Pinecone MCP (when it finally worked):

Memory system that actually works:
```markdown
# MyMemories.md
- Visual preferences
- Content preferences

- Examples of preferred outputs

lessons.title: 1) How to Build Your OWN MCP Servers
lessons.description:
This will be one of the more helpful tutorials you'll see on Claude Code 🦾
What you'll watch: Creating two custom MCP servers from scratch:
- Lazy Calculator (doubles the real answer for fun)
- Forecasting Calculator (statistical predictions with Python)
- The autopilot debugging strategy that changes everything
GitHub Repos (attached to this post):
- [Lazy Calculator MCP]
- [Forecasting Calculator MCP]
- Complete implementation guides included
The core concept explained:
MCP servers are wrappers around functions. Instead of letting an LLM do math (unreliable), you write deterministic Python functions and wrap them with MCP for Claude to access.
The game-changing autopilot strategy:
1. Copy config path: ~/.config/claude/claude_desktop_config.json
2. Copy log path: ~/Library/Logs/Claude/mcp-server-*.log
3. Tell Claude: "Fix errors yourself. Edit the config directly. Check logs. Loop until it works."
4. Claude debugs itself without your intervention
Files provided (attached below):
- MCP Implementation Guide (comprehensive documentation)
- MCP 101 (LLM-friendly explanation)
- One-shot prompt for building MCPs
- Error resolution cheatsheet
- Complete working examples
Critical discoveries:
- FastMCP vs Standard MCP (Mac treats them differently)
- Extension mode vs server mode issues
- Why third-party MCPs steal context
- Building your own is 10x more efficient
The forecasting calculator demo:
Input: "$2, $6, $7, $9, $9.50, $12"
Output: Multiple forecasting methods (linear, exponential, polynomial)
All calculations done in Python, instantly, reliably
Common errors solved:
- "Broken pipe error" - permission issues
- "MCP server not found" - config placement
- "Transport error" - wrong MCP framework
- All solutions documented in the guide
The brutal truth about existing MCPs:
"OpenMemory's codebase looks like hot garbage... imagine a garbage truck on fire, rushing off a cliff. That's how well structured these MCP servers are."
Why build your own:
- No context window theft
- Deterministic, instant results
- Complete control over functionality
- Actually understand what's happening
- Reusable across all projects
The one-shot prompt approach:
After suffering through the first build, create a comprehensive guide that lets you build the next MCP in one prompt. Included in the repo.
Pro workflow:
1. Build first MCP (painful, 3-4 hours)
2. Document everything that broke
3. Create one-shot guide
4. Build next MCP in 10 minutes
5. Keep expanding your toolkit
Mac-specific notes:
Built on M2 Mac 2023. Windows/Linux users may need adjustments. The guide includes OS-specific considerations.
All files shown in the video are attached to this post. Clone, customize, and never depend on broken MCP servers again.

lessons.title: 2) Replace Superapps with Claude Code ☢️
lessons.description:
With this technique, you can say BYE BYE to apps like Manus 🙏🏽
10 minutes to turn Claude Code into your all-in-one business automation suite - generating complete AI audit packages that would take days in other tools.
What you'll watch: Building an entire consulting package in 40 minutes:
- Process automation summaries for HVAC business
- Mermaid diagrams for every workflow
- ASCII art for client presentations
- Project timelines with cost estimates
- All from ONE prompt
The mega-prompt that replaces Manus:
"Create folders with assets: process automations, mermaid diagrams, ASCII art for Miro boards, project plans with timelines and costs at $120/hour plus 40% agency premium"
Live client example shown:
- Capital Comfort Systems (Ottawa HVAC)
- Web scraping for business understanding
- AI implementation strategy
- Complete automation roadmap
Assets generated automatically:
/process_automation_summary
Lead generation workflowsEmergency dispatch automationQuote conversion systems/mermaid_diagrams
Complete workflow visualizationsLead scoring with emojisHuman-in-the-loop indicators/ascii_art
Customer journey mapsArchitecture diagramsImplementation roadmaps/project_plans
Timeline breakdownsCost estimates per automationROI projectionsComplete prompt provided below - copy, customize for your industry, deliver professional consulting packages in under an hour.
"Okay, so I want you to come up with a full plan on how we could implement AI in this business. But I don't just want you to come back with a verbal plan. I want you to create multiple folders with assets that you generate per folder, ideally in a Markdown file each. One is I want you to go through what this business might need in terms of AI and automation and be practical, don't be airy-fairy. Don't use random stuff, as of 2023, because your data is limited, you're stuck, and it's 3 years past your last training. So we have things like right now we have N8N, we have Make.com, we have Zapier. N8N has this thing called an AI Agent Module that lets an AI agent use different tools and have some like a chat trigger. You can even use it to build let's say chatbots instead of using something like VoiceFlow or Bot Press, but those are options as well. Given the nature of this business, go through and come up with a series of processes that could theoretically be automated and implemented. You can imagine what the lead generation process for such a business might be, what the lead nurture process might look like, what answering the phone might look like. Now in 2025, we have something like voice agents, so you can go do some research if you want on things like VAPI on retail on different platforms that exist in 2025 that again you're unaware of given your training. So go through all of that and come up with a series of processes and store the processes and the summary of what can be done in a folder called Process Automation Summary. Then create another folder where you create a Mermaid diagram to represent what it would look like to automate the part of the process. Some of these processes might be able to be handled A-to-Z, so maybe an email process where there's an inbox they have managed on Gmail, then they get inquiries. We could theoretically handle the inquiry responses end-to-end or it could be a draft. But there will be many parts of the business that need a human in the loop, so for those parts of the business outline where the human intervention is needed and outline as well what could be done from an automation standpoint and what platforms might be useful. And in the Mermaid diagram try to be platform-agnostic, so you can just say AI agent as a proxy if you think AI agent is good, but remember that being more deterministic is better when it comes to building these workflows. Folder number two should be our Mermaid diagrams corresponding to your suggested automations. Number three should be ASCII art that I can paste onto a Miro board, so when I'm walking through the client on our first discovery call together, what we can do for them in terms of automation, I would have a diagram that I can easily import into something like Miro and make it easier for me to explain my point visually. And after that, I want you to create one more folder where we go through what the timelines might look like and the project might look like for each and every automation from the initial timeline to get it done, the most likely the necessary feedback loops we would have throughout the process, where we would need check-ins, and try to come up with an estimate of cost per automation, so assume that my hour is $120 USD per hour, and try to think through how many hours it might take to actually complete that, and try to add some form of a 40% premium for my agency to come up with a project plan as well as associated costs with every single automation in the other folders, and naturally, I want you to put this in another folder as well."

lessons.title: 3) Building Your OWN Perplexity MCP
lessons.description:
We don't need to wait for service providers to make MCP servers 'for us'. We can technically make them ourselves 😉
I just spent 4 hours building something that didn't exist: a custom Perplexity MCP server that actually works the way I want it to
While everyone's waiting for "official" MCP servers, I went ahead and built my own. Result? Three custom tools (Search Web, Search Academic, Quick Search) that tap directly into Perplexity's Sonar API with full control over parameters.
What you'll see in this 12-minute walkthrough:
- Live demo of the MCP server in action (with real academic search)
- The 7-minute documentation sprint that made this possible
- Copy-paste methodology from API docs to working MCP
- Real debugging session with Claude Desktop logs
- Why I broke it when I "got fancy" (and how I fixed it)
We have to remember --> MCP servers are just Python functions with wrappers
Once you understand this (truly), you can build MCP servers for ANY service that has an API
Key technical process:
1. Copy API docs directly into .md files
2. Include sample curl requests + responses
3. Let Claude Code build the wrapper functions
4. Debug through Claude Desktop logs
5. Iterate until it works
The philosophy shift: Instead of waiting for someone else to build the MCP server you need, build it yourself. Own your infrastructure.
GitHub repo (ready to clone): https://github.com/promptadvisers/perplexity-mcp-server
This changes everything. Any API can become an MCP server in hours, not months
Building in public continues 🛠️

lessons.title: 4) The Perfect Feedback Loop (Session Mining) ⛏️
lessons.description:
💡 DISCOVERY ALERT: This is a debugging technique most people don't know exists (that I found out by accident)
If you've been using Claude Code for days/weeks/months, you're sitting on a goldmine of learning data.
I just discovered how to access EVERY terminal session you've ever had in Claude Code - even the ones that scrolled off into oblivion
More importantly, I found a way to get AI to roast your own prompting habits and tell you exactly how to improve.
The hidden treasure location: ./claude-projects/ folder on your machine.
What you'll learn in this 7-minute session:
- How to find and open those thick JSONL files (spoiler: use Sublime Text)
- Upload technique to Gemini 2.5 Pro for analysis
- Getting personalized feedback on your prompting patterns
- Real example: AI telling me my "test MCP" prompts are lazy
- How to audit tool usage and catch MCP servers that aren't actually working
Instead of saying "test MCP", I should say "please test the connection to Lazy Calculator MCP server, then try the Forecasting Calculator."
Advanced technique: Load current best practices research alongside your session data for expert-level auditing of your Claude Code habits.
Key commands shown:
- Finding project folders with simple Google search
- Using Warp terminal to open directories
- JSONL extraction and analysis workflow
Your terminal sessions = your prompting report card. 📊

lessons.title: 5) n8n to Python Script Converter
lessons.description:
I just cracked the code on something game-changing --> taking any n8n workflow and converting it to a production-ready Python GUI app in minutes, not hours
You don't "NEED" to use n8n, zapier, or make to build automations ... they're all based on scripting languages anyway
Watch me take this 5-step n8n automation (Perplexity research → OpenAI processing → webhook) and transform it into this sleek Flask dashboard with one upload and one perfectly crafted prompt.
Core steps:
Upload n8n JSON
→ Claude Code reads every node
→ Builds complete Python app with SQLite database, dark/light mode, download functionality, and execution logs.
When this makes sense:
✅ API-heavy workflows (just HTTP requests)
✅ Client deliverables that need customization
✅ When n8n changes take hours but Python changes take minutes
When to stick with n8n:
❌ Auth-heavy workflows (Slack, complex OAuth)
❌ Quick personal automations
❌ When visual flow matters more than speed
Key insight: "No-code" tools abstract code because code used to be painful. Now Claude Code makes Python easier than visual builders for many use cases.
The process:
1. Export n8n workflow as JSON
2. Upload with my conversion prompt (crafted over days)
3. Claude analyzes every node and recreates as Python functions
4. Adds GUI, database, error handling automatically
5. Deploy anywhere (Vercel, Netlify, local)
Result: Professional dashboard that clients can actually use and modify, instead of being locked into n8n's interface.
The conversion prompt is yours to use. 🎁

lessons.title: 6) Chrome's Native MCP Server - Playwright Killer
lessons.description:
This is a webscraper's dream
If you're comfortable with web scraping concepts and MCP servers, you're ready. This assumes you understand browser automation and have Claude Code setup.
Google Chrome just dropped their own MCP server for browser automation - and it's about to make Playwright obsolete for most use cases.
I tested the same multi-step navigation task: Playwright took 30 minutes and failed. Chrome's MCP server? Completed in under 5 minutes with perfect accuracy.
The setup is dead simple:
1. Create .mcp.json file in your project
2. Copy-paste the config from their repo
3. Install Claude Code extension (makes permissions easier)
4. Start automating
What I tested: Navigate to Skool.com → Find my classroom → Go to "Start Here" → Scrape latest Q&A posts → Extract Chrome extension link
The magic differences:
- 30-40% fewer tokens than Playwright
- Native Chrome integration (no spinning/struggling)
- Better error recovery (handled 404s smoothly)
- Faster navigation between pages
- More accurate element detection
Key technical advantage: When browser creators build their own MCP servers, they understand their DOM structure better than third-party tools ever could.
Real-world applications:
✅ App testing and QA automation
✅ Design schema auditing
✅ Content extraction pipelines
✅ User journey validation
The one caveat: You need to use Chrome (obviously), but if you're already on Chrome, this is a massive upgrade over Playwright for most web automation tasks.

lessons.title: 1) Building Agent Swarms with Claude Flow
lessons.description:
I paid $600 to get an hour with Reuben (rUv), the creator of Claude Flow - the framework that's quietly revolutionizing how we build agent swarms in Claude Code -- wanted to learn it so I can teach it
This is a raw, unfiltered knowledge transfer from someone running 50,000+ downloads/week and serving 20 Fortune 500 companies, to someone who looks like a caveman (me) while he tells me what commands to execute
What you'll learn from this 60-minute session:
- Setting up Claude Flow from absolute scratch (including Mac-specific debugging)
- Building a 5-agent concurrent swarm that actually works
- The difference between Swarm memory and Hive Mind architectures
- Why rUv uses Rust over Python (and when it matters)
- MCP debugging techniques that will save you hours
- Real architecture decisions from production deployments
Key technical takeaways:
- Initialize with: npx claude-flow@alpha init --force
- Concurrent agents = 5x speed (30 min task → 5 min)
- Remove Redis, use Supabase real-time
- Use Vite.js not Next.js
- Create aliases: dsp (new session), dspc (continue session)
- Debug MCPs with: claude mcp list --debug

lessons.title: 2) Agent Management & Agent Design Patterns
lessons.description:
We're almost near the end! 🦾 time to bring together multiple concepts to level up
If you're still learning basic Claude Code workflows, bookmark this for now
This assumes you understand MCP servers and have built functional apps.
I just spent serious time testing multi-agent setups and discovered why most "6-agent coding workflows" you see on YouTube are actually counterproductive
The brutal management truth is that adding agents is like hiring employees
The more you add, the less control you have
Without proper "one-on-ones," agents work in silos, compete for tasks, and overwrite each other's code.
My agent philosophy:
- Start with ZERO agents
- Add one only when there's clear need
- Keep roles mutually exclusive
- Never go YOLO mode with multiple agents mid-project
The one agent usually worth building: UI Validation Agent using Chrome DevTools MCP
Watches localhost, audits design, provides feedback loops - perfect single-purpose role.
Smart agent combinations that work:
✅ Research → Architect → Coder (staged workflow)
✅ Coder + UI Validator (complementary roles)
❌ Multiple coding agents with overlapping tools
The secret sauce: Spend 30-40 minutes planning agent architecture BEFORE coding. Use agentic design patterns repo + Cursor for consultation
Think like a startup: hire one person, push to limits, then add the next.
Demo result: "Beginner to Beast" learning app built with single UI agent providing real-time feedback loop.
Repository: github.com/promptadvisers/agentic-design-patterns

lessons.title: 3) Claude Code 2.0 - The Quality of Life Updates
lessons.description:
8 minutes covering the game-changing features in Claude Code 2.0 that actually matter for your workflow.
What's new in 2.0:
- Automatic session history (like Lovable/Bolt)
- Rewind feature (time travel for your code)
- Better token usage tracking
- Control+R prompt search
- Smarter model defaults
The Rewind feature (biggest win):
- Type /rewind to go back to any previous point
- Restores both code AND conversation
- "Like a Disney movie where you can go back to when things were easier"
- Demo: Reverting from broken neon theme to working canvas
Live demo shown:
Built infinite canvas app (like Excalidraw) in one shot
- Persistent text boxes
- Drag and drop functionality
- Browser storage (no database needed)
Control+R (prompt search):
- Search through all previous prompts
- Jump to specific points without scrolling
- Essential for long conversations
- Prevents hallucination by finding exact context
Model hierarchy changes:
Old: Opus first (bankruptcy mode)
New: Claude 3.5/4.5 default, Opus as backup
Result: 80% less token cost for same quality
Token tracking improvements:
- /usage shows weekly breakdown
- Separate category for Opus (the wallet killer)
- /context reveals MCP tool token usage
- Catches hidden global MCPs eating tokens
Pro workflow tips:
- Use /clear to free context mid-session
- Spin up new sessions for new goals
- Rewind > Compact for fixing issues
- Multiple tabs for different build aspects
It's a much better model and experience, but still imperfect. I had my fair share of rage quitting this morning using it to its fullest.
Missing feature:
Opus planning mode not directly accessible
Workaround: Use full model name
claude-opus-4-1-[full-string] --model
Best practices with 2.0:
1. Lay foundation in first session
2. New sessions for new features
3. Use rewind liberally
4. Monitor token usage weekly
5. Keep tabs organized by function

lessons.title: 4) Capstone Project - Building A Voicenote App 🗣️
lessons.description:
[Final 'Core' Module Before AI Consulting Playbook Series]
36 minutes building a voice recording app that demonstrates everything we've learned - plus AWS S3 for unlimited, shareable storage.
Resources:
- 🔗 [GitHub Repo - Clone and Deploy]: Github Voicenote App
- 🔗 [Perplexity Chat - Full AWS Setup Guide]: Perplexity Chat History
---
What you'll watch: Building a complete voice recording SaaS in 2.5 hours:
- One-click recording with browser mic
- Automatic AWS S3 cloud storage
- Real-time Whisper transcription
- AI-generated smart titles
- Shareable links that work anywhere
- Glass morphism UI with waveform visualization
The problem solved:
"I wanted to respond to community questions with voice notes, but needed them shareable, transcribed, and professionally stored - not trapped in WhatsApp or local files."
Architecture breakdown shown:
Frontend (Browser)
→ Recording
→ Upload to S3
↓
Whisper Transcription
↓
AI Smart Titles
↓
Shareable URL Generated
Why AWS S3 (not Supabase):
- Netflix uses it for streaming
- BCG/Deloitte use it for documents
- $0.006 per 1000 minutes of audio
- 99.99% durability
- Instant shareable URLs
- No file size limits
The planning phase (3 hours):
- Problem definition
- Feature requirements
- Tech stack selection
- Cost analysis
- User journey mapping
- THEN 2 hours of building
Core features delivered:
1. Voice Recording
- Glass morphism mic button
- Real-time waveform feedback
- Timer display
- WebM/MP4 high quality
2. Cloud Storage
- Automatic S3 upload
- Permanent storage
- Public/private options
- Folder organization
3. AI Processing
- Whisper transcription ($0.006/min)
- GPT-4.0-mini for titles
- 1-3 word smart naming
- Download transcripts
4. Instant Sharing
- Direct playback links
- 2x speed option
- Works in any browser
- No login required
Critical bucket settings revealed:
- Uncheck ALL "Block Public Access" options
- Configure CORS for PUT, POST, GET
- Create IAM user with specific permissions
- Generate access keys securely
The mega-prompt evolution:
1. First attempt: AI dictating code to AI (failed)
2. Second attempt: Concept-focused, code-agnostic (worked)
3. Final: Complete with AWS credentials and URLs
Lessons for builders:
- Plan exhaustively before coding
- Use AI to navigate unfamiliar platforms
- Don't over-index on one tool (Supabase isn't everything)
- Screenshot errors for AI debugging
- Build with monetization in mind

lessons.title: ABOUT THE COMMUNITY GARDEN 🪴
lessons.description:
This is where member-built tools, workflows, and resources live.
If you've built something that solves a problem - whether for yourself or for others in the community - tag me in your post and I'll add it to a pinned collection here. That way it doesn't get buried in the feed and people can actually find it when they need it.
A lot of the best contributions start as pain points. Someone hits a wall, posts about it, and another member builds something to fix it. That's how the good stuff happens. One person's frustration becomes another person's solution.
This isn't about polished, production-ready tools. It's about sharing what works. A quick n8n workflow. A prompt template that saves time. A script that automates something annoying. If it helped you, it'll probably help someone else.
Browse what's here. Use what's useful. Remix what you find. And if you build something worth sharing, post it in the community and tag me - I'll make sure it ends up here.

programs.title: The AI Consulting Playbook
programs.description: Land clients, run discovery calls, and price AI solutions

lessons.title: 🎁 Fantastic Resource by Community Member
lessons.description: Consulting Playbook App: Link Here

lessons.title: 1) Why Most AI Consultants Will Fail 📉
lessons.description:
Your positioning determines your price -- and your survival rate
20 minutes on why saying "no" to clients makes you more money than saying "yes" to everyone, and the framework that's survived 2,000+ hours across companies you'd recognize
What you'll watch:
- The "Yes you're all wrong" positioning strategy
- Four readiness checkpoints that determine if someone can even use AI
- Three-tier pricing model (Implementation → Prep → Education)
- Why refunding clients gets you more business
The core positioning shift:
Don't give clients what they WANT (newest tools, all the features, DevDay highlights)
Give them what they NEED (readiness assessment, process fixes, honest limits)
The provocative statement to steal:
"AI is a luxury, not a right. You earn the ability to use AI. You don't just get it."
The Four Readiness Checkpoints:
Before selling ANY implementation, ask:
1. Processes: Do they exist? Documented? If not, you're building on a garbage truck on fire
2. Data: Where does it live? SQL? Do they have a data dictionary? (Litmus test for maturity)
3. Buy-in: C-suite on board or Trojan horse situation? Wrong management = 40% failure rate
4. Systems: Built automations before? Speak Make/N8N? Different hemisphere entirely
If they fail 2+ checkpoints? Don't sell implementation. Sell readiness.
The Three-Tier Strategy Framework:
Tier 1 - Implementation (they're ready):
- Build automations or consult so well they let you build
- $200/month retainer to stay embedded
Tier 2 - Prep (not ready):
- Organize their processes first
- Fix the foundation before adding AI
- This is where you make the most money 👈
Tier 3 - Education (need training):
- ChatGPT/Claude workshops (still the best seller)
- Train on fixing organizational problems
Critical lessons from 2,000+ hours:
Short-term vs long-term tradeoff:
- Hype positioning: More clients faster, more churn, temporal income
- Honest positioning: Fewer clients, zero churn, premium prices, referral machine
Real example: "I've refunded consults when clients push me toward solutions I don't believe in. Every time I do this, I get MORE business."
The consulting arbitrage window:
6-12 months before AI can teach better than us. Use this time to:
- Do free 15-min consults if starting at zero
- Rack up dozens of calls
- Build pattern recognition across industries
Common consulting pitfalls:
- Being the Trojan horse (hired by non-C-suite, hated by leadership)
- The $5K/month subscription audit that saves $3K (builds trust for $30-40K engagements)
- Teaching tools to companies with no processes
- Over-indexing on one tool (voice only, chat only, Claude only)
The buy-in technique for hostile rooms:
"Let me show you three types of people. Jim (burning down ChatGPT) - some of you hate that I exist. I come in peace. I'm skeptical too, which is why [stakeholder] trusted me."
Call it out. Acknowledge it. Move forward.
Why generalist knowledge matters:
Companies need:
- Systems that share the same brain
- Tools outside their database to avoid technical debt
- Multiple automation layers
- Someone who understands the full spectrum
"We're going to crush huge consulting firms because they're bloated, not nimble, and their consultants watch our videos to make their decks."
The positioning that wins: ✅
Not: "Here's everything from OpenAI DevDay and how to use it all"
But: "Here's what matters for YOUR situation, and here's why 90% of that doesn't apply to you yet"
Result: Premium pricing, clients that stay for years, referrals that pre-qualify themselves.

lessons.title: 2) The Art of the Discovery Call (P1)
lessons.description:
Be an AI doctor, not an AI drug dealer.
How to diagnose client readiness, ask the right scope questions, and identify red flags before you waste time building solutions on broken foundations.
What you'll watch:
- The opening framework that positions you as the expert
- Three critical scope questions (Scale → Stack → Knowledge)
- How to map their entire tech ecosystem in real-time
- Red flags that mean "don't take this client yet"
The core philosophy shift:
AI Doctor: Diagnose → Provide prognosis → Prescribe what they actually need
AI Drug Dealer: Hear pain point → Immediately pitch your sexiest solution
"Drug dealers are profitable in the short term. Doctors build trust and are profitable long-term."
Opening the call:
When they ask "How do you usually run these things?"
Your answer: "My goal is to fix your pain point or help you reach your goal. I already read your Calendly notes and briefed myself on your company. I'd love to hear in your own words: what are the day-to-day pain points you, your team, and your company are dealing with?"
This immediately positions you as:
- Prepared (you did your homework)
- Focused on their problems (not your solutions)
- Diagnostic (listening before prescribing)
Critical paid call rule:
If someone spends 10 minutes explaining their pain point, extend the call by 10 minutes. Those were venting minutes, not solutioning minutes. Don't nickel and dime people. Build long-term relationships.
The Three Scope Questions:
Question 1 - SCALE (How many employees?)
Pattern matching by company size:
- 5-20: Flat hierarchy, minimal processes
- 21-50: Policies start appearing, first managers
- 51-100: Series B/C territory, real structure
- 100-500: Defined departments, politics emerge
- 500-1,000: Could be public, series D/E
- 1,000-5,000: Enterprise tier
- 10,000+: Full enterprise, maximum bureaucracy
Pro tip: VC-backed startups (50-100 employees) usually have budget set aside by investors for efficiency improvements. They're AI-first, AI-leading, very open to investing.
Bootstrapped vs VC-backed changes everything about budget availability and speed of decision-making.
Question 2 - STACK (What tools do you use day-to-day?)
While they're talking, run Perplexity searches in the background:
- "Does [tool name] have an API endpoint open to developers?"
- If no API: Can we migrate? How important is this tool?
- If locked in: Note this as a constraint for later
Real-time pattern recognition:
"We use Google Drive, Notion, Slack"
→ Google ecosystem, easier integrations, straightforward automations
"We use Outlook, OneDrive, Microsoft Teams"
→ Microsoft ecosystem, often public sector/non-profit, OneDrive permissions are devastating, need internal IT help
The stack categories to map:
1. Comms: Slack vs Teams vs Discord
2. Docs/Knowledge: Google Drive vs OneDrive vs Dropbox vs Notion
3. CRM: HubSpot vs Salesforce vs Pipedrive (and how well is it configured?)
4. Project Management: Monday vs Asana vs ClickUp (does it have an API?)
5. Data/BI: Power BI vs Tableau vs Looker vs Google Sheets Empire
6. Design: Figma vs Canva
7. Dev Tools: GitHub vs Bitbucket vs private repos
8. Database: Supabase vs Firebase vs Convex
9. Meeting Notes: Fathom vs Gong vs Fireflies (each has different integration capabilities)
Critical discovery about CRMs:
"These systems are only as good as the people who set them up and the hygiene in which they use the tool."
Real example: Sales team using HubSpot, but campaigns are named "001_week", "001_week(1)", "001_week(2)"
Result: You accept the contract, get the milestone payment, dive into their system, and realize it's way more work than expected. Now they think you understand the assignment, but you barely understand what's involved.
Question 3 - KNOWLEDGE (Where does your data live?)
Three possible answers:
1. One central repository (rare, ideal)
2. Scattered but organized
3. Completely disparate and scattered 🚨
If answer is #3:
"The first exercise will NOT be an automation. It will NOT be a vibe-coded app. The first exercise is getting your stuff in order so you're eligible to even benefit from AI."
The doctor's diagnosis framework:
What they present with:
- Use AI individually, no integrations
- Scattered knowledge across platforms
- Overwhelmed by options
- Want to "AI-ify everything"
- Spending days on LinkedIn

lessons.title: 3) Reading the Room & Red Flags 🚩
lessons.description:
How to diagnose three client scenarios and when to pump the brakes
24 minutes on pattern matching by company type, the recommendation framework that prevents scope creep, and the red flags that scream "this client will be a nightmare."
What you'll watch:
- Three company archetypes and what each one actually needs
- The platform prescription framework (ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini vs Custom)
- Blue sky scenario sessions that unlock hidden use cases
- Red flags that mean pump the brakes immediately
The core problem this solves:
Discovery calls fail because of noise on both sides:
- You're rambling, adding too much context
- They're confused, pivoting left/right/center
- The message gets lost in translation
Goal: Bridge the gap when articulation fails on one side and digestion fails on the other.
Scenario A - Small Team (5-20 people)
The setup:
- Everyone uses free ChatGPT
- Real startup, broke, maybe bootstrapped
- Core tools: Gmail, Notion, Slack
- Budget: $500-$2K/month (including your fee)
- Needs: Simple setup, quick wins, low overhead
The diagnosis:
If max budget is $2K and ChatGPT Teams is $25/person:
- 20 people × $25 = $500/month (25% of budget)
- If they're risk-averse and only spend $1K, that's 50% of their AI budget
Can you do everything with just ChatGPT? Yes.
Why: Gmail, Notion, Slack all have MCP servers/connectors → Everything can route through ChatGPT Teams → Add agent builder, custom GPTs, projects, workflows → They're set.
Your prescription: "Just pay for ChatGPT Teams. Higher security than free version. You can connect to any API with custom schemas if there's no native connector."
This team needs ONE tool done right, not 15 tools done poorly.
Scenario B - Mid-Size (21-100 people)
The setup:
- 10-15 paid AI tools
- Using each tool only 20% of potential
- Tools conflict (didn't know ChatGPT does images like Midjourney)
- Knowledge in multiple places
- Budget: $2K-$10K/month
- Needs: Phased rollouts, department focus, integration priority
The diagnosis:
Not broke. Not lacking resources. Lacking direction.
Your goal here: Small retainer to keep your foot in the door ($200-500/month)
Why: You stay embedded → Trusted advisor position → When workshop/roadmap opportunity comes up, you're already there
The phased rollout strategy:
1. Start with 10 power users (revenue-adjacent roles)
2. Run a pilot for 2-3 weeks
3. Get scattered data into central repo (Google Drive/Dropbox/OneDrive)
4. Cut 15 tools down to 5 (or even 3)
The secret weapon: Claude Code
"I don't care if you're not developers. I don't care if you're afraid of the terminal. This is your Swiss Army knife for building anything at scale."
Focus on sales/revenue-adjacent departments first.
Why: If they report to a board, they need tangible return on initial value (not ROI, but first taste of what's possible) → More budget gets unlocked → Skeptics start paying attention
The Blue Sky Scenario Session:
After 2-3 weeks of pilot success:
Bring in people who experienced the first "aha moment"
Ask: "Now that you know a sliver of what's possible, what would change your day-to-day? How could you become 10x more efficient? In a perfect world where AI didn't hallucinate, was affordable, what would you want to accomplish?"
Will you grant 90% of these wishes? No.
But you'll hear ideas from people who wouldn't otherwise speak up.
Take those ideas → Run them through RICE scoring framework:
- Reach: How many people affected?
- Impact: What gets solved?
- Confidence: Out of 100, can you actually build this?
- Effort: How much work required?
Use this to prioritize the blue sky ideas systematically.
Scenario C - Enterprise (100+ people)
The setup:
- Many systems (Workday, Greenhouse, Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho - 8 systems where 1 would satisfy a normal company)
- Heavily siloed knowledge
- Complex approval processes, bureaucracy, red tape
- Needs: Pilot programs, security reviews, change management
The brutal reality:
6-12 months to fix their system architecture → That's long-term, not immediate win
These enterprises need to LOOK like they're embracing AI (public/private shareholders) → They need shiny things that work NOW
Your hack: Build something that lives OUTSIDE their spaghetti back-end mess
Example use case: Knowledge sharing tool for 522 product SKUs
Vibe code something immediately accessible by dozens of users → One central knowledge repo → User research to populate it → Shows the org what "moving quick" looks like
Why you care about moving quick: Break down red tape ideologies, resistance to change, inspire them to throw resources at manifesting the AI dream
Enterprise warning: Never the same content twice. Each situation has unique political factors, EU regulations, medical constraints, controversial products, etc.
The Recommendation Framework:
Step 1 - Choose the platform (match requirements to tools)
ChatGPT when:
- Need broad integrations
- Google Workspace heavy
- Team collaboration required
- Want image generation + voice features
- Want one ecosystem (plus fine-tuning, assistants, agent builder)
Claude when:
- Deep reasoning required
- Complex analysis needed
- Document-heavy workflows (unless docs are too large)
- Research tasks
Gemini when:
- Google ecosystem locked in
- Video input required
- YouTube integration needed
- Cost-sensitive (very gracious free rate limits)
Custom Build when:
- Enterprise with resources
- Blue sky scenario made it obvious
- Production-level code deployed on cloud provider (not vibe-coded app)
- This is enterprise play only
Step 2 - Explain the trade-offs
Make it abundantly clear: "Your recommendation comes with sacrifice."
Example: They love Gamma's yellow/blue design. Claude's PowerPoints look more dry.
These are touchy-feely things you need to rip off like a bandaid.
Be politely savage: "Like a personal trainer, being nice when you're 100 pounds over your weight loss goal won't help. You need to cut refined carbs and hit the weights 5× a week."
The honest advisor play:
"I could charge you hourly and you'd need me for 6 months. I could accumulate billables. But I want to sleep at night knowing I gave you highest information density per minute. I want to get you there in as few steps as possible."
Step 3 - Create Plan B (the redundancy plan)
Every organization has its own heartbeat → Plan A might not work
Plan A roadmap: 30/60/90/180/365 days
Plan B: If you get crazy C-suite pushback or Luddites poison-pill the company
Backup options:
- Open source models?
- Self-hosted N8N instances?
- No licenses needed if someone blocks subscriptions?
Real talk: "I've been on calls with 10 people who hate my existence just because I'm trying to help them embrace AI. You won't always be seen as the hero. Sometimes you're the villain." 👀
Common pitfalls to warn them about:
Don't chase features
Don't overbuild (don't use 10 agents because YouTube made it look sexy)
Don't skip foundations
Don't rush rollouts
Don't ignore training (upsell opportunity for workshops)
Don't forget security (some companies will streak naked with customer data)
Build philosophy:
Simple > Sophisticated
Build 80% first, worry about 20% later
Foundational > Flashy (unless you need quick win for political reasons)
In-house > Agency (business requirements change constantly)
RED FLAGS - When to Pump the Brakes: 🚨
"We need AI agents NOW"
→ No you don't. You need to get your grip together. Even in best workflows, AI agents are used once or twice max. Determinism is the best way to build workflows.
"I saw this on LinkedIn"
→ Scope creep superhero. Red flag incarnate.
"My competitor has it"
→ You're building AI for wrong reasons. Different companies, different heartbeats, different bureaucracy. What works for them won't work for you.
"It MUST be cutting-edge"
→ Cutting edge = bleeding edge = high risk of bugs, landscape changes, technology shifts. Don't build enterprise-scale on something that came out last week.
When you see multiple red flags: Pump the brakes. These clients will be more headache than blessing.
The future-proof recommendation:
"Here's the minimum stack this organization needs. No matter what Gemini or Anthropic releases next month, stick to this. Even if it looks like you're behind for one month, next month you might have the leading model. Once this permeates through your team's lens of AI, you'll be the trusted advisor."
Next episode: Solution design - how to architect AI implementations that actually stick

lessons.title: 4) Solution Design & Pricing That Scales
lessons.description:
From chaos to systematic execution 🦾
23 minutes on mapping current state, building KPI frameworks that justify your work, and the pricing tier strategy that keeps clients in your ecosystem forever.
What you'll watch:
- The 4-part current state mapping framework (tools, knowledge, processes, owners)
- Baseline metrics that prove ROI before and after
- Pilot → Scale → Optimize rollout structure
- Pricing tiers from $10/month community to $15K+ enterprise
- The 60-minute call structure that closes deals
The confidence principle:
"The more systematic you are, the more they derive confidence from YOUR confidence about the process."
Even if they're not 100% sure how well you'll do, a solid systematized plan gives them confidence to move forward.
Part 1 - Current State Mapping (The Murder Mystery Framework)
Think of this like a murder mystery dinner: Someone murdered time. Someone murdered efficiency. Your job: Find out who, what, when, and why.
Worksheet 1 - Map All Current Tools:
Columns you need:
- Tool/Platform Name
- Type of Tool (AI or non-AI)
- Use Case (why they're using it)
- Department
- Number of Users
Why number of users matters: "If we do this first, it will affect 500 users. That's a third of your company." → Gives you ammo for prioritization.
Worksheet 2 - Find Knowledge Sources:
Map every single knowledge source:
- Notion, Slack docs, Confluence, Jira, internal wikis
- Type of document
- Connected AI tools (including quick and dirty RAG setups)
- Where it's stored
- How often it's updated (critical for RAG viability)
- Size and volume
The RAG decision questions (always ask these):
"What data types? PDF, doc, image, video?"
"How big? How many?"
This tells you: Can we dump this in a large context window, or do you need proper RAG? If RAG, what level? Quick and dirty? Hybrid RAG? Agentic RAG (10,000 pages → 100 page cheat sheet)?
Worksheet 3 - Document Processes:
Spend 1-2 hours mapping:
- Decision points
- Operational procedures
- Tacit knowledge they forget to mention
Warning: Even if they think they understand their own process, once you get into solutioning, tacit knowledge gaps will appear. This worksheet gives you a starting point.
Worksheet 4 - Identify the Owners (The Suspects):
Map who owns what:
- Business owners of each tool/process
- Technical owners (many companies have zero)
- Who holds the money (CFO? Controller? C-suite?)
Why this matters:
- Solutioning requires knowing the budget
- Understanding political allegiances ("Do you like Gamma because you've used it for 2 years, even though Claude 4.5 Sonnet now does the same thing?")
- Knowing who to contact for specific decisions
Part 2 - Baseline Assessment (Prove Your Value)
"Otherwise people will say we spoke to an AI consultant and accomplished nothing because you couldn't make it tangible."
Metrics that matter:
Before/After measurements:
- Time spent on manual tasks
- Error rates on processes (50 out of 100 applications are edge cases)
- Cost per transaction/output
- Employee satisfaction score
- Customer satisfaction metrics (if client-facing)
Target outcomes:
- % reduction in manual processing time
- Accuracy improvement targets
- ROI and cost saving goals
- User adoption rate targets
- Output quality improvements
The KPI framework structure:
For each metric, document:
- KPI name
- Baseline value
- Target value
- Measurement method
- Review frequency
Review frequency nuance: Don't measure day-by-day. Fridays = less productive. Mondays = less productive. Humans are humans. Weekly or monthly is better.
"The numbers are here as anchors for you. When you're grilled by C-suite, this is what you hold onto."
Part 3 - Implementation Rollout Strategy
Phase 1 - PILOT (30-60 days):
1. Select pilot group (count on one hand how many people)
2. Train core users
3. Run for 30-60 days
4. Collect feedback
5. Analyze results
6. Document wins in metric format (tangible reporting)
7. Document issues
8. Refine processes (assumed people always do X, Y, Z → turns out only 60% of the time)
Adherence metric: "What % of people doing X continue doing X every Y frequency?"
Phase 2 - SCALE:
1. Train next cohort (much bigger group)
2. Deploy to entire department
3. Monitor adoption and adherence
4. Track metrics from pilot issues
Phase 3 - OPTIMIZE (3-6-12 months):
Expand gradually across organization.
Reality check: Some departments don't need AI for a while. Some are so messy with technical debt it's not worth expanding until you have proven momentum.
Goals:
- Track KPIs
- Identify bottlenecks (they keep popping up)
- Stay on top of model changes (3.5 Turbo deprecated mid-2026)
- Critical gaps identified
- Recommended next steps
"As models change and deprecate, bottlenecks will continue. Your responsibility: Either YOU monitor this, or give the organization what they need to do it themselves."
Part 4 - Centralized Strategy Checklist
Before implementation, know:
- Where SOPs, customer data, company knowledge lives
- Tool structure (using MCP servers? Which ones and why?)
- If they use Zapier for 90% of things, does it make sense to say "switch to N8N" just because you prefer it?
- Assess connectors, custom APIs, etc.
Department priorities for pilots:
Revenue-adjacent = highest value. Even 1% incremental positive change = tangible benefit = wind under your sails.
1. Sales use cases:
- Lead research
- Email drafting
- CRM updates
- Qualification
- Follow-up sequences
- Meeting prep
2. Support use cases:
- Ticket summarization
- Response templates
- Knowledge search
- Escalation routing
- Customer history
- Auto-drafting replies (with human-in-the-loop approval)
Real example: E-commerce getting hundreds of tickets/day. Low-level informational tickets = autonomous response. Edge cases ("I ordered in Slovenia, now in Australia") = human intervention.
3. Marketing use cases:
- Content creation
- SEO research (very popular now)
- Social posting
- Ad copy
- Campaign analysis
4. Operations use cases:
- Process documentation
- Data cleanup
- Report automation
- Workflow optimization
Part 5 - Engagement Options & Pricing Tiers
The community downsell hack:
Instead of losing clients after 1-2 paid calls, create a mini-community (5-10 people):
"You don't have to pay $150/$500/$1000 for another 2-hour call. Get fractionalized access to me. I'll answer everyone's questions once a week in this community."
Could be on School, Circle, wherever. Price: $10-20/month.
Goal: Your foot stays in the door with people who otherwise wouldn't book another call.
Tight Budget Tier:
- $10-20/month community
- Your foot in the door
- Even if starting out, fractionalized access beats nothing
Medium Budget Tier:
- $250-500 per call
- Prepare beforehand (don't get informed minutes before)
- Minute 1 after weather talk = straight to value
- Bundle: Pay per session + answer X emails associated with the call
- Most popular: 15-minute calls (15 min prep, decent ROI)
Real Budget Tier (Retainer):
- $2K-10K/month
- Weekly check-ins
- Ongoing support by email (use templates so they don't send Hobbit essays)
- Small builds: GPTs, projects, automations you can repurpose
- Team training, priority access
- Bundles increase value
Template for decision point emails:
"When you send me a question about a decision point, frame it like: Problem, What You've Tried, Question."
Makes it easier for you to answer without syncing every time.
Enterprise Tier:
- $15K+ project-based
- Full implementation
- Multi-department engagements
- Subcontract developers to extend LTV and average order value
- Ongoing change management
- Public sector = way more expensive (nice numbers, but red tape/bureaucracy/audits)
"Instead of the Big Four getting it, why don't you get it?"
How to frame options when budget is unknown:
"I have three ways we can work together:
1. Most affordable: $20/month community
2. Middle ground: 30-minute or 15-minute call
3. Premium: Package of 4 one-hour calls for $1200"
Let them choose. Don't push. Having tiers increases likelihood of working together.
The brutal truth about knowledge vs relationships:
"In the next 12-18 months, all knowledge for knowledge's sake will be worthless. Relationships will be paramount."
Don't nickel and dime, but balance it. Don't be taken advantage of where they pay way less than the value you give in time spent.
Real example: Got email from former client about decision point. Two-sentence answer, one minute of thinking → Didn't charge. Pro relationship over nickel and diming.
Part 6 - Be The Calm In The Chaos
"When you converse with a client, be the person they come to for calmness, not agitation or friction."
Most people in this space = overwhelmed and lost (deservedly so).
Phrases to use:
- "Ignore the noise"
- "Start simple"
- "You're not behind"
- "We'll build this correctly together"
Position yourself as a PARTNER, not a dollar sign relationship.
Always have a next step before next session:
Examples:
- List all the tools you have
- Map all data locations
- Define three use cases
- Get budget approval (could take multiple sessions)
- Pick pilot department
- Assign a champion
The 60-Minute Call Structure:
0-5 min: Pain and goal
5-15 min: Real discovery
15-25 min: Diagnosing
25-35 min: Recommendation (walk through why it might not be realistic for their business)
35-40 min: Next steps
Call checklist:
- Start with pain/goal
- Ask scale questions
- Map their stack (mentally if you think in workflows)
- Find knowledge gaps
- Give HONEST advice
- Warn of pitfalls
- Show alternatives (different ways to solve same thing, which are quicker/cheaper)
- Offer pricing tiers
"The more reps you get in, the more readily available this information will be for you."

lessons.title: 5) The Call "Autopsy" Protocol 📲 🔎
lessons.description:
You're probably not as good at discovery calls as you think you are 😳
You can use Gemini 2.5 Pro's video input capability to audit your own performance, catch verbal and non-verbal mistakes you don't notice, and systematically improve every call you take.
What you'll watch:
- The "Arya" prompt system that reviews your calls frame-by-frame
- Scorecard framework: Clarity, vocal delivery, body language, questioning, client engagement
- Real autopsy of an actual client call (name redacted)
- How to catch filler words, pacing issues, and moments where clients disengage
- Why AI feedback hits different than human feedback
The brutal reality:
"You can get curveballs left and right. Sometimes it takes 30-40 minutes for someone to get to the point of why you're there to begin with. You assume it's either a good call or bad call, rarely neutral."
Most consultants take the outcome at face value. Winners dissect every call systematically.
The problem with self-assessment:
On the call:
- You think you're articulate
- You think you're smiley and composed
- You think your points are landing
Reality:
- You used 47 filler words in 15 minutes
- Client's body language showed confusion at 7:35
- You leaned back the entire call (signals low engagement)
- You spoke for 45 minutes of a 60-minute "discovery" call
You need an external audit. Human feedback = defensive reactions. AI feedback = neutral truth you can actually absorb.
The Arya System (Full Prompt Framework):
Persona setup:
"You are Arya, an expert in communication and executive presence coaching specializing in high-stakes B2B technology consulting. Your methodology combines principles of linguistic analysis, sales psychology, and nonverbal communication."
Task:
"Analyze the attached video. I need a comprehensive, brutally honest, yet productively constructive performance review. Your feedback should be direct, but framed as opportunities for improvement, focusing on actionable advice."
The scorecard categories:
1. Clarity (How clear were you?)
2. Vocal Delivery
3. Authority
4. Body Language and Presence
5. Questioning and Listening Skills
6. Client Engagement and Resonance (Did your points land?)
"These things you can see in person easily. Posture. Tone. But virtually, much harder to gauge on the fly."
How to use it:
1. Record call on Fireflies, Zoom, Google Meet, or Fathom
2. Download the video file (doesn't need high resolution)
3. Upload to Gemini (accepts video input)
4. Run the Arya prompt
5. Get timestamps, quotes, and specific moments to improve
Critical: Only do this if calls don't have sensitive client information.
Breakdown by principle requested:
Principle 1 - Clear Articulation:
- Are you structured and concise?
- Filler words and pauses (uhh, uh, you know)
Principle 2 - Verbal and Nonverbal Cues:
- Eye contact (or looking up when ideating)
- Posture and confidence
- Gestures (are you moving hands too much?)
Real comment from YouTube: "Someone told me to tie my hands together. Interesting. I actually more consciously see what I'm doing with my hands now."
Principle 3 - Client Analysis:
- Analyze CLIENT's body language throughout call
- Pinpoint moments they seem convinced, unsure, or skeptical
- Timestamps with quotes: "This is where you could have done better"
Principle 4 - Ask More, Say Less:
- Talk-to-listen ratio
- Are you giving them space to process?
Principle 5 - Storytelling and Analogies:
- Did you articulate thoughts in a consumable way?
- Did analogies land?
Output request:
"List top three most impactful things I can focus on to improve my next client call."
Real autopsy example (actual call breakdown):
Executive Summary:
"High energy session where your passion and deep subject matter expertise were clearly evident. This strength is currently coupled with a rapid-fire delivery that risks overwhelming the client and undermines your authority."
Scorecard results:
- Client Engagement: Amazing
- Questioning and Listening Skills: Clearly not the best
- (Other categories scored and broken down)
Actionable feedback received:
Issue 1 - Over-indexing on ideas:
"Your mind moves exceptionally fast. But you tend to present a fire hose of ideas without sufficient breaks for the client to process. At [timestamp], you rapidly list at least five distinct complex concepts in 12 seconds. This barrage can cause a senior client to disengage from the details and simply wait for the storm to pass."
Fix: Rule of Three
"When brainstorming, limit yourself to the top three points."
Issue 2 - Filler words:
Primary fillers: "like," "you know," "right" (with upward inflection)
"These are the most prevalent." [Shows exact timestamps where they occurred]
Real reaction: "I usually don't have these. I pride myself on speaking decently well presenting-wise. But in a call, you're taken from your zone of comfort into a different zone."
Issue 3 - Fear of silence:
"You often use a filler word to bridge the gap between your thoughts."
Nuance discovered: "I've used pauses before. Then clients will use it to speak over me."
Context matters for feedback interpretation.
Body language breakdown received:
Eye Contact:
"Strong and consistent. Minor natural breaks when thinking at 7:35 are acceptable."
Posture:
"You're leaned back for much of the call. While this can signal relaxation, for high-stakes C-suite conversation, slightly leaning forward non-verbally communicates higher engagement."
Context revealed: "This was me in a hotel in Dominican Republic working from there. I can totally see how I would be more relaxed than normal."
Gestures:
"Your energy is fantastic." (More praise than critique here)
Client body language analysis:
Moments of Conviction:
"Client was most engaged when you provided clear, actionable frameworks. He nods his head affirmatively and leans in slightly when you start to discuss concrete models like RICE."
Moments of Uncertainty:
"Client displayed contemplative or slightly overwhelmed body language when your pace accelerated." [Exact timestamps provided]
Speech mastery feedback:
Pacing issue:
"Many of your declarative statements end with an upward inflection. Like: 'And that's a huge opportunity, right?'"
Why this happens: "Sometimes I do that to make sure the person on the other end of the line is still with me."
AI catches the pattern you rationalize away.
Talk-to-listen ratio:
"Ratio is heavily skewed towards you talking."
Context provided: "This client wanted to listen more than they wanted to share with me."
Lesson: "Pull out some nuance. Keep in mind all your cases will be different."
Why AI feedback works better than human feedback:
"If anyone ever told me this themselves, I would definitely go into defensive mode. But because AI is neutral, I will take it more without the grain of salt."
Human feedback = ego protection kicks in
AI feedback = pure data you can act on
The systematic improvement loop:
1. Record every call (with permission)
2. Run Arya analysis within 24 hours
3. Note top 3 improvements
4. Practice those 3 things on next call
5. Re-analyze next call
6. Track improvement over time
Speaking headlines principle:
Instead of: Five complex concepts in 12 seconds
Do: Three clear points with pauses between
Instead of: 45 minutes of you talking
Do: 20 minutes talking, 25 minutes listening, 15 minutes co-creating
Instead of: Leaning back entire call
Do: Lean slightly forward during key moments
The compounding effect:
Week 1: Identify you use "like" 47 times per call
Week 2: Down to 23 times
Week 4: Down to 5 times
Week 8: Clients describe you as "articulate and confident"
Small fixes compound into massive presence upgrades.
Critical reminder:
"A lot of times you will get a client that pushes your boundaries of how well composed you can be. Other times, it's on you. You've had an off day, busy day, might not have given 100% to someone that paid for your time."
The Arya system catches both: When it's the client being difficult, and when you're off your game.
The full prompt will be attached to this post. Upload to Gemini. Get your autopsy. Improve systematically.
Next episode: Handling difficult clients and navigating political landmines in enterprise deals.

lessons.title: 6) The $20K Audit Automation (Yours Free)
lessons.description:
A client offered us $20,000 to build this system last year 💰
Today, you're getting it for free (sample output attached in first pinned comment)
Our community's automation genius
@Sydney Nichols
walks you through this entire build in the 25-minute video attached
What you'll watch:
- Full n8n workflow breakdown (Google Sheets → Perplexity → OpenAI → Gamma → Video)
- How to clone your voice with 11Labs for automated voiceovers
- Gamma API implementation for slide generation
- PDF.co for image conversion
- CreatorKit template setup for video assembly
- Real demonstration: Delta Airlines audit generated in 5 minutes
The backstory for the original request,
A year ago, a client offered us around $20,000 to build a system that automatically creates personalized audit presentations with voiceovers.
Back then, the technology wasn't quite there (or was really difficult to implement).
Now? Sydney built the entire thing in a few weeks
And we're giving you the complete workflow template 🫶🏽
Why this matters for consulting:
Instead of spending 3-4 hours creating a custom presentation for each prospect:
- Lead comes in → Workflow triggers
- Research happens automatically (Perplexity)
- Analysis runs (OpenAI comparing against your audit framework)
- Slides generate (Gamma API with your branding)
- Video assembles with YOUR voice (11Labs clone)
- Final deliverable in Google Drive in ~5 minutes
You go from manual proposal creation to scalable audit generation.
Prerequisites:
Required accounts:
- OpenAI (or preferred LLM)
- Perplexity API
- PDF.co (free tier works for low volume)
- Gamma (paid plan required for API access)
- CreatorKit
- 11Labs (paid plan recommended for quality voice cloning)
- Google Drive
---
We're moving some Thursday meetings to accommodate people setting this up
We think this automation will set you apart from other consultants
Plus: Personalized video with YOUR voice increases close rates
How to use it:
1. Watch Sydney's 25-minute walkthrough (attached)
2. Clone the n8n template (attached)
3. Add your API keys
4. Customize for your audit style
5. Join Thursday's clinic if you need setup help
The template, video walkthrough, and sample output are all attached to this post.

lessons.title: 7) Why Communication Is EVERYTHING 🗣️
lessons.description:
Communication beats coding & n8n ability. Every. Single. Time.
This video is way longer than usual, but I'm very passionate about this topic, because few things matter more
~40 minutes on the business communication rubric that determines whether clients hire you, trust you, and refer you—regardless of how well you know N8N or Claude Code.
What you'll watch:
- The university-level communication scoring system (verbal + non-verbal breakdown)
- Pre-call AI research workflow (Perplexity → N8N workflow generation in 15 minutes)
- Discovery framework: Problem scoping, requirements gathering, constraints
- The "ask why 5 times" technique for getting to root problems
- How to handle accents and improve articulation (speech coach hack)
The brutal truth: It doesn't matter how smart you are at coding, vibe coding, Make.com, N8N. If you suck at communicating, if you don't have proper verbiage, proper way to frame problems, show you're listening—it doesn't matter how good you are. You won't get clients. Or if you get clients, you won't retain them.
Communication is everything.
The background story:
This comes from teaching Business Communication Skills for 5 years at university.
Personal journey: Failed first presentation (got 51%). Eventually did so well they asked him to teach the course. Used to do competitive French and Spanish debates. Started as introverted, shy, pure nerd.
"My operating system is as dry as a rock. I've had to put makeup and teach myself to be a real human being over time instead of a bot that just does Claude Code."
The university rubric (adapted for consulting):
Original rubric: 5-minute presentation with PowerPoint, scored on:
- Setup/Agenda
- Body content
- Conclusion
- Transitions
- Message clarity
- Creativity
- Verbal communication
- Non-verbal communication
Real student example shown: 56 "ums" in 5-minute presentation.
"How many times do you think people got distracted or stopped paying attention? If you can't articulate a thought succinctly and concisely, it doesn't matter how great the solution is. People won't trust you."
Verbal Communication Breakdown:
Voice control:
- Speed
- Intonation (critical for authority)
- Projection
Clarity and fluency:
- Filler words (uh, um, like, you know)
- Every filler word = listener distraction
- Can't demonstrate mastery with constant fillers
The upward inflection problem:
Bad example: "Are you looking for some form of automation service?"
This sounds like you're asking permission, being subservient, at the beck and call of customer.
Good example: Downward inflection on declarative statements. Confident, not questioning.
"Many declarative statements end with upward inflection. Like: 'And that's a huge opportunity, right?' This undermines authority."
Non-Verbal Communication Breakdown:
Projected confidence and mastery:
- Shadowed people on first sales calls: "Complete fear in their voice"
- Everything was upward intonation
- Made them sound unconfident
Facial expression:
- Especially important on Zoom
- Technical folks often don't care about being animated
- Explaining with passion matters
"Non-technical people have advantage here. But if you're technical AND can do verbals/non-verbals, you become hard to beat on the battlefield."
Eye contact (critical on video calls):
"I visually look at the camera lens itself. I don't look at the screen. When delivering a message, I want to look piercingly through this camera to get to the other person."
Why it matters: Strong eye contact shows you're not lying. People who lie look to the side. Exaggerators look to the left.
"At some animalistic level, we can smell when the other person is not being forthcoming."
Hand movements:
- Less important on Zoom
- But be aware (someone on YouTube told him to tie his hands together)
- Now more conscious of hand gestures
Posture:
- Leaning back entire call = low engagement signal
- For high-stakes C-suite calls, lean slightly forward = higher engagement
The adapted rubric for consulting:
Setup and Agenda (Intro):
- Who are you?
- What makes you different?
- What's your goal? (intertwined with THEIR goal)
"Best relationships in business = aligned incentives. Shouldn't be zero-sum game."
Body (3-4 minutes):
- Walk through their problem
- Obsess about it
- Don't ask from ground zero (use qualifying questions beforehand)
Critical shift: "Instead of saying 'What problems are you trying to solve?' say: 'Based on my research, I feel like these are the top three things. Please correct me if I'm wrong.'"
This gives you different leverage. You've done homework. Easier to edit an essay you've written than stare at blank canvas.
Conclusion (Next Steps):
- Always book a call from a call
- Don't leave saying "I'll send you a proposal" and then get ghosted
- Get next meeting on calendar BEFORE ending current call
"Very different to be on someone's calendar vs in someone's inbox competing with all kinds of distractions."
Transitions:
- Don't be choppy
- Don't go from "Where are you from? Colorado? Cool. So what are you trying to accomplish?"
- Make smooth segues
Example: "I love Boulder. But let's discuss the boulder you're trying to push up a hill with this AI stuff."
"You may get a little chuckle. This person is not a bot or NPC. They have personality."
The pre-call preparation workflow (with AI):
Real example demonstrated: Coca-Cola Canada marketing client
Perplexity prompt used:
"I have a client coming in from Coca-Cola Canada. They're responsible for marketing. They're looking for help to build agentic or automation workflows. Go through all Coca-Cola's operations, find most eligible AI automation scenarios that are practical (not hype-based agentic stuff). Also research N8N's new Text to Workflow feature. Come back with prompts under 500 characters I could input into N8N to draft workflows, with rationale for why each workflow matters."
Takes 9-12 minutes to run.
Result: Multiple workflow prompts + business rationale + ready to demo
"In next minute this gives me something that's a cool talking point. I'm not just nodding and smiling saying 'yeah we can automate everything.' I can talk about THIS specific thing."
Why this works:
Most consultants: Generic discovery, asking basic questions
You: "Listen, based on my background research, here are the top three things. Now please correct me if I'm wrong."
Massive leverage shift. They respect that you did homework.
"It's way easier to make really quick progress in a conversation if you already have an assumption and you're opening yourself saying 'This is everything I could do before I met you. Roast me.'"
The automation for this:
"I literally automate this in the community. Every time someone joins, I have an AI agent do background research on them. Sends me email about it. Helps me know the types of folks coming through the door."
You can build same automation. Won't take too much time.
Initiative and resourcefulness principle:
From original rubric: Bringing a prop to make presentation lively
Consulting equivalent: Going the natural next step beyond just Googling the company
"Being hyper-prepared—not just knowing their name, where they work, what the company does—but trying to solve the problem before they've properly articulated it will make you look amazing."
The discovery framework:
Don't ask: "What's wrong?"
Ask: "What's the goal you're trying to achieve? What's the pain point that's prevented you from fixing it until now?"
Also ask: "Am I the first agency you're contacting? Or have you tried this in-house?"
This dissects political issues, bureaucracy, why you're actually there.
Problem scoping:
Don't ask: "What do you want to automate?"
Ask: "What repetitive, low-value tasks really waste your team's time that could be eliminated? What golden nugget tasks where your team spends 8 hours but it's responsible for 20% of revenue?"
The "ask why 5 times" technique:
Client: "We want to implement a lead qualification nurture system."
You: "Why?"
Client: "Our 3 salespeople are overwhelmed. We get a lot of lead flow."
You: "Why can't you just upskill them or have them work more?"
Client: "They work really hard. One is both an account exec and salesperson—filling two roles. We're waiting to hire after our next funding raise."
You: "Why are you waiting for funding?"
Now you've discovered: organizational issues, hierarchy problems, budget constraints, money about to arrive.
"Are you going to extort them because you know they're getting more money? No. But you might offer bigger scope projects with bigger budget because you know they can afford and rationalize it—as long as those projects actually benefit them, not just your pocket."
Requirements gathering (the "what" phase):
Turn vague into specific.
Client: "We have a lot of tickets in HubSpot"
You: "How many per day?"
Client: "Process is slow"
You: "Let's define slow. How many hours? Per day? Per week? Per month?"
Scope the problem in tangible metrics to work your way to solution.
The magic success question:
"Fast forward 6 months from now. This project is a wild success. Everyone's bought in. Your board loves you. What specifically changed in the business? What does that look like?"
Helpful to go blue sky, especially when someone's in the weeds memorizing YouTube tutorials about agents.
"As soon as I hear those words, I turn off my ears. I'm ready to go back to big picture. Step away from the tools. Let's talk about why we're actually here."
Identifying constraints:
Technical: "What platforms does this process touch? Talk to me dirty in data. Salesforce, HubSpot, QuickBooks?"
Budget (the elegant way):
Don't ask: "What's your budget?" (This sounds like: Are you poor? Can you afford nice stuff?)
Do ask: "Do you have any funds allocated in the company for these AI initiatives? Separate pot for that? Helpful for me to work backwards from your expectations."
"Notice how I'm not asking a very blunt, awkward question. Asking in elegant, confident manner makes you look better. You worry about them first, money second."
