The paraphrasing technique (active listening):
When you paraphrase what someone said—even just repeating their last word—it shows you're listening.
Example conversation:
"I'm so tired, been working on tutorials, haven't released YouTube video yet."
"No YouTube video released yet?"
"Yeah, it's hard to juggle everything..."
"Overwhelming?"
Done elegantly: "If I'm hearing you correctly, the real issue is X, not actually Y. Is that correct? Y is just the vehicle to solve X."
This is how you get to the bottom of things.
Talk-to-listen ratio:
Requirements gathering call: They speak 80%, you listen, you speak 20%
Paid guidance call: You speak 80%, they listen 20% (because they paid for your guidance)
The personality and charisma question:
"A lot of people struggle with: what are the micro actions that make someone charismatic? You can't just give a command 'be more charismatic.'"
Micro actions:
- Compliment something when they join ("Cool background," "Nice painting")
- Look for things to build rapport instead of having them handed to you
- Be proactive
"Society is dying in personalities, trust, ability to build relationships. If that trend goes downward, and we keep these values while everyone else is good at vibe coding but bad at communicating, you'll have an advantage."
Pre-call checklist:
Deep recon:
- Business model, revenue
- Key personnel
- Industry trends and competitors
- Recent news, launches (especially if they're in AI market)
Form hypothesis: Guess 2-3 pain points before the call
Have ready:
- Demo tabs open
- Case studies from prior engagements
- Screenshare prepped
- Pricing sheets or proposal drafts
"Sometimes failure is in the proposal. I've taken 72 hours to drop a proposal when I would've closed if it was done in 12 hours."
Even if client no-shows, not wasted effort. You build discipline and habit of being 10× more prepared.
Opening framework:
"Thanks for your time. I want to make sure this is valuable for you."
Obsess about their incentives. They're the only thing that matters on the call.
"Your goal is their goal."
The accent and articulation reality:
For non-native English speakers selling in English:
Real story: Friend (smaller YouTuber) had thick European accent affecting views. Hired speech coach on Upwork for 4-5 weeks of weekly exercises.
Result: "Not unrecognizable. Still has accent. Not magic. But way more articulate, way easier to listen to."
The truth: "Very unfortunate world we're living in. If you're not easy on the ears to someone native, the AI stuff is already complicated. Add accents, it's amplified complication. Even more subtle friction to conversation."
Two options:
1. Hire speech coach or articulation coach (not to remove accent, but articulate better in non-native language)
2. Use ChatGPT voice mode for free practice:
"Tell it: I have an accent. I want to practice. Help me focus on phonetics, pronunciations I get wrong."
Personal example: Before Spanish consulting call (first in a while), used ChatGPT voice mode to practice discovery questions. "It corrected my pronunciation because my Spanish was rusty. I'm not too proud to do this."
Also hiring speech coach in December: "For next year's podcast appearances. Sometimes I don't find words I'm looking for, look up a lot. Helpful to know where you have deficiency."
"If I'm not too proud, you shouldn't be too proud."
The call structure breakdown:
Pre-call: Due diligence
On-call: Discovery → Diagnosis → Solutioning → Close with next step
Post-call: Follow-up
All supported by communication fundamentals.
Why this matters more than technical skills:
"When ChatGPT-6 comes out and it's way better than you and me at articulating, researching, putting the whole picture together—the one thing humans will want is someone who has been there, done that. Has empathy for where they're struggling. Understanding of organizational behavior, human behavior. Can marry everything together."
Technical skills = one thing
Communication skills = the multiplier
"If you have no idea how to carry yourself in a way people want to work with you, they see you as advisor they see themselves working with 3-6-12 months from now, all technical learning will be in vain."
The 2-3 month project:
"This could be a 2-3 month project. It's not the fact you don't know N8N stopping you. It's the fact you can't communicate, or can't communicate effectively enough."
Focus on communication. Nothing else in this community or other communities will matter without it.

lessons.title: 8) The Chinese Menu Technique 🥡 🥢
lessons.description:
A client asked for our "Chinese menu" on a discovery call. We had no idea what they meant.
Now it's how we're going to approach closing even more education deals (and maybe you should too)
9 minutes on packaging your consulting services the way Fortune 500 companies expect to see them -and why this format makes clients say "we want 3, 4, 6, and the light version of 8."
What you'll watch:
- Why companies call this a "Chinese menu" (and how to build yours)
- The tier structure: Appetizers → Soups → Intermediate → Advanced → Desserts → Banquets
- Using Claude to auto-format professional Docx menus (ChatGPT can't do this yet)
- Real example: AI Workshop Menu 2025 with 30+ offerings
- How to use this for both education and technical consulting
The origin story:
Three discovery calls deep with a financial sector client. They asked: "Can you send us your Chinese menu?"
Response: "...deer in headlights. Had no idea what they were referring to."
Their explanation: "In a Chinese menu, you have tens or hundreds of items across 5-6 pages. Give us enough variety of what you could teach our team so they can pick specific items."
Built them a literal Chinese menu (not well formatted initially). Listed as Order #1, Order #2, Order #3, etc.
Closing call: "We want 3, 4, 6, and the light version of 8."
That's when it clicked. This is genius for packaging consulting services, especially education-heavy engagements.
Why this works:
Most consultants: Send vague proposal with 2-3 generic service tiers
You: Send menu with 30+ specific, categorized offerings
Result: Clients can mix and match exactly what they need. No full-length conversation needed—just pick from the menu.
The structure (5 tiers + bundles):
Appetizers (Beginner Level):
- ChatGPT Mastery (Zero to Hero)
- Claude Mastery
- Gemini Essentials
- Prompt Engineering 101
- Custom GPTs and Claude Projects
- AI for Daily Productivity
- Voice AI and Conversational Interfaces
- AI Search and Research
- Reasoning Models Deep Dive
- Memory and Personalization in AI
Why ChatGPT/Claude basics? "One of the best ways to get foot in door of all kinds of companies—SMBs to enterprises. They want teams to use ChatGPT the RIGHT way to fullest potential."
Custom GPTs = crowd-pleasers. Always include.
Soups (Practical Wins):
- Document Intelligence and Analysis
- AI Meeting Assistance and Transcription
- AI-Powered Research Workflows
- Data Analysis with AI
- AI for Presentations and Storytelling (Gamma, Beautiful AI)
- Building Prompt Libraries
- AI for Social Media Content
These are quick wins that show immediate value.
Intermediate Level:
- How Transformers Work (nuts and bolts of LLMs)
- Image Generation Mastery
- Video Generation Tools
- Multimodal AI Applications
- RAG Implementation (big crowd-pleaser)
- MCP Server Development
- Agentic Workflows
- Coding Basics for Non-Developers (0 to 30%, not 80%)
Note: "How Transformers Work is not common request. But it has happened. Some companies appreciate the nuts and bolts."
Advanced Level:
- AI Coding Tools (Cursor, Windsurf, Replit)
- Claude Code and Computer Use
- Building Production AI Agents
- API Integration Without Coding
- Building AI-Powered Apps
- Voice Agent Development
- Custom Automation Platforms
This is where you can offer "light versions"—like showing tools without teaching coding behind them.
Desserts (C-suite Oriented):
- AI for Business Strategy
- AI for Investment and Due Diligence
- AI for HR and Talent Management
- AI for Marketing Transformation
- AI for Sales Enablement
- AI for Finance and Operations
Why separate C-suite tier? "Core employees want meat and potatoes. C-suite wants 100,000-foot view. Even telling them there's N8N and Make.com is reasonable amount of information."
C-suite questions: Which tool lets employees go faster? Which is cheaper at scale? Which has better security? Where can we get a deal?
Banquet Options (Pre-Packaged Bundles):
Instead of clients picking 15 individual items, offer pre-curated packages:
Banquet A: Foundation Package
Banquet B: Technical Implementation Package
Banquet C: Leadership Strategy Package
Banquet D: Full Transformation Package
Banquet E: Custom Tailored (they pick)
What's included with banquets:
- Template library
- Custom GPT setup guides
- Workflow automation templates
- 30-day post-workshop email support ✨
The upsell opportunity: "This could be your entry point to offer small retainer or big retainer, depending on who you're dealing with."
The formatting hack (Claude only):
ChatGPT can't do this level of Docx formatting yet. Claude can.
How to use it:
1. Enable Claude's code execution and file creation in Settings → Capabilities
2. Upload the template Docx (attached to this post)
3. Ask Claude to customize for your offerings
4. It creates high-fidelity formatted documents
Real prompt used: "Do deep research on latest and greatest topics in AI. Supplement with my old workshop lectures. Create Chinese menu format."
Result: Professional AI Workshop Menu 2025 (attached)
Each item includes:
- Module name
- Duration (90-150 minutes delivery time, NOT prep time)
- Difficulty tier
- Brief description
The delivery details section:
Mix and Match: Choose any combination to create your perfect workshop
Duration: 2-2.5 hours per session
Format: Remote or on-site (specify both options)
Updated: October 2025 (update monthly as tools change)
Footer message: "Serving fresh AI knowledge updated for October 2025. Things will change, tools will change, models will change."
This signals you stay current and clients need ongoing support.
How to customize for your niche:
Take this structure, adapt categories:
For automation consulting:
- Appetizers: Basic n8n/Make workflows
- Soups: Quick automation wins

lessons.title: 9) Learning from Failure
lessons.description:
A Consultant's Reflection 💡Today I walk you through a rare but recent failure of mine.
We're dissecting a $300 refund and the Golden Parrot Strategy that would've prevented it 🦜
13 minutes on why correct advice delivered lazily loses clients, how to package borrowed expertise as white-glove service, and the 3 red flags that should've made me reject this consult.
What you'll watch:
- Real refund breakdown: PPC ads consulting gone wrong
- The "Golden Parrot Strategy" for repackaging existing solutions
- Red flags that scream "reject this client"
- How to over-deliver when you're not the domain expert
- Why clients care more about packaging than correctness
The brutal stats:
Only refunded 2 consults since starting Prompt Advisers in 2022.
Most recent one: Few weeks ago.
Instead of hiding it: Full autopsy of what went wrong.
The setup:
Client came through Fiverr (rare, supposed to be blocked but they'd consulted before so permissions allowed it).
Their requirements:
1. Upskill PPC team on Google Ads, Meta Ads, TikTok Ads with AI
2. Generate super advertisement concepts for clients
3. "Use AI, practical usage, not just show us ten tools and good luck" ⚠️
4. After consultation, show team how to use AI for e-commerce ads, product ads, UGC ads, lead ads, building media assets
Context: English not their first language. European client. High expectations from someone with zero background in those areas.
Red flag #1 that should've made me reject:
"Practical usage, not just show us ten tools and good luck."
This was the clue. Don't be lazy in delivery. But I missed it.
Red flag #2 that should've made me reject:
"I don't have a background in pay-per-click. I know how it works. I've done a project involving it. But I'm not an industry expert."
I'm good at AI. I'm good at solutioning tools and neural pathways. But this person wanted specific, critical path solution. Not general conceptualization.
Red flag #3 that should've made me reject:
The scope was massive for 30 minutes. Building media assets, UGC ads, product ads, lead ads—all specialized domains I haven't run campaigns in.
"I know how to build UGC using Sora and Veo. Technically, I got it on lock. But I assumed I could just give instructions and they'd be happy."
Wrong assumption.
What actually happened on the call:
Gave honest disclaimer: "I'm not a PPC expert. You probably realized that from our first consult (which was SEO-focused)."
Gave rational advice: "There's this person, Robon August. Decent-sized YouTube channel, big community. He specializes in UGC,

lessons.title: 10) Create AI Workshops in Minutes
lessons.description:
If you want a cheat code for creating tailored workshops, this is for you.
I recently generated an entire workshop deck at 11pm, and presented first thing the next day - the client had no idea it took 60 minutes (of which, I just dictated feedback for 10).
15 minutes on using Claude's deep research + docx generation to create fully customized workshop decks with client brand colors—and why "old-school" PowerPoint beats AI-looking Gamma slides for enterprise credibility.
What you'll watch:
- The 2-step Claude workflow: Research → Deck generation
- Real example: Serhant real estate company N8N automation deck (13 pages of research → 10-slide presentation)
- How to work around context window limits
- ChatGPT Enterprise workshop deck generated from scratch
- Why traditional PowerPoint format has "knowledge arbitrage" over Gamma
---
The 2-step workflow:
Step 1 - Deep Research (35 minutes):
Prompt given:
"Research the company Serhant and come up with a list of areas and/or departments where they may benefit from adding N8N automations in the business. Be specific and dive deeper into what opportunities in 2026 they may be able to tap into using these automations."
Used Claude's deep research feature (more comprehensive than normal search).
Trade-off: Deep research burns context window faster, so plan accordingly.
Step 2 - Synthesize to Docx:
Prompt: "Can you create a fully synthesized document based on your research that I could pass on to my team? Create a docx file that's well-formatted and has really easy-to-read sections."
Result: 13-page formatted document with:
- Executive summary
- Lead management automation opportunities
- Intelligent lead intake and enrichment
- Multi-channel lead responses
- Predictive lead scoring
- All the gold you need for deck creation
Why docx first? "Crystallize all research so I can take this document as context for new chat."
Context window workaround: Research in one chat → Export docx → Import to new chat for deck creation → Preserves tokens
The deck generation step:
New chat prompt:
"I want to create a full deck, ideally optimized to Serhant's (the real estate firm) brand colors. I want to put together a full 10-page deck walking through and explaining how they would use and apply N8N. I've attached a document with key opportunities. It's important we create a slide deck to educate them on the discovery, what N8N is, how it works at high level, and how they could benefit from it."
Added: Design instructions for how PowerPoint should look
What Claude did automatically:
1. Found Serhant logo online
2. Extracted brand colors (dark blue hex codes)
3. Applied branding throughout deck
4. Created 10 professional slides
The deck structure created:
Slide 1: Title with actual Serhant logo (didn't have to upload it)
Slide 2: What N8N is (open-source workflow automation platform)
Slide 3: How N8N works (triggers → actions → workflow)
Slide 4: Game-Changing Features (Text-to-Workflow, Agent Builder, 400+ integrations, Error Recovery)
Slide 5: Serhant's Key Automation Opportunities (specific to their business)
Slide 6: Implementation Strategy and Roadmap
Slide 7: Expected ROI and Benefits (detailed breakdown)
Quality: "This is an example of a slide I would literally create myself, and it would probably not look as good, especially if I didn't use something like Gamma."
The ChatGPT Enterprise workshop example:
Different use case: Generic workshop deck (not company-specific)
Prompt given:
"Research all the latest and greatest features in ChatGPT that an enterprise interested in real estate would care about as of October 2025. Do full research on whether it's YouTube channels, OpenAI's website, all the features in ChatGPT that they would care about."
Time anchoring: "October 2025" tells Claude to prioritize sources from that time period
"I purposely gave a very crappy prompt to show you it will take all the heavy lifting."
What Claude found:
- GPT-5 launch (August 2025)
- Atlas browser (days old)
- Apps in ChatGPT
- Company Knowledge feature (shared RAG resource for Teams plan)
- Zillow app integration with connectors
Initial request: 20 pages (topped out context window)
Adjusted: 10 pages (moved research to new chat for more room)
The resulting deck structure:
Slide 1: Workshop goals
Slide 2: ChatGPT Enterprise overview
Slide 3: Features of ChatGPT Enterprise
Slide 4: GPT-5 intelligence (core facts about the model)
Slide 5-6: Company Knowledge feature (benefits, what it does, why it matters)
Slide 7: "Built for Real Estate" (Zillow app integration, example queries)
Slide 8: Competitive advantage of using ChatGPT
Slide 9: Enterprise security
Slide 10: Real estate use cases at a glance
All tailored to real estate industry automatically.
---
The knowledge arbitrage principle:
Old-style PowerPoint vs Gamma-style slides:
Gamma aesthetic: "People of the old world see Gamma and think: 'Oh, this is AI-generated stuff. You probably didn't put in the work.'"
Traditional PowerPoint aesthetic: "Things might not be as shiny and beautiful, but structured in a way enterprises and businesses have been used to. They'll think you did this pretty much all yourself."
Two wins:
1. Cheat time (1 hour instead of 8-10 hours)
2. Display yourself in best way possible (looks like manual effort)
Knowledge arbitrage = AI-generated content that doesn't look AI-generated
When to use this workflow:
Paid workshop prep: Client-specific decks in 1 hour
Free webinar strategy: "If starting out, cheat code is doing things for free. Session on ChatGPT, Claude AI, anything—super helpful."
Last-minute presentations: 11pm to midnight → ready for 9am delivery
RFP responses: Show deep company knowledge fast
Discovery call follow-ups: "Here's what I researched about your company"
The prep-to-delivery ratio transformation:
Old way:
- Manual company research: 3-4 hours
- Synthesize findings: 2 hours
- Design deck: 3-4 hours
- Format and polish: 1-2 hours
- Total: 9-12 hours
New way:
- Claude deep research: 35 minutes (automated)
- Generate docx: 5 minutes
- Generate deck: 15 minutes
- Review and tweak: 5 minutes
- Total: 1 hour
Time savings: 8-11 hours per deck
How to extend this for different industries:
Real estate: N8N automations, ChatGPT listings, AI property analysis
Financial services: Compliance automation, risk analysis workflows
Healthcare: Patient intake automation, appointment workflows
E-commerce: Inventory management, customer service automation
Legal: Document review, case research automation
Just change the company name and industry focus in prompts.
Pro tips for best results:
1. Use deep research when possible (more comprehensive, burns context faster)
2. Always include time anchor ("as of October 2025") to prioritize recent sources
3. Export research to docx before deck generation to preserve context window
4. Specify brand colors or let Claude find them automatically
5. Start with 10 slides, extend to 20-30 after initial generation if needed
6. Give specific page count to avoid context window errors
7. For paid clients: Always review and add personal touches (but 80% is done)
Common mistakes to avoid:
→ Don't try to do everything in one chat (context window will die)
→ Don't use Gamma-style if presenting to traditional enterprise audience
→ Don't skip the research docx step (you'll need it for reference)
→ Don't forget to specify industry/audience (generic decks are weak)
→ Don't present without reviewing (Claude occasionally hallucinates features)
The competitive advantage:
Most consultants: Spend days researching and building custom decks
You: 1 hour from research to final deck
Your client: Thinks you spent days on their custom presentation
The value perception stays high while your time investment drops 90%.
Why this matters for consulting business:
→ Lets you take on more clients (1 hour vs 10 hours per deck)
→ Last-minute opportunities become possible (11pm request → 9am delivery)
→ Free workshops become viable (low time investment for lead generation)
→ Proposal quality increases (every prospect gets custom deck)
→ You can test new industries without massive prep overhead
Files attached to this post:
1. Serhant N8N Automation Research (docx) - 13-page research output
2. Serhant N8N Workshop Deck (pptx) - 10-slide presentation example
3. ChatGPT Enterprise Real Estate Workshop (pptx) - Generic industry deck
Instructions:
1. Copy prompts from txt file
2. Modify company name and industry
3. Run deep research in Claude
4. Export to docx
5. Import to new chat
6. Generate deck
7. Review and present

lessons.title: 11) Understanding AI: Bridging Traditional Machine
lessons.description:
Learning & Generative AI 🌐Machine learning used to be my one true (nerdy) love. And then, Gen AI was born, and most people stopped caring about it.
One skill separates six-figure consultants from prompt engineers cosplaying as experts: knowing when NOT to use ChatGPT (and language models)
32 minutes on why your banking app still uses ML (not GenAI) for credit approvals, the decision framework that makes you sound like you actually know what you're doing, and the job description hack that reveals what enterprises really pay for.
What you'll watch:
- Why traditional machine learning still runs most apps on your phone
- Supervised vs unsupervised learning explained for consultants (not engineers)
- The decision framework: When to use GenAI, ML, or hybrid
- Reading job descriptions as a backdoor to what clients actually need
- 12 real-world scenarios broken down (fraud detection, forecasting, sentiment analysis)
The background context:
10 years working not in generative AI, but with:
- Machine learning models
- Stats
- Prediction models
- Regression algorithms
- Natural language processing (learned manually, before you could just use natural language)
"There's an appreciation for techniques that existed before you could just use natural language to accomplish X."
The job description cheat code:
Weekly LinkedIn observation: 90% of roles wanting GenAI/LLMs also require machine learning background.
Example shown: Assistant Vice President of AI Engineering
Requirements include:
- Design, build, and serve LLMs to solve complex business challenges
- Experience in AI engineering and/or machine learning with focus on LLMs (NLP)
- Deep expertise in writing, reviewing, and productionizing code
Key insight: "This literally gives you a backdoor cheat code into what people, what teams, what companies, what enterprises are really looking for."
Pro tip: "If you want to get into consulting, look at the jobs for what companies are actually hiring for and read those descriptions."
Why companies still need traditional ML:
The scale problem: "At scales of millions of computations per day, you probably still won't use GenAI. It's still way too expensive."
Real examples from daily life:
Banking apps: Still using ML to predict mortgage/credit card/loan approvals (not GenAI)
Biometric analysis: Driver's license verification uses older-school image recognition models with ML, not vision LLMs
Why? "Vision's even expensive. Even going down open source route hosting something like Llava, it's still not fully there at scale, especially with hallucination rates."
The old world advantage: "A lot more determinism and reliability."
The two main ML categories:
Supervised Learning (predicting something specific):
Definition: Had something you were predicting for, with labeled training data
Examples:
- Classification: True/false outcomes (Should this person be approved for loan? Yes or no)
- Multi-class classification: Non-binary outcomes (If approved, what loan amount? 6+ possibilities)
- Regression: Predicting continuous values (exact loan amount, not just categories)
Real use case: "We would create algorithms that would go through series of training data to see whether or not I could get pattern recognition of what an avatar looks like from an element standpoint of someone who should be trusted with a mortgage."
The modern advantage: "You now have luxury I didn't have. I had to write all this code by hand, figure out libraries and weird errors by hand. Things like Claude Code, Cursor can now automate the process of writing a lot of this ML code."
Unsupervised Learning (algorithm learns patterns itself):
Primary technique: Clustering
Definition: "You don't tell anything to the AI. You give it a dataset and it tries to mathematically find ways to look for similarities based on what it sees."
Visual explanation: "It tries to segregate a dataset into different groups."
Real example: Customer segmentation at Coca-Cola
"If you had customer dataset, I used to do segmentation. Give it all customer data and say: Go find me who these people are based on attributes without me telling you who they should be."
Result: Algorithm created 6-8 clusters of customer types without being told what to look for.
Why it matters for consulting:
Most YouTube rhetoric: "All you need to do to get $50K-$80K a month is learn AI automation, build automations, sip margaritas on beach with Lambo."
Reality: "Probably not going to work in most companies. You need combination of this AI-generalist skillset."
The positioning advantage: "Being aware of other types of AI will separate you from most consultants who only know ChatGPT."
The 12 scenario breakdown (ML vs GenAI vs Hybrid):
Scenario 1 - Fraud Detection:
Recommendation: Machine Learning
Why: "Scale, cost, need for accuracy. Every transaction going through thousands per second—can't use GenAI at that price point and accuracy requirement."
ML methods: Anomaly detection, pattern recognition on structured transaction data
Scenario 2 - Customer Support:
Recommendation: Generative AI (with some ML)
Why: "Natural language, creating responses, talking to humans—GenAI's bread and butter."
Possible hybrid: ML for routing/categorization, GenAI for response generation
Scenario 3 - Lead Scoring:
Recommendation: Machine Learning primarily
Why: "Predictive analytics on structured data (job title, company size, engagement metrics). This is regression/classification territory."
Could add GenAI: For analyzing unstructured data like email content tone
Scenario 4 - Content Personalization:
Recommendation: Hybrid (ML + GenAI)
Approach: "ML can do initial segmentation and preference prediction. GenAI can generate the actual personalized content."
Example: ML determines user cluster, GenAI writes email copy for that segment
Scenario 5 - Pricing Optimization:
Recommendation: Machine Learning
Why: "Math-heavy, needs precision, structured data about costs/demand/competition. This is regression models territory."
Not GenAI: "Don't want hallucinated pricing that could cost millions"
Scenario 6 - Chatbots:
Recommendation: Mostly GenAI (some ML for intent classification)
Modern approach: "LLMs are so good at conversation now. But ML can help with intent detection routing before GenAI generates response."
Scenario 7 - Churn Prediction:
Recommendation: Machine Learning
Why: "Predicting binary outcome (will churn/won't churn) based on usage patterns, payment history, support tickets—structured data perfect for classification models."
Could add GenAI: To generate personalized retention messages once ML identifies at-risk customers
Scenario 8 - Recommendation Systems:
Recommendation: Hybrid (but complex)
Confession: "Back in the day, recommendations were a pain in the behind to build. They're not easy systems. They're very math heavy."
Challenges: "Could be: What's wrong with my dataset? My algorithm? My code? All of it? Am I even recommending based on right thing? Am I missing data completely?"
Modern approach: "Sprinkling in some GenAI can alleviate a lot of manual work you'd have to do otherwise."
Scenario 9 - Image Analysis:
Recommendation: Depends on scale
Low scale (hundreds/thousands weekly): GenAI could work. "We have enough cheap models that do vision if there's error tolerance of 15-20%."
High scale: Traditional computer vision (ML/deep learning). "Use convolutional neural networks."
Personal example: "Took me 3 months to build an algorithm that could look at Nike logo and tell you it's different from Pizza Hut logo. Just identifying that was an ordeal."
Fraud detection use case: "If someone uploads their license, is this the person they say they are—ML would be ideal. GenAI would be more accurate, but not financially viable yet due to scaling and cost."
Scenario 10 - Document Processing:
Recommendation: Flexible (could be ML + GenAI + combination)
Why: "Depends on what you're extracting and how much structure the documents have."
Scenario 11 - Forecasting:
Recommendation: Machine Learning
Techniques mentioned:
- ARIMA (autoregressive integrated moving average)
- SARIMA (seasonalized version for data with seasonal patterns)
The modern advantage: "These things I had to learn writing by hand on an exam, you can avoid that pain with GenAI to help you write it. But the algorithm itself should ideally be ML driven."
Use Claude Code/Cursor: "Have it help you write these algorithms. You might not even have to know what's happening."
Scenario 12 - Inventory Optimization:
Recommendation: Machine Learning
Why: "Because of how many different metrics and elements you have, numbers, volume—ML probably best."
Bonus Scenario - Sentiment Analysis:
Recommendation: 95% Generative AI
Why it's controversial: "Back in the day with NLP was a pain. Had to remove punctuation, make everything lowercase, attach valence (whether something's good, neutral, or bad)."
Hybrid approach shown: "Use NLP to look for keywords that are valent (free, deterministic). Python libraries can detect whether keywords point to negativity or positivity. Then feed to GenAI (paid or open source) that's really good at language."
Benefit: "If you want deterministic failsafe, you can have both coexist."
The decision framework cheat sheet:
Use Generative AI when:
- Creating content
- Talking to humans (or AI talking to humans)
- Unstructured data
- Creative output
- Don't need 100% accuracy (95% okay, some tolerance)
Use Traditional ML when:
- Predicting something
- Need 95%+ accuracy
- Numeric in nature
- Very structured data (math-heavy, mathematical features)
- Money at scale is involved (cost matters)
Use Hybrid when:
- Complex workflows
- Multi-step workflows
- ML does math/categorization/clustering
- GenAI interprets results of that clustering ("That could be the superpower")
The cold truth about AI projects:
"70% of AI projects fail."
Why? "Because people are obsessed with outcomes and with mediums to accomplish those outcomes. Not the journey of going from zero to building proper infrastructure."
They can't write a prompt and have it work on first try, so they give up.
The success path formula:
1. Start small (don't try to transform everything at once)
2. Be vigilant on which approach fits (ML vs GenAI vs hybrid)
3. Focus on 1-2 high-impact use cases first
4. Pilot before scaling
5. Invest in training clients: "I just taught you a sliver about machine learning. Will you retain most? Probably not. But I opened the doors that this other world exists."
Why this knowledge matters:
"Most companies, most things on your phone that you rely on when you log in every day and it does stuff reliably and there's some form of AI in it—most of the time, no GenAI. For now, at hyper scale."
"Good to know this exists and be aware of it because it will make you sound that much smarter in your next engagement."
The positioning differentiation:
90% of "AI consultants": Only know ChatGPT, Claude, prompt engineering
You: Understand when to recommend traditional ML, when to use GenAI, when to combine both
Result: You can actually solve problems that need deterministic math at scale, not just chatbot interfaces.
Real-world application for consultants:
When client says: "We want to use AI to predict customer churn"
Bad consultant response: "Great! We'll build a ChatGPT agent to analyze your customers"
Good consultant response: "This is a classification problem with structured data. We need ML for prediction accuracy. We could add GenAI to generate personalized retention messages once ML identifies at-risk customers. Let me show you the hybrid approach."
The expertise signal: Understanding which tool for which job makes you sound like you actually know what you're doing.
Why most consultants miss this:
The old AI isn't sexy. "Sexy does not always mean ROI."
ML is harder to understand than "just prompt ChatGPT"
YouTube doesn't teach regression algorithms (not clickable)
But: "Most of what runs reliably at scale still uses traditional ML."
The action plan:
1. Don't try to become ML engineer overnight
2. Understand the categories (supervised, unsupervised, GenAI, hybrid)
3. Know the decision framework (when to use what)
4. Read job descriptions to see what companies actually need
5. Position yourself as AI generalist who knows full spectrum
"You want to be astute and competent in all the main areas that end up weaving together, whether you like it or not."
The final positioning:
You're not just an "AI consultant" who knows prompts.
You're a consultant who understands:
- When GenAI is perfect
- When it's completely wrong tool
- When traditional ML is required
- How to combine both for superpowers
That knowledge alone separates you from 90% of people calling themselves AI consultants.

lessons.title: 12) Mastering AI Consulting: Be An AI Architect
lessons.description:
Effective Solution ArchitectureKnowing every AI tool makes you useless - knowing WHICH tool for WHICH problem makes you irreplaceable 👑
24 minutes on the solution architect framework that turns you from "I can build automations" into "I'll guide you through the minefield"—and why one deep ecosystem beats shallow knowledge of 50 tools.
What you'll watch:
- The 4-category framework: 1 chat platform, 1 LLM, 1 automation tool, 1 IDE
- The "tree" concept: Going deep vs wide in AI knowledge
- Real trash vs treasure breakdown (what to avoid recommending)
- Live Google Colab + Gemini ML demo (weeks of work → 5 minutes)
- How to simplify client tech stacks and look like a hero
The core problem:
"Every single day you open this community, there are new frameworks, new tools, new updates, new models. For people very into this, like yourself and myself, this is overwhelming but at least we're used to it."
"For someone who's a business owner running a business, they barely have enough time to walk through this minefield, let alone the minefield of day-to-day."
The solution architect mindset:
"Our goal every single time we're doing solution architecture or solution consulting: We want to lead them. Here's A, here's B, here's every tool that would probably be thrown your way to solve this thing. But I know about this tool or this framework that'll make it faster."
That's where you derive your core value.
The 4-category framework:
If pitching to overwhelmed, scattered, stressed client with FOMO about AI:
1. One chat platform (ChatGPT or Claude)
2. One LLM (go deep in its ecosystem)
3. One automation tool (Zapier, Make, or N8N)
4. One AI IDE (Cursor, Windsurf, or Replit)
"Each one gives you enough of a product tree or domain tree that will allow them and you to go really deep in this topic."
The "tree" concept explained:
Example: Mastering Claude (not even Claude Code, just Claude front-end)
The tree branches:
- Step 1: Learn how to prompt
- Step 2: Make a Claude Project (what makes it effective vs not)
- Step 3: Explain RAG while teaching projects
- Step 4: Using connectors (requires explaining MCP concept)
- Step 5: Building custom MCPs
- Step 6: Managing context windows
- Step 7: Memory feature
- Step 8: Chat retrieval feature ("Give me every chat ID where I mentioned X")
Timeline: "Just with one topic, assuming your client or their employees are starting at zero, this would take a couple weeks just to get them acquainted with this model."
The depth principle: "All you need is just to show them one piece of each domain and it will inspire them to provoke the questions of what needs to happen to go deeper."
Category 1 - Chat Platforms (pick one, go deep):
Don't overwhelm with options. Pick ChatGPT or Claude and master the ecosystem.
The value isn't knowing both shallowly. It's knowing one deeply enough to solve 90% of use cases.
Category 2 - Language Models (know when to use which):
Don't just say "compare all these LLMs for our use case" (they can do that themselves).
Become useful by knowing specialized features:
Claude for Financial Services:
"You're a financial services company? Claude came out with Claude for Financial Services. They have Excel add-in that can meet you and your employees where you're at, tabulate everything in cells without uploading anywhere."
This is specific service Claude offers. Showing you know this = instant credibility.
Gemini for SOPs:
"If they don't have SOPs in business (most businesses have zero SOPs or their SOPs suck), what can you do to help them alleviate?"
Solution: "Instead of having them document things using grunt work, what if they did screen recording using Loom doing the task? Upload to Gemini: 'Watch this video and make an SOP.' You can do this at scale."
Bonus vibe coding trick: "While call is happening, you could go to AI Studio, vibe code as you're speaking, use Gemini 2.0 Flash with video understanding, make an app for them where they could upload this, white label it with your service name. Now you've created value out of thin air that looks like magic."
ChatGPT for Company Knowledge:
"Recently released something called Company Knowledge. Instead of individual RAG, you now have company RAG that you can tap in all core knowledge everyone in company should access."
Demo shown: Prompt Advisors agency → Plus button → Company Knowledge → Can be rooted in different drives
"The value of staying on top of things—you might not need to have done or used the element. Might be useful to just know that it exists."
The golden BB example:
Client problem: "Do you have good person for logos? We like logos in ChatGPT but I wish I could make slight modifications to text and style."
Bad consultant: "Let me find you a designer"
Good consultant: "You can go on AI Studio right now using Gemini 2.0, and it probably will be able to take care of it for you without hiring anyone."
"These are golden BBs or silver bullets that elevate you. You're knocking through pain points they thought would cost hundreds if not thousands of dollars and hundreds if not thousands of hours, solving them in minutes."
Category 3 - Automation Platforms (pick one ecosystem):
"I don't care which one you like. Whatever one you choose, make sure you understand the ecosystem of that tool."
Zapier ecosystem:
- Zapier Agent Builder
- Highest number of native integrations
- ~3000+ integrations
Make.com:
- Around 3000 integrations
- Different strengths
N8N ecosystem (example deep dive):
- Least number of native integrations
- Most malleable and versatile platform
N8N ecosystem mastery example:
Client question: "We only have 5000 records. Should we use Google Sheet and hook that up? Or Airtable?"
Amateur response: "Let's use Google Sheets"
Expert response: "Because I know N8N ecosystem, I know they have data tables now which is a substitute for Google Sheet. We don't have to create a Google Sheet, then create authentication for Google in N8N and go through those motions, especially for company that doesn't want unnecessary friction."
The ecosystem knowledge cascade:
From knowing N8N deeply, you can explain:
- What vector storage is
- What Pinecone is
- Differentiate between types of vector databases
- Explain that if you want best of both worlds (Postgres + vectorization), use Supabase or PG Vector
- When to use Airtable vs alternatives
"Knowing what options are there and knowing when and why you choose one vs the other is most important."
Real cost example: "Airtable is really expensive if you have multiple users. Many seats, that bill can add up. I used to pay $500-600 without knowing it just for seats. Once I understood Supabase is $10/month to spin up instance, and won't change as long as I don't explode number of transactions, this became really good."
When to use SQLite: "If it's super straightforward database, you have so many options. Goal is that you know not just names of options, but when you might want to use them."
Category 4 - IDEs (pick one, specialize):
Options shown:
- Replit (+ Replit Agent)
- Claude Code
- Docker MCP
Why Replit included: "Used to use Replit before there was Replit Agent. Known for allowing you to deploy code on cloud really easily without getting wires crossed in AWS, Azure, or GCP."
Advantage: "Now you can use Replit Agent, it's pretty good, has native database, more secure than average vibe coding tool like Lovable."
"You can also use it without Agent at all, just use it to deploy and edit code."
Claude Code: "That's my baby."
Docker MCP (the overlooked gem):
Problem: Client overwhelmed by MCP. "What are these words you're using? Claude Code? You want me to jump into terminal? I'm not technical. What are you crazy?"
Then: "Let's introduce you to MCPs." Client: "No, I don't want to deal with JSON. Don't want to copy paste anything anywhere."
Solution: Show Docker MCP toolkit
"Now you can just go to catalog (they keep adding to it). People don't talk about this. They add Stripe, Apify, Perplexity. A lot of magic here people take for granted."
One-click installation: "Click on clients. If you have Claude Desktop, one click syncs all MCP tools. With Cursor, one click syncs all MCP tools."
"Amazing to be able to show cheat codes for people."
Security option: "Even if it's company that cares about security and wants to host private models on LM Studio, you can also hook up some of these MCPs to LM Studio."
Value of knowing this one thing: "Could stop a bunch of discovery calls having to MacGyver and Frankenstein some patchwork solution."
The trash vs treasure framework:
Not just guiding people on right path—also tell them what's trash.
Google Experimental Studio breakdown:
"Don't just take every app in Google Experimental Studio and assume it's great. Some are actual gems."
Trash list shown:
- Opal (automation platform): "Trash"
- Firebase Studio: "Trash on fire"
- Jewels: "Nobody on earth uses it"
- Stacks: "I don't even know what this is"
Why this matters: "You might tell them 'this is great' and they make assumption, okay, everything in this ecosystem must be great. No. That's why you're there."
The vibe coding landscape shift:
Observation: "Google Labs experimental stuff—now you can technically vibe code without Loveables and Bolt.news of the world."
"I'm barely going to be covering those moving forward because their moat of making vibe coding easy is eroding."
Why? "Frontier model providers are going to offer service themselves. Then these platforms, outside of willy-nilly integrations like Shopify with Loveable (which is cool), will be taken over as well. Those will erode over time."
The super app principle:
"If super app is taking over dedicated app, and you want everything to live ideally in as lean a stack as possible, then you want to take this thinking and convey that to whoever you're consulting for or architecting solutions for."
Feel free to plagiarize that rationale.
Voice AI ecosystem (stable category):
Unlike vibe coding tools, voice has crystallized:
Stable providers:
- Vapi
- Retell
- 11Labs
- OpenAI real-time API for real-time voice
- Pipecat (if willing to get in trenches for very customizable systems)
- LiveKit and LiveKit Cloud
"Very crystallized environment where enhancements happening at voice level, at latency level, at multi-turn level. But not like ever-changing names every single day like you do with vibe coding tools."
Everything apps category:
Disclaimer: "Most everything apps are wrappers on top of Claude hooked up to 30-50 tools, pumped with ton of capacity and looping."
Manus example: "People still love Manus. I used to love Manus back in day. Now I just use my Claude Code as my Manus since it's technically same thing, just easier to run in browser."
Why everything apps can work: "They're willing to be loss leader on compute. You get more juice for squeeze."
When to recommend: "If they want to dip toes, want gateway drug to learn AI and automation and change, everything apps could be a recommendation."
Relevance AI warning:
"Long-time hater of this because I used it for a while. Did a couple client deliveries, then things broke overnight. Their entire stack broke overnight."
Current state: "They've improved very much since then. Very cute app. Like watching Pokemon or playing Pokemon with AI agents."
Verdict: "For production scale stuff, I wouldn't stand behind this. Anything you can do in Relevance, you can now do in Claude Code. Is it harder? Yes. More painful? Yes. Gonna be more scalable and you're not dependent and owned by whole platform and your agent infrastructure is yours? Also yes."
The 2026 simplification strategy:
Key principle: "One useful thing is trying to simplify everyone's stack."
Example conversation:
Client: "I love Gamma"
You: "Gamma's cool. I like Gamma. Gamma has cool API now. But why Gamma? What do you get out of Gamma specifically?"
Client: "I love the way it can do deep research then also supplement that research and create slides out of it."
You: "Did you know that in Claude AI, if you go to Settings → Capabilities → toggle this, this will let you create pretty solid PowerPoint-style slides as well?"
Result: "They might be like, oh, we're paying X amount per month and we have five licenses. We might be able to simplify our stack and our finances at the same time. That's an example of value for you."
The OG comeback: Machine Learning + Google Colab:
Why this matters: "Some companies might be eligible where they want to do machine learning, or you need to educate them that machine learning, this whole stats world, is also a thing they could do related to AI, especially if there's number crunching, prediction, analysis, anything related to forecasting."
Google Colab introduced: "Equivalent of Google Doc in cloud that lets you run code. But now they have equipped and hooked up Gemini to it. This would have saved my life in university."
Live demonstration shown:
Step 1: "Generate a 100-row dataset about people surviving apocalypse that would be good for machine learning"
Step 2: Gemini plops in code, creates fake dataset with age, gender, fitness level, etc.
Step 3: "Could you make a machine learning model that would help me predict who would survive? And can you create the evaluation so I can see how accurate this machine learning model is?"
Timeline shock: "This used to take me weeks to just manually code myself. But now we live in a world where you can tinker."
What's happening behind the scenes:
Uses sklearn library (very common in ML)
Splits dataset into train and validation
Can iterate and fix its own mistakes automatically
Example error it fixes: "Could not convert string to float"
Explanation: "Machine learning models deal in ones and zeros. You can't just use string value like male or female. Now it's running it, fixing it, running predictions on who would survive and who won't survive."
Historical context: "This would usually take a data analyst two and a half years ago a week to put together. You'd need whole team of data scientists. I worked on team of 10 data scientists for a company all working on ML models. You don't need 10 anymore."
Model evaluation shown: Accuracy metrics displayed, but expert eye catches problem
"This model sucks because if you have perfect accuracy and precision, that means there's something wrong. Your model's overfitting."
The point: "Even with the OGs, the OGs are now enhanced with outside of Claude Code and Cursor."
Google Colab advantages:
- Use hefty hardware in cloud pretty easily
- Integrates with GitHub seamlessly
- Colab Pro: Use TPUs, GPUs, multiple GPUs for fraction of price you'd use elsewhere
Real differentiator:
"This is what will differentiate you from a lot of folks in other places, other communities looking at 'this is how you build the automation.'"
"You might build 4-5 automations where half of those could have been Claude Code or one of those was actually a feature in Gemini you could have used to your advantage and not done this to begin with."
"This is where the value lies."
The expert riffing ability:
"Being able to explain and riff the way I'm riffing right now without a script in front of me is where you become more expert level and people really come to you for your two cents on things."
The summary formula:
"Goal is you are wealth of information. You are fountain where people can go to and you know ideally a little bit about everything so you have that in your back pocket."
"But you do know a lot at least about:
- One chat platform
- One language model
- One IDE
- One automation system"
"Do you understand A to Z of that ecosystem? It's up to you what that ecosystem is. You might bias one side vs the other."
The final positioning:
Amateur consultant: "Here's how to build this automation in n8n"
Expert consultant: "Wait, do you really need an automation? There's actually a Gemini feature that does exactly this. Let me show you. And if you do need automation, here's why N8N is better than Make for your specific use case, and here's the n8n ecosystem depth that'll serve you for 3 years."
That's the difference.
Next episode: The consulting tech stack audit—cutting client costs by 40% while looking like a genius.

lessons.title: 13) Mastering Inbound Strategies
lessons.description:
Attracting Clients Without Cold OutreachI've started 43 businesses. Never done a single cold call, cold email, or cold DM for my own company.
26 minutes on the inbound playbook that makes clients chase you: Reddit arbitrage, Skool community infiltration, LinkedIn value bombs, free workshops that convert at 40-60%, and why you have 6-12 months left to exploit the naivety gap.
What you'll watch:
- Why cold everything is dying (and what replaces it)
- The Reddit strategy that generated 10-15 leads/day for voice agents
- Skool community infiltration for non-AI industries
- LinkedIn without AI slop (and why rocket emojis kill your credibility)
- Free-to-paid conversion framework (office hours → proposals)
- AI-generated lead magnets in 15 minutes
- The naivety arbitrage window (closing fast)
The uncomfortable confession:
I am very shy, very introverted. I already feel like I'm burdening people just by speaking to them, let alone asking them to buy something from me.
I specialize in making people come to me because I'm more than happy to sell someone if they already are pre-sold themselves on potentially working with me. Very easy sale.
The yin-yang partnership ☯️
I handle inbound. I give free value and attract clients organically.
Taha handles outbound. Cold calling, cold emailing—he's exceptional at it.
I even make Taha ask the burdening annoying questions when we're traveling together. If we go to a hotel and I have an annoying request, I will make Taha ask it because he is more than fine doing that. I absolutely hate doing that.
Why this matters for you:
A lot of people try to force a way of solutioning or getting leads or client acquisition that is contrary to their personality.
If you're shy: Cold calling/DMing/emailing can force you out of your shell (can work)
But: We're moving into a world where doing cold everything will be that much more scalable. Millions of people doing cold to tons of people who are always receiving cold. You get numb to it.
The novelty of AI-generated outreach will expire.
Strategy 1 - Reddit (The Underrated Goldmine):
The logic: If language models are primarily trained using Reddit as information for how humans behave, then why not go to where they live and where people are complaining about what they're struggling with?
Your avatar of whoever you're trying to sell to or consult for is complaining about their problem somewhere.
Real case study:
Someone I knew got 10-15 leads per day through Reddit for voice agents.
How: Joined r/VoiceAgents and r/AISolutions forums, answered questions without selling, just solved problems.
Result: DMs full from Reddit where everyone has fake usernames.
I didn't believe him at first. I thought he was BSing me. We jumped on Discord video chat. I was shocked to see his DMs were actually full from Reddit.
The principle: The more you are helpful to other people, the more you're recognized, the more people are familiar with your name, the more likely they are willing to reach out proactively.
The tool recommendation: Gummy Search
What it does:
- Search through growing Reddit communities
- Add them to your audience
- Ask questions in natural language
- See which forums/threads discuss specific issues
- Track keywords like AI voice agents or anyone know good AI consultants
- Sentiment analysis
Really good way to monitor discourse of what people are struggling with. Even if you're at zero on LinkedIn followers, you can start making posts that are very tacit knowledge addressing pain points you're hearing through this ether.
The knowledge arbitrage:
Being part of this community and watching all YouTube videos you consume gives you very biased perspective. You might assume everybody knows what an MCP is. You might assume everyone knows about connectors in Claude.
Reality: Most people are still asking what's the best way to scrape YouTube transcripts. Even though there's now multiple ways to do this in one click and multiple MCPs—literally called YouTube Transcripts MCP. One click, it's in Cursor, good to go.
The Reddit roadmap:
Spend 5-7 hours per week for a couple months:
- Posting
- Answering questions (like I do in this community)
- Spying on discourse
- Offering AMAs or free workshops
Strategy 2 - Skool Communities (Non-AI Industries):
The insight: Very similar to Reddit strategy. You can go into Skool communities. Join a flurry of not necessarily AI communities.
Why non-AI communities: Join lower paid communities for industries you care about.
Example: Accounting
Scenario: Join paid accounting group (in addition to this one, don't cancel this one)
Be fly on wall, hear what people ask about.
Typical question: Does anyone know how to automate this annoying thing in Excel? We do DCF valuations and I hate using VBA to do XYZ.
Your golden response: Did you know there's actually this thing called Claude for Financial Services that just came out a few weeks ago? Really cool Excel add-in that you can bring in, blah blah blah XYZ.
Result: Wow, I had no idea, that's good to know.
The value recycling strategy:
You can literally take the sauce that I provide all of you, repackage that sauce, take it elsewhere, look like a complete genius.
You are a genius already, but supplement that genius with the drops I give you. Keep delivering value there.
The law of large numbers principle:
If you give so much one-way traffic and you ask for zero in return, people—good people who want to work with you, typically people willing to take out credit card to compensate you for your time—are more likely to reach out to you.
If you offer these nuggets for free with no expectation of getting something in return.
Mind blowing. 🤯
The tactical approach:
Join 2-3 AI automation communities that have hiring boards (free communities work best)
Why free over paid: People that join paid want to solve their own problems, scratch their own itch. You have more chance to stand out if it's not in AI-oriented community where everyone is somewhat aware of what's happening.
If you go to pockets of the world where people gathering around certain topic, industry, tool—that's where you can really shine and use the arbitrage of all the knowledge we try to disseminate here.
The community downsell strategy:
Your offer tiers:
1. Top tier: AI workshop
2. Mid tier: Hourly consulting or bulk hours
3. Downsell: $20/month community
Pitch: If you don't want to pay $200 for one hour of my time, I do have a community for only $19.99 per month. I answer questions every single day for people that either find $200 per hour too expensive, or you don't know whether I'll give you what you're looking for, and you want a taste of the firepower and knowledge I have.
You can literally cancel at any time. Amazing downsell.
Coming soon: We have another community for enterprise clients where I'll be less involved. Even structuring that is getting my creative juices flowing on how you could replicate the same thing at smaller and more niche level.
Strategy 3 - LinkedIn (Without The AI Slop):
The problem: People use and abuse LinkedIn the wrong way. I hate the way people use LinkedIn. It's very parasitic. The AI slop is very apparent.
The telltale signs:
- Rocket emojis 🚀
- Emoji on every single bullet point
- Em-dashes everywhere
If you're watching this and you do that, please tell your ChatGPT to not put em-dashes or emojis on every single line. It looks very jarring. People now are recognizing this is AI-generated content.
Why dilute your brand, dilute your offering by having these little sloppy lines that don't make you look as white glove and polished as you should?
The correct LinkedIn strategy:
Step 1: Answer questions on viral posts
Go in on viral posts where people are asking questions, where the author doesn't have bandwidth to answer said question. Answer the questions as detailed as possible.
The arbitrage: Every single time someone likes your comment, it shows up on their connections feeds. The more genuine sauce you add...
What NOT to do: Don't take the whole thing of the thread and post and say can you please go answer that question in a very thoughtful way and write like a human or else I will send you to Mars.
What to do: Answer it yourself, make it look superhuman (lowercase). If you refuse to type it out or dictate it the way I dictate most responses in community, then at least make it look very human.
Step 2: Recycle your answers
You'll be able to recycle a lot of your answers to questions between Reddit, between Skool, between LinkedIn. People have same problems, just venting about it on different platforms and through different modalities.
Step 3: Build connections and offer free webinars
After providing series of sauce:
- Drop your own version of my Loom Bites
- Walk through how X solution is useful for Y avatar
- Offer useful n8n workflows or things that actually work
The LinkedIn template trap:
Bad strategy: Comment Donkey Kong and I will send you this horrific template that I downloaded from n8n template library. I have no idea how to set it up.
Why it works short-term: Because people are naive on LinkedIn, they will comment Donkey Kong and you'll get more visibility. You'll see hundreds of comments and likes.
Why it kills you long-term: Those people build a bad reputation. One, they didn't build it themselves, meaning they have no idea how to set it up if people ask questions. Two, if they don't know how to use n8n and act like they do, once they get on discovery call, people can smell when you're full of it.
The right approach: If you actually share things you've built, you always have that tacit knowledge, that been there done that. You'll be surprised how basic of a thing that's actually useful, that actually works, that people will value.
Examples of things to share:
- Really cool hack using custom GPT
- Custom action for custom GPT that hooks up to n8n workflow
- Two basic webhooks and AI agent in couple different tools
There's so much value you can extract by just giving that value upfront. Do it on recurring basis with non-AI-generated slop statuses and descriptions. Write it yourself, it doesn't take that much time to be thoughtful.
The LinkedIn-to-YouTube path:
I know some creators 10 on LinkedIn that are now beginning YouTube because they built decent audience of 20K-30K people on LinkedIn.
Why it's easier: Easier for someone to click follow on LinkedIn than subscribe on YouTube. It's even harder for you to be discovered on YouTube.
Strategy 4 - Free Workshops & Office Hours:
The conversion machine:
Offer weekly office hours
Record the sessions
Post recordings back on LinkedIn as content
This becomes foundational flow
The wheel: You offer value → You record you offering value in real time → You offer value through the artifact or sawdust of creating that recording to begin with.
Result: You propel a lot of content and build a portfolio of content of you giving that goodwill.
When someone asks for case studies: Now you have an army of different recordings that you can link them in two seconds. Makes you look that much more polished, that much more genuine, and more importantly, in this increasingly HeyGen AI avatar environment, more human.
The structure:
Time commitment: 3-5 hours per week office hours
Conversion rate: 40-60% of people on calls will be interested in working with you
Why it works: If you give something away for free in a world where everyone wants to put something behind a gate (even though they know the avatar can get it for free if they look hard enough), this builds a trustless society.
If you can be a trustworthy person in a trustless society, you will inevitably—whether English is your first language, whether you're doing this in different language—you will build a loyal following.
Size doesn't matter: Whether it's 10 people, whether it's 100 people, whether it's 50,000 plus people. That will depend on quality and your ability to distill very difficult ideas that are hard to articulate, and how valuable you package it.
Strategy 5 - AI-Generated Lead Magnets (15 Minutes):
Real example: Divorce law lead magnet
Built in 15-20 minutes using Gemini AI Studio (free)
What it does: When you walk through your scenario of what's happening with your divorce and answer a few things in natural language, I just used Gemini 2.5 Flash to process whole thing, then come back with legal response or blurb you can use in your contract to help protect you from being taken advantage of in XYZ way.
Result: Very simple lead magnet, pretty effective. Can look polished very quickly.
The skill myth: It's not like oh Mark you're so smart you did it in 15-20 minutes. I literally just asked Gemini 2.5 Pro on AI Studio for free to build me this thing. I was able to share it and give it to this lawyer, and now they can use it, repurpose it, whatever.
The text-to-workflow opportunity:
Now that we have things like text-to-workflow in n8n, why not take that to your advantage?
Action plan: Build 10-15 workflows using it. Set it up, make sure it works. Pin the data so you can demonstrate it works on whatever platform you want to use.
Share on: Skool communities, LinkedIn, your platform of choice, warm email to people you actually know
The asymmetry principle:
Perceived value is here (high). Time it took you to actually build it was here (low). Really good asymmetry.
The big picture most miss:
People are obsessed about using vibe coding to build SaaS apps that will only die tomorrow and won't make a dollar. I'm telling you that very bluntly.
Real opportunities: Now that we have this arbitrage of being able to create valuable stuff at scale while most people have no idea you can do this—this is where you have ability to create so many valuable lead magnets.
The urgency: While we still have this opportunity of demonstrating goodwill, take advantage of it. Please. (voice cracks) I'm passionate about it because people are missing the big picture.
The SaaS reality check: Everyone's obsessed about building this tool they think someone's gonna pay $5.99 per month for, $10.99 per month for. Newsflash: Most SaaS tools will die. Most B2B SaaS tools will die.
So while we still have this golden arbitrage, take advantage of it.
Strategy 6 - Open Source Everything:
The Claude Code advantage:
We have ability to now use Claude Code to build useful frameworks.
Example: Build something to audit a vibe coding tool to say how you should improve it to make it look better. You could vibe code a tool to allow that and offer that as GitHub repo.
The GitHub simplification: Now you don't even have to figure out how to use GitHub in depth. Just do Install App in Claude Code, use that. Push that code and have it write a README that's really well put together that would help complete beginner use your repo.
Why it works:
As long as they're actually thoughtfully built and they work and you put effort to set it up: One, that's an artifact and portfolio point for you. Two, we have that arbitrage of naivety where people don't know that you were able to build whole framework or whole app or whole Chrome extension in 5-6 hours.
Get it out there and offer it complete for free. People will look at you like you're a saint, especially those that don't know this is achievable now with current modern technology.
Project ideas:
- Ultimate n8n template library for different industries
- Templates for different avatars
- Different AI tools
- Custom GPTs and Claude projects
So many things you could do.
The arbitrage window (critical):
Timeline: 6-12 months left where you can take advantage of these arbitrage opportunities of naivety.
What to do: Build something super valuable and you attract people to come to you to bang your door to open it so you can say yes to them giving you money without you going and saying let me convince you why you should give me money.
I like the inverse scenario because: One, I sleep better at night, I feel better. Two, if someone doesn't want to work with me, it's all good. I didn't exert any effort outside of putting value that I can then repurpose for all kinds of different situations and organizations.
The origin story myth:
When people tell me oh lucky you Mark you can do inbound, you're lucky you're in a privileged position—yes, sure. But I only got here because I didn't have an audience for my first 1,000 hours of consulting.
I had just Fiverr. And I ranked on Fiverr because I also did a lot of things for free on Fiverr for like $10 per month, for $10 per order. So I paid all my dues in very low ticket things given my skillset.
But every person has their path.
The Fiverr hack (before they cracked down):
Put free 15-minute Calendly link at bottom of every Fiverr job post
Result: On average, 3-7 free 15-minute discovery calls per day.
Outcome: How quickly do you think I was able to aggregate a lot of understanding of how different companies are looking for AI, what pain points people are dealing with?
The reciprocity trigger: Turn those 15-minute calls naturally into me giving such good advice that they feel like why did you do this for free? Now they feel this need for reciprocity.
Result: At least a portion of folks would want to work with me and ask me to build things for them.
The risk-free value exchange: One of easiest ways to do this value exchange in risk-free way in very noisy environment.
The final truth about inbound:
Some people with 300 YouTube subscribers have CEOs watching their stuff. Because some people don't care about vanity metric of how many subscribers you have. They care about the sauce.
They actually look for people with less following and believe them more. Because they're less inclined to want to over-hype and BS. Since they don't have this propelling propaganda machine trying to get more people into their ecosystem.
They have nothing to lose and kind of nothing to gain because they're so small. A lot of people trust smaller creators on all kinds of social media platforms.
Even if you have zero voice, you can still have a lot of opportunity.
The cold truth about cold outreach:
It can work. Taha proves it. But we're moving to a world where millions of people doing cold to tons of people who are always receiving cold. You get numb to it.
If I get like 4-5 AI automated calls, eventually I'm just gonna stop responding or not give you the time of day.
Choose your path based on your personality, not what some guru tells you everyone must do.
Next episode: The proposal framework that closes 60% plus of qualified leads without pressure tactics.

lessons.title: 14) Mastering Engaging Workshops
lessons.description:
Strategies for Effective Teaching ✨I made this second last module a special one. The secrets to how I teach any audience.
Teaching is acquired through reps, feedback, and stimuli. Not talent.
37 minutes on the workshop structure that captures attention from minute one: the airplane analogy framework, using silence as a weapon, why you need 50 slides for a 30-slide presentation, and how introverts survive radio-silent audiences.
What you'll watch:
- The 5-part framework: Sell the dream → Show the path → Announce the flight plan → In-flight service → Land the plane
- Why 80% of your audience is unaware but the hook determines if they stay
- The Machiavellian silence technique for forcing participation
- Over-preparing content for cricket audiences
- Industry-specific examples as client magnets
- Slide density rules and visual structure
The core truth about teaching:
Teaching is a skill like everything else. It's acquired through reps and feedback and stimuli.
Most of the times, the majority of the audience you'll be speaking to is unaware of 80% of what you're gonna teach. But if you don't capture them in the hook, you lose them.
The hook window: First 30 seconds to a minute. That's when you grab attention or give the plans so they understand what's in it for them if they stick around.
The unpredictability problem: On YouTube people know the video is 8 or 38 minutes. But a presentation is unpredictable. The likelihood someone loses interest is very high.
The 5-part framework:
Part 1 - Sell the outcome (sell the dream):
Not in context of sales. Paint the picture: If you listen to me, even if you don't like the way I look or the way I speak or both, this is what's in it for you. This is what you'll be able to hopefully do after I'm done speaking.
Part 2 - Walk through the path:
People need a map of where you'll be going, how you'll be transitioning them through novice to intermediate or advanced—whatever the scope of the workshop is.
Be flamboyant and brash: Listen, I'm not gonna show you something you can just watch on YouTube. I've tried to distill all XYZ knowledge in the easiest way that you specifically will benefit from.
Acknowledge their assumptions: I know all of you are probably thinking about these main pain points or this is the way you think you should use AI. But this is probably where you have to use AI and why some of those don't actually matter.
Clarify and quantify: Step by step why this is the case.
Part 3 - Announce the flight plan (the airplane analogy):
Like a pilot announces: Today we're flying at 50,000 foot views, negative 23 degrees outside (you don't hear the muffled sound but in this case they should hear the specific agenda).
We're going to move the next 10 minutes. Then we're gonna go in flight. Then we'll have in-flight service. Then there'll probably be turbulence one or two hours in.
Why this works: Conditions you as a passenger to not be shocked. If you suddenly feel turbulence (analogous to teaching), if you're going through a very rough part where you know nine times out of 10 the average person would lose interest, you want to bring that up front.
Example warning: I know you all hate coding or might not enjoy seeing code on screen. But around one hour in, I'm going to quickly but surgically and precisely walk through a technical part that if you don't understand, the rest of this is useless. But depend on me. I'm going to make this as digestible as I possibly can.
Acknowledging everything upfront: The passenger on the flight is less likely to be shocked or surprised.
The disclaimers (health and safety demonstration):
Walk through assumptions: Listen, I'm going to assume you've used ChatGPT at least once before. If not, you can ask me a clarifying question throughout, but my base here is I'm assuming you've written some prompt.
You've automated writing an email even if that email was kind of trash. You've at least tried to create an image, whether that's a Ghibli image or an image of yourself holding a unicorn. You've at least tried XYZ feature.
Apply this to whatever you're trying to teach.
Part 4 - In-flight service (retention through golden nuggets):
On YouTube, retention is the game of games. You have the hook that gets someone to click, but then retention is a series of golden nuggets you plant throughout the presentation to keep people's interest, attention, and wanting more.
The show-first strategy:
Common thing I do on YouTube: Show the end result first in the first two minutes.
Hook → This is what I'm trying to do. I'm going to show you how we can do X with Y.
Now before I even walk through how to do X and Y, let me walk through the end solution. Walk through the final outcome.
If you click on this little thing in n8n and you can click on execute workflow, watch what happens. Boom boom boom boom boom.
As the viewer, you get satisfaction that okay clearly this thing works.
Then: Great, now that I've shown you this works, now I'm going to show you how this works step by step.
Result: I've already created some level of retention. I've dropped a little golden nugget and I'm validating that it's worth your time being here.
The retention drop reality:
Four to five minutes in: Look at any retention graph, even if you're not a YouTuber. Go on Google Images and put on YouTuber Retention Graphs.
You will see dwindling after minute one, two, three, even on the most famous videos. Videos with millions of views will have retention graphs if you're lucky between four to five minutes.
The solution: Keep giving nuggets. Plan out when you'll have your cool nuggets.
Example nuggets: Did you know you could actually do this in ChatGPT, or you could do this in Claude? If you connect your connector and it does this to Claude AI, you can actually accomplish ABC.
Drop those every couple minutes. That's what keeps people's retention.
The 80/20 attention rule:
When you have parts where you're explaining, 20% of people are actually listening to you. 80% are staring at your mouth or thinking about what they want to eat tomorrow.
That's where you want to really recapture the audience over and over again.
The blunt check-in:
Depending on how comfortable you are with teaching, be blunt: Listen, am I just moving my mouth or is this actually helpful for people? Give me a silent nod if this is helpful, just so I know there's a pulse on the room.
When you say stuff like that, you'll get some wry smiles.
The hardest scenario: Teaching workshops to cameras off, 150 people with like three people on cameras, and me saying any questions with like a long 30 second silence. Those are the hardest ways to manage a workshop.
The independence principle: Sometimes it's going to be out of your hands. You have to learn the skill of not requiring external validation for every single thing you do.
When I do get participation, when people are nodding and smiling and asking questions, that just makes me a better teacher because I feed off of that energy. But the trick is you don't make your energy dependent on the audience you're teaching or speaking to.
Part 5 - Land the plane (the conclusion):
Just as important as you begin and you're very explanatory and explicit in where we're gonna go, how we're gonna get there, and what are we gonna use to get there—you also wanna conclude recapping what is it that you learned, and how they could apply this, and what could happen next, and that you invite questions.
This process: Pretty straightforward. If you master this, then all kinds of lectures and workshops will become easier.
The deep dive framework:
Pre-workshop design:
Know your avatar:
- Who are they?
- What are their role and daily responsibilities?
- Are they an analyst? Are they C-suite?
Those are two very different audiences. C-suite like to be focused on ROI and very high level. If you're going through n8n, you would less likely explain what a curl request is to C-suite versus an analyst, because an analyst is meant to go into the weeds. C-suite is basically trying to design what the forest looks like.
The key question: Do they want to be here or were they told to be here?
Also very important. If a company's mandating that their employees upskill themselves and they're all having to be here instead of doing something else or they're cancelling meetings or pausing their work—you're already going to have an audience that's looking at you with a blank stare, mad at your existence.
What you can do: At the base case, all you can do is outperform their expectations.
If they're told to be there, there's going to be higher resistance to them wanting to listen to you. I'll probably show more magic tricks in that workshop than the one where people want to be there.
People that want to be there probably want to learn first principles. They want to learn foundations. You want to be able to double click on that.
Address fears directly: People are there and they're worried, potentially subconsciously, of losing their job. If they don't learn this stuff, you want to acknowledge the fear.
Say things like: Someone using AI is going to replace you (even though that's kind of cope in my opinion for a lot of things). You might want to say that or acknowledge it.
Or if it's true: Let's say AI can do 80% of what they do but they're needed in the last 20% or the last 8%. A human's still needed there.
What's in it for them: Listen, I know things sound scary. AI's kind of overhyped a lot of things. So maybe acknowledge things that make them feel better because AI is overhyped. Generative AI is not the solution to all problems. It's the solution to a lot of problems, not all problems.
Content planning - The over-preparation rule:
Design 100% of the needed content.
This is something I learned a long time ago when I used to teach lectures at university, when I used to teach for my public speaking course, anytime of being a teaching assistant.
The burn scenario: There's a few times I've been burnt where I've gone from having classes—and I've had thousands of students now—I've had classes where people are talkative or they're at least medium level talkative. So time flies, and the same content that got me through class one, two, and three, I expect to get me through class four.
But once in a while: You'll encounter an audience, a class, or people around the table that are radio silent. They give you no stimuli, no feedback, or validation that you're doing a good job.
The insurance policy: If you think it's going to take you an hour to deliver 30 slides, I make 50 slides. I like to have the insurance policy that if I'm getting zero back in terms of participation, even if I feel like I'm doing a good job, I want to make sure I can persist until the end.
