lessons.title: AMA on July 20
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: w723tCt*
📝 Meeting Summary:⚙️ Vapi Custom Logic and Local Development Tips
[01:15:54–01:21:56]
The discussion detailed how to locally test Vapi logic, use ngrok for tunneling, and debug agent behavior. Suggestions included using single-turn prompts and terminal logs to troubleshoot agents before scaling to more complex tasks.
🧭 Handling Complex Conditions with Fallback Flows
[01:22:09–01:26:28]
Fallback flows were recommended for edge cases where agents may fail. Instead of handling everything in the voice call, participants suggested gracefully routing users to text follow-ups or human agents when logic becomes too complex.
🎤 Single-Turn Simplicity for Better User Experience
[01:26:45–01:30:38]
The power of keeping voice interactions simple was emphasized. Single-turn agents that confirm one task per call—like sending a reset link—were considered highly effective and easier to implement than complex multi-step conversations.
👂 User Feedback as Iteration Fuel
[01:30:40–01:35:58]
Members discussed using real transcripts to refine prompts and system logic. By analyzing user behavior, teams can improve flow, adjust voice assistant personality, and tune agent performance with direct data.
🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Human Hand-off for High-Trust Scenarios
[01:36:03–01:38:22]
Some scenarios—like legal or financial conversations—require empathy or deeper expertise. In these cases, participants agreed AI should collect initial info and pass it to a human for follow-up, rather than risk a poor user experience.

lessons.title: AMA Call on August 2
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: rD4d@9*f
Meeting Summary:
🧑‍💻 Strategic Tooling: From MVPs to Production with AI Agents
[00:04:00–00:10:20]
Mark advised documenting project architecture with Loom walkthroughs and leveraging Gemini/Claude for deep app analysis. He suggested treating AI tools like junior devs—provide detailed context to avoid rework, and use foundational prompts before progressing to more advanced steps. Emphasis was placed on clarity and thoughtful planning for tool stack migration (e.g., Lovable to Claude Code or Cursor).
🗂️ Outcome-Driven Prompt Engineering & Feedback Loops
[00:49:15–00:54:47]
The group discussed building robust “super prompts” for nuanced business processes. Tips included: documenting workflows, running real-world tests, and incrementally refining prompts based on actual results and feedback. Mark detailed his process of prompt “sculpting”—iterating over days, capturing what works and doesn’t, and continually evolving a dynamic master prompt.
⚙️ AI Agent Ecosystem: MCPs, CLIs, and Tool Stack Strategy
[00:25:44–00:41:54]
Conversation compared the strengths of Claude Code, Cursor, and new CLI tools (like 11 Labs). Members shared their approach to minimizing “tool overload” by anchoring on a single command center with modular add-ons (MCPs, agents). There was debate over budget, rate limits, and the value of running parallel platforms for model flexibility (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, etc.).
🎯 Real-World AI Application Wins: Project Management, Construction, Vision
[00:14:00–00:23:04]
Camille shared a case study: automating scope-of-work creation for construction projects by merging video transcripts and blueprints using Claude Code. Advice was given on fine-tuning vision models for specific image analysis, with Mark warning about the cost and complexity of manual training versus leveraging GenAI tools or open-source alternatives.
🛠️ Best-of-Breed Knowledge Bases: Building “Top Hits” for Prompting & Workflows
[00:40:31–00:48:48]
Mark and others explored creating curated markdown files from “top hits” of various creators (e.g., best prompts from Mark, best workflows from Nate). This modular approach enables mixing prompt engineering and workflow strategies for consistent, high-quality output across projects—while also addressing the legal and ethical aspects of sharing public vs. private resources.

lessons.title: AMA Call on August 16
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: #1jQim%u
Meeting Summary:
🤖 Sub-Agents in Claude Code00:05:09 – 00:09:14
Mark explained Claude’s new sub-agents feature, which allows specialized agents (e.g., database engineer, QA tester) to collaborate within one project memory. He shared a “cheat code” repo with pre-written agent prompts and demonstrated how to orchestrate them via cloud.md files, avoiding the need for multiple fragmented chats.
🧩 Dashboard & Memory Management Insights00:13:14 – 00:18:24
Camille described building a command center dashboard for clients, emphasizing Claude Code’s output styles and memory/session management. She warned that poorly structured sub-agents can overwhelm context, stressing the need for careful orchestration and a personal system for dates, sessions, and project organization.
🔄 Make vs N8N Workflow Migration00:22:42 – 00:33:23
Jake asked about migrating automations from Make.com to N8N. Mark advised against self-hosting unless running extremely high volumes due to maintenance issues. He demonstrated a Make-to-N8N conversion approach using JSON exports and MCP servers, noting challenges with community nodes and limited native integrations compared to Make and Zapier.
📚 Lightweight Knowledge Base for Employee Support00:42:07 – 00:52:27
Wioletta asked about structuring an AI assistant for employee engagement. Mark suggested skipping vector databases for small document sets (~20 pages) and instead creating a distilled FAQ cheat sheet. He showed how to chain a qualifying agent with a FAQ responder sub-agent in N8N, using memory only for conversation continuity.
🏗️ Scaling Knowledge & Deployment Options00:54:02 – 01:11:34
For larger document sets, Mark recommended knowledge distillation (summarizing 10k+ pages into a 100-page cheat sheet) before considering vectorization. He also introduced tools like Claudia (Claude Code front-end) and Aura (AI-assisted UI builder). Camille showcased her client dashboard; Mark suggested fixing broken icons, adding the 21st Dev MCP server for design polish, and deploying safely on Vercel via GitHub.

lessons.title: AMA Call on August 30
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: dCVnH*7c
Meeting Summary:
🎓 AI Tutoring Concept Feasibility00:07:32 – 00:16:34
Andres asked if an AI tutor could dynamically assess a student’s math level and build a personalized curriculum. Mark explained it’s possible but advised a hybrid approach using surveys for onboarding and RAG to ground the AI in official grade-level documents for reliability.
⚡ N8N Text-to-Workflow Rollout Insights00:17:13 – 00:19:22
Mark shared that N8N’s text-to-workflow feature is top priority but may take 3–4 weeks to roll out fully, with uncertainty about costs and credit limits. He noted access is random, not tier-based, though some users luck into it by opening new accounts.
📚 Comparing Hormozi’s RAG Model00:20:02 – 00:23:38
Arnold asked how Mark’s rebuild compared to Hormozi’s chatbot. Mark said Hormozi’s version is more sophisticated with private workshop data, advanced RAG methods, OCR for images, and re-ranking, whereas his quick build uses Pinecone Assistant for speed and simplicity.
🏢 Agency Strategy & Client Focus00:26:06 – 00:32:44
Justin described projects in forensic accounting, real estate, and restaurants, but Mark advised against restaurants due to razor-thin margins. Instead, he recommended focusing on high-value verticals like forensic accounting and positioning AI to add revenue during off-hours rather than replacing staff.
🚀 Building Production-Grade AI Systems00:55:31 – 01:00:51
Abiola asked about making AI workflows production-grade on AWS and moving away from N8N. Mark confirmed AWS services like DynamoDB and Cognito are secure but stressed the importance of testing for concurrency, latency, and misconfigurations, plus considering custom Python builds for long-term robustness.

lessons.title: AMA Call on Sept. 13
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: Zs3^j*0A
Meeting Summary:
🧩 Prompt Management & Model Drift00:07:08 – 00:12:52
Ian showcased his CRM-content generator app and asked whether prompts should be hardcoded or stored in Supabase. Mark advised building a prompt management table with metadata, since model drift or degradation often causes failures, not just the prompts themselves.
⚙️ Cloud Code vs. Vibe Coding Tools00:13:40 – 00:15:33
Mark recommended moving projects from Lovable/Bolt into Cloud Code or an IDE for stability, as vibe coding tools introduce bias and spaghetti code. He stressed Cloud Code allows reverts, cleaner builds, and scalable long-term production compared to no-code prototypes.
🛡️ Scope Creep & Client Boundaries00:34:16 – 00:43:04
Roko raised concerns about a Dubai client adding endless new requests, delaying MVP delivery. Mark emphasized setting strict scope boundaries, showing clients the cost of constant changes, and walking away if respect for agreements is lacking.
🛠️ MCP Optimization & Community Tools00:43:10 – 00:48:13
Ady introduced tools to dynamically load MCP servers only when needed, reducing wasted tokens, and a session manager to preserve context efficiently. Mark praised these as critical for transparency, noting his own move toward building MCPs in-house to control inefficiency.
🌐 Data Prep & Scaling Considerations00:51:04 – 01:04:35
Tanya asked how companies should prepare data for AI, and Mark outlined thresholds: under 100K rows manageable locally, millions requiring databases, and 20M+ needing Snowflake or AWS pipelines. He explained SQL vs. NoSQL structures, stressing schema design and data hygiene before layering AI.

lessons.title: AMA Call on Sept. 27
lessons.description:
Link to Recording: Zoom Link
Passcode: Q9g#UP1&
Meeting Summary:
🏠 Voice Agents for Retirement Communities00:05:18 – 00:08:54
Vicky Carne shared her pilot project in UK retirement communities, where an AI assistant provides residents with appliance info, event schedules, and safety policies. Mark compared it to North American use cases where AI agents offer resident updates to family, noting cost pressures make automation valuable in elder care.
🧑‍💻 Project Management with Claude Code & Cursor00:09:05 – 00:15:11
Ian Garlic discussed struggles managing his AI-driven app without a human PM, often losing track after breaks. Mark recommended using Cursor in ask mode as a “virgin eyes” co-pilot, generating artifacts like Markdown progress logs and Jira boards to maintain continuity across sessions.
🗂️ Supabase, MCP & Database Choices00:18:20 – 00:20:49
Mark explained three approaches to modifying Supabase: using MCP, local CLI, or SQL queries, warning MCP is better for early prototyping but risky in production. He stressed balancing Postgres vs. NoSQL, suggesting MongoDB for dynamic JSON payloads that evolve over time.
📊 Tool Selection Framework & Workflow Planning00:22:51 – 00:29:24
Rohit Anand presented a decision framework for choosing MCP, n8n, or direct APIs, with Mark advising tool choice depends on authentication needs and project clarity. He stressed spending 30–40 minutes in Claude Code on planning before coding, since detailed simulation drastically reduces dev time.
📚 Prompt Management, Knowledge Bases & Security00:36:16 – 00:56:24
Igor Beylin asked about organizing prompts, and Mark suggested a private GitHub repo with metadata for easy retrieval and version control. On knowledge bases, he recommended Pinecone Assistant over Perplexity for grounded co-pilots, and advised security best practices like using Azure-hosted OpenAI to protect financial data in transit and at rest.

lessons.title: AMA Recording October 12
lessons.description:
Meeting Summary:🎯 Reverse Prompt Strategy (one-shot focus)
[00:00:00–00:08:30]
Design a reverse prompt that has the model act as a prompt engineer, summarizes the current code snapshot to enable a near one-shot rebuild, and avoids prescribing specific versions/tools unless essential. De-prioritize brittle details (e.g., local file paths, 150-word caps). If tokens are tight in Claude, reference the prior chat name in a new chat (with memory) as a fallback.
📚 NotebookLM vs. RAG (plus the Loom embed hack)
[00:08:31–00:13:30]
NotebookLM is essentially a consumer-friendly RAG wrapper that relentlessly grounds the conversation in your uploaded sources (e.g., PDFs, transcripts). Many users load Loom transcripts to get a “smarter Mark.” Quick tip: use browser dev tools to grab the Loom embed link for easy downloading/ingestion—mind security; free tools mean you are the product.
🧪 Testing & Agents (Chrome MCP > Playwright; hire slowly)
[00:13:31–00:22:00]
For UI validation, Chrome’s native MCP server is preferred over Playwright—fewer hallucinations and 3–5× lower token use. Start with zero agents and only “hire” new ones for clear, independent roles (e.g., a dedicated UI-validation agent). More agents ⇒ more coordination overhead and compounded hallucinations.
🔀 Multi-Model Workflow & Human-in-the-Loop
[00:22:01–00:33:30]
Route the same task to Claude / OpenAI / Gemini (and others), then use an LM-as-judge to compare outputs for novelty and reasoning. Models have domain strengths (e.g., Gemini for video/context cues), and personalities change—so don’t over-index on one. Keep humans in control to steer trade-offs and prevent “garbage in, garbage out.”
🔐 Security, HIPAA & Dashboards; Build for Pain
[00:33:31–00:50:00]
For tighter control, use Bedrock (Anthropic), Azure OpenAI, or Vertex; mid-tier options include ChatGPT Teams / Claude for Business. HIPAA compliance is achievable via cloud services + correct data handling; certification can be optional (small paid “stickers” add little). For CEO dashboards, choose Tableau/Power BI/Looker or build custom if you need rapid changes and LLM interactivity—always design around decisions, not charts. Prioritize products that solve real pain; “conversational APIs” or “empathy engines” are nice-to-have until tied to urgent problems.

lessons.title: AMA Recording October 25
lessons.description:
🎥 Balancing High-Level Work & Team Delegation
00:00:00 – 00:05:10
Speaker shares workload reality: enterprise consults are handled personally, while dev execution and editing are delegated. Team supports content, community uploads, and editing — freeing time to stay ahead on AI workflows and YouTube.
⏱️ Time Strategy: No-Meetings Routine & Content Cadence
00:05:11 – 00:11:42
Mark blocks 90% of calendar to stay in deep-work mode, only doing intentional meetings and consults. Plans two YouTube videos per week and five community content drops; prioritizes speed-learning new tools to teach quickly.
🍼 Life Trade-offs & Creative Energy
00:11:43 – 00:17:10
Guest reflects on parenting’s impact on creative time. Mark acknowledges he sacrifices social life, friendships, and family time for a temporary “AI sprint” window — emphasizing opportunity cost and intentional sacrifice.
🎬 Film Editor’s AI Tool Journey & Re-architecture Challenge
00:17:11 – 00:26:30
Retired film editor building AI film-production tool in Claude Code + n8n struggles with hybrid architecture. Mark advises repo audit, pruning code, possibly rebuilding cleaner staging branch, and using plan mode + GitHub diagnostic approach.
🛠️ Refactor Strategy: When to Rebuild vs Push Through
00:26:31 – 00:34:29
Mark recommends snapshotting repo, validating code dependencies, pruning dead files, and using GPT to audit repo health. Suggests codecs for linear execution, avoiding bloated Claude context, and structured refactor checkpoints.

lessons.title: AMA Recording November 09
lessons.description:
📝 Meeting Summary🧠 Optimizing AI workflows (modular logic approach)
00:00:05 – 00:04:30
Roko showcases optimized content-generation workflow using validation loops per question, templated prompts, and simplified nodes; Mark confirms modular design is the correct long-term direction.
⚙️ Agent vs LLM node guidance
00:04:31 – 00:07:30
Mark advises replacing agent nodes with basic LLM nodes for stability and reduced misfires, unless tool-use is required; more deterministic and scalable.
🛠️ Transition to code & handoff strategy
00:07:31 – 00:13:00
Roko plans to hand off workflows to client's dev agency; Mark notes custom-code conversion ideal normally, but simple workflow handoff is fine since agency will rebuild.
💰 Pricing & lessons from big projects
00:13:01 – 00:16:20
Roko underpriced at $4k/month and acted as PM + dev; Mark stresses higher rates, retainer base + per-workflow charges, and avoiding hourly for complex automation.
👩‍💼👨‍💼 Beginner encouragement & adoption mindset
00:16:21 – 00:19:00
New members express learning stage; Mark reassures strategic value in early-stage AI consulting and importance of foundational exposure over technical depth.

lessons.title: AMA Recording November 23
lessons.description:
📝 Meeting Summary🌐 Call Warm-Up & Technical Lag Acknowledgement00:00:00 – 00:03:50
The session opened with casual greetings, light banter, and Mark mentioning he's in Santorini with occasional internet lag, establishing an informal start before shifting into technical discussions.
🧩 AG / Anti-Gravity Tool Issues & Why It's Not Ready00:03:51 – 00:15:40
Participants discussed AG’s unreliability—making schema mistakes, losing context, apologizing mid-session—and Mark emphasized avoiding it for now because early-stage IDE tools always ship with bugs and aren’t stable enough for serious work.
🤖 New Agent Framework for Cloud Code & Single-Task Agent Strategy00:15:41 – 00:23:20
Mark shared a new technique using multiple agents assigned to sub-tasks within one bigger task, drastically improving quality, consistency, and speed, and teased a demo showing how this enabled building a full ML model in minutes.
🧠 AI Performance Mode Discovery & Model Speed Insights00:23:21 – 00:33:00
Holger revealed a finding that certain prompting structures trigger an undocumented “fast mode” across models like Claude and GPT, improving speed by up to 8x, though some versions were temporarily patched by model providers.
🚀 Client Acquisition, Free Audits, Pricing Mindset & First-Wins Strategy00:33:01 – 01:35:00
A large portion of the call focused on Andy’s struggles with pricing and confidence; Mark advised offering free AI audits, building relationships through real client pain discovery, using templates creatively, and prioritizing early wins over high-ticket targets, while Tahar emphasized avoiding premature partnerships and instead building entrepreneurial confidence through direct client work.

lessons.title: AMA Recording December 07
lessons.description:
📝 Meeting Summary:🤖 1. Comparing Open-Source Models vs. Claude Code00:00:00 – 00:12:45
The group discussed open-source coding models like GLM 4.6 and Kimi, noting that while they can be useful for planning or lighter tasks, they often lack efficient reasoning, produce noisy chain-of-thought, and consume excessive context. Claude Code remains preferred due to its speed, stability, and practical usefulness in real development workflows.
🧭 2. Getting Started in AI Consulting & Choosing a Direction00:12:46 – 00:30:15
Guidance was given on when to begin consulting, emphasizing that strong domain knowledge is essential for delivering credible value. Instead of building automations randomly, members were encouraged to specialize in areas close to revenue impact—such as onboarding, lead nurturing, and reactivation—rather than broad lead generation, which is crowded and less differentiated.
🤝 3. How to Approach Partnerships Carefully00:30:16 – 00:52:30
The discussion covered best practices for forming business partnerships, stressing the importance of testing collaboration through small paid projects before formalizing any agreement. Members were advised to avoid 50/50 decision splits, define clear ownership areas, and seek complementary skill sets, ensuring alignment before committing long-term.
🎯 4. Choosing a Niche, Understanding Market Access & Domain Authority00:52:31 – 01:17:10
Members explored how to select an effective niche by evaluating personal expertise, access to decision-makers, and the sector’s ability to pay for solutions. The most reliable path is focusing on one audience, one offer, and one problem—building domain authority by understanding industry language, problems, and workflows deeply.
🏗️ 5. Enterprise Adoption Challenges & Internal Resistance to AI Tools01:17:11 – 01:42:00
The group examined how large organizations resist adopting tools like n8n due to political, cultural, or engineering barriers rather than technical limitations. To drive change, a structured internal education process and top-down leadership support are needed. Demonstrating clear business benefits—speed, reduced workload, and competitive necessity—helps overcome resistance and enables meaningful implementation.

lessons.title: AMA Recording December 21
lessons.description:
📝 Meeting Summary:🏪 1. SMB vs. Enterprise Strategy in Voice & AI Consulting00:00 – 15:00
The discussion emphasized starting with small and mid-sized businesses to reduce risk, learn system failures safely, and build operational confidence before pursuing higher-stakes enterprise contracts where margin pressure, reputational risk, and technical complexity are significantly higher.
🤝 2. Partnership Models, Margin Risk, and Scope Creep15:01 – 35:00
Participants critically examined partnership-driven growth models, highlighting that while partnerships appear attractive on paper, they often compress margins and amplify scope creep, making solo or lightly referral-based client acquisition a safer early-stage strategy.
💰 3. Shifts in Call Center Economics and AI Opportunity35:01 – 55:00
The group explored how traditional per-seat call center pricing is declining in favor of value-based or outcome-based models, creating opportunities for AI-driven voice systems to replace or augment legacy providers through subscription-style offerings.
🧠 4. Tooling Focus: Cloud Code, n8n, and MCP Integration55:01 – 1:25:00
The conversation focused on practical implementation questions around Cloud Code, n8n workflows, MCP servers, and deployment strategies, stressing the importance of simplifying stacks, avoiding unnecessary MCP overhead, and using automation only where it meaningfully reduces feedback loops.
🎓 5. AI Education, Adoption, and Consulting Leverage1:25:01 – End
The session concluded by reinforcing AI education as a high-leverage entry point inside organizations, particularly HR and operations, where prompt engineering, basic automation, and document generation can drive adoption while positioning consultants as trusted internal AI leaders.

lessons.title: 📞 Book Your Call With Me (PLEASE READ)
lessons.description:
⚠️ Read This Before Booking
I started this community to give you fractionalized access to me - and access to coaches that are already paid for as part of your membership.
Before you spend anything extra:
- Post your question in the community. I answer the majority of posts comprehensively.
- Attend the biweekly AMAs. You can ask me anything there - live.
- Reach out to our coaches. That's literally what they're here for.
If you've done all that and you're still adamant that you need dedicated 1-on-1 time with me to get unstuck - then book the call.
But please, exhaust the resources you're already paying for first. That's what they're there for.
Note: The call will be recorded for my reference, but it stays private. It will not be shared with the community.
What This Is 👈🏼👈🏼👈🏼
A private 1-on-1 call with me at 50% off my regular consulting rate. This is exclusive to community members - you won't find this rate anywhere else.
These calls are for guidance, strategy, and direction:
- "Should I use n8n or Make for this?"
- "Here's my AI roadmap - does this make sense?"
- "I'm stuck between two approaches - which one?"
- "How do I position myself as an AI consultant?"
- "Can you help me set up Claude Code / Cursor / my dev environment?"
Basically: big picture thinking, decision-making, getting unstuck.
What This Is NOT ❌❌❌
This is not a tech support call.
If you're coming to me with:
- "Why isn't my workflow running?"
- "I'm getting this error message..."
- "Can you debug my code?"
- "Something broke and I don't know why"
That's not what these calls are for.
That's what our community coaches are for. That's what posting in the community is for.
You'll get faster, better help there - and it won't cost you a call slot.
Before You Book
You'll answer a few questions so I know what we're meeting about.
The clearer you are upfront, the more I can help during our time together.
If you're unsure whether your topic fits, ask in the community first
I'd rather you save your call for something that actually needs 1-on-1 time.
BOOK HERE: Mark's Paid Calendly

lessons.description:
BIO: Eric Ross brings over 8 years of hands-on experience building and scaling AI products for enterprises and major disruptors.
As an AI Product Management expert, he specializes in AI product strategy, go-to-market execution, and helping builders navigate the complex landscape of enterprise AI adoption.
Eric's approach combines deep technical knowledge with strategic business acumen. Having worked as a Principal Technical Product Manager at ODAIA and led AI initiatives at multiple startups, he understands the unique challenges of building AI products that actually ship and scale. His focus is always on practical, actionable strategies that drive real business results.
BOOK A PRIVATE SESSION → Calendly Link
DISCLAIMER: These sessions are for builders actively working on AI products—whether for clients, SaaS platforms, or go-to-market strategies. Please come prepared with specific challenges. All sessions will be recorded and shared with the community so everyone benefits from the insights discussed. 🎯

lessons.title: 1) Understanding Machine Learning
lessons.description:
Concepts and Applications 🤖Machine Learning Mini-Series Day 1: ML 101 + Core Concepts 💎
Going back to my ML roots for a few lectures (so please 'watch', not read this series)
Machine learning gave myself and the team an 'unfair' advantage when ChatGPT launched
50 minutes on why you can't call yourself an AI expert if you only know generative AI: the cost advantage (1000x cheaper), when ML dominates over Gen AI, supervised vs unsupervised vs reinforcement learning, and live examples in Google Colab where 99% of the code was written by AI.
What you'll watch:
- Why traditional ML still dominates (fraud detection, credit scoring, product recommendations)
- The difference between traditional programming and machine learning
- Supervised learning (classification and regression with live examples)
- Unsupervised learning (clustering customer segments)
- Reinforcement learning (the OG agent - maze escape simulation)
- Deep learning preview (neural networks and why it's last resort)
- Overfitting vs underfitting (memorizing vs understanding)
- Training vs test sets (the exam concept)
- Google Colab walkthrough (free GPU access)
The unfair advantage:
My roots are in machine learning. This entire domain is what gave myself and my team an unfair advantage when ChatGPT came out.
When we learned about natural language processing as well as deep learning (which is really the basis for everything that we have today), that gave us a competitive advantage where we could understand the lingo, understand the mechanics, and most importantly understand that generative AI is not pixie dust.
Everything at the end of the day is a statistical probability of some sort.
The goal of this series:
Will I be boring you, going back to my professor roots, pulling out a whiteboard and writing some math and matrices on a board? No.
The goal: Whether you're obsessed with one specific domain in generative AI or whether you want to be able to just know how to be more complete from a machine learning standpoint or just as an AI practitioner—you can't say you're an expert in AI if you just know about Gen AI.
Because Gen AI is the younger sibling of everything else that came before. Like machine learning, deep learning, reinforcement learning. All of those had to be married together to give the offspring we have today.
If you have even an understanding or familiarity with some of the words I'll show you today, the goal is not that you necessarily build machine learning algorithms. Maybe you could. Maybe you would.
But it's more so just being aware that there's this whole other world that nobody cares about anymore on YouTube because everything has to be quick and instant gratification and sexy and generative.
Even though the majority of the apps that you use day to day still use machine learning behind the scenes.
The code reality (don't worry):
Instead of just yapping about this and going through slides, we'll iterate and go through slides as well as actual practical examples.
If I show you all of this code, do you look at this as a non coder and immediately want to tap out of this video? Probably.
What I'm going to tell you is: Don't worry and don't care about the code you're going to see here. Worry about the concepts I'm going to go through.
I just want to be able to show the application of a concept versus just alluding to some word that you'll forget by the end of this video.
99% of this code was by the way written by AI, written by Gemini. I did practically nothing outside of knowing what to tell it to do.
We live in a world now where unlike myself where I had to live on this platform called Stack Overflow and I would have to beg for help on small errors that would take me days to figure out—we don't live in that world anymore.
The hard part is not writing the code for the machine learning algorithm. It's understanding where and when you should and could use it.
Traditional programming vs ML:
Traditional programming: You have some form of data, then you run it through a program or a routine, then you get some output.
Machine learning: You have data and then you look at the output of that data. In between here and here, if we look at this little arrow, you have tons of data cleaning.
Just like in the world of Gen AI, most client data is garbage. Most client database information either has too much information or too little information or information that needs to be distilled.
Machine learning is about focus. You are relying on math to figure out patterns in your data.
If you have 100 columns and 10,000 rows, and of those 100 columns only 10 are relevant and needed, you have to do the pre-work to make sure you put everything on a silver platter.
ML is much more about prep than it is about the execution post prep. The easiest part honestly in many cases is running the training. The hardest part is looking at the results of the training and realizing you have to do a better job.
What is machine learning:
Machine learning is about finding patterns in data. These patterns are found through stats. Then it makes predictions on new inputs.
If you train a machine learning model, the goal is that by the end of the training you know what inputs specifically to give this model to have it run the prediction based on all of its learning on different patterns.
Unlike generative AI: In the world of Gen AI you now have a text box for everything. So you can input whatever you want wherever you want and whether or not it's right or not it will generate something. The spectrum to how good it generates that thing is really contingent on how good you prompted. That's where we go back to prompt engineering.
In the world of ML: There's no prompt engineering. There's no natural language. Everything is in the form of numbers or elements that you put in.
You have categorical features and you have discrete features. Categorical would be what color is your car? Blue, green, black. That's a categorical feature.
A continuous feature: How many miles or kilometers does your car have in terms of mileage? That's a different type of feature.
When you have an ML model, if you want to be able to use the ML model, you need to input exactly every single parameter that you've set it up to ingest to run that prediction.
So it's inflexible. It's not malleable like something like Gen AI where you can go to ChatGPT, dump whatever you want, and get some outcome whether or not it's helpful or not.
Gen AI is about generating. Machine learning is about predicting.
Can you use Gen AI to predict things? Absolutely. Just typically at scale, at a certain level of security, you still want to use machine learning. And it's way more affordable for now.
At hyper scale when we talk about billions of customers where micro decisions have to be made: Good example is if you go apply for a credit card limit increase, there's no Gen AI happening behind the scenes today. And I don't think there will be Gen AI for the next three to five years. Let's say three years.
The three types of machine learning:
If you leave this video with three words, I'm happy: Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement.
The most difficult one is reinforcement. That's the one that I probably am the worst at from a creating code standpoint because there's a lot of math in here. It's really hard to understand reinforcement learning without the math underlying it.
Reinforcement learning in practice: Used very widely in Teslas. When one Tesla in the world hits a dog (hits a husky, God forbid my husky), it now takes that footage and then it runs that through its training network so that tomorrow when all the Teslas update or wake up, they now have this new added data point.
One Tesla in the world hitting this husky that thought because it snowed and the road was black and the husky was black and white and there was snow all over the place—it was not able to properly determine it. So it got a slap on the hand and that slap on the hand is the equivalent of punishment for a non-emotionally-feeling algorithm.
Hopefully the next time another Tesla across the world encounters that exact same scenario, there's a higher likelihood that if it was running on autopilot, its ABS system (automatic braking system) would stop the car before that actually happens again.
That's a small example of what reinforcement learning looks like and sounds like.
Supervised learning breakdown:
With supervised you have classification and regression.
Classification: Good example is should this person be approved for a mortgage? Yes or no? Let's assume it's not how much the mortgage should be, it's yes or no. That would be classification.
The key word here is you are classifying one of two possible realities: True, they should be approved. Or false, they shouldn't be approved.
Regression: This is about how much. So it's a continuous amount. You wouldn't say is Mark going to make $100,000 in the agency this month? Sure that could be an example. But the better question would be: How much money based on the number of leads they've had this month in their inbox as well as the number of folks in the community asking for custom development?
How much revenue could they generate based on past performance from the prior months where the prior months would be that training data?
Supervised learning explained:
Supervised learning is learned from labeled examples. Supervised means that you take a bunch of data and you label some form of outcome.
You can't just predict randomly with supervised learning should someone be approved for a mortgage or not. You should have tens of thousands, ideally hundreds of thousands if not millions of past examples where you have approved a mortgage and someone ended up defaulting or not making their payments and the inverse where someone was approved and they ended up being on time current all the way maybe to fruition of completing that mortgage.
Once you have an aggregate of all of the profiles of these people:
When I worked on a mortgage application approval algorithm back in the day, the core things was: Is this person this age group? Are they between 25 to 35, 35 to 45, etc.?
Naturally age is a good proxy for net worth, at least back in the old world where the economy made sense.
Other factors:
- Their occupation, what they did for a living
- The neighborhood they were buying or trying to get approved in
- Their salary per year (obviously super important)
- Their credit score
You can imagine all of these different factors that go into this little concoction or stirring pot of different elements that could give this mathematical algorithm or these algorithms the ability to create associations and understand what are the most determining factors you wish for approval and successful approval.
The spam example: If you ever go to your email and you go to your spam box, that spam is machine learning. It's supervised.
It's seen billions of emails across time and it's seen billions of people add things manually to spam. It has this constant training and constant information that's streaming in where it can get better and better over time.
Unless you're on Outlook.
Unsupervised learning:
You have things like customer segmentation. This is something we used a long time ago. I did a project for Coca-Cola. They had billions of customers.
They wanted to create some segments to understand: If we had to create buckets of our different customers and give them a name and give them some form of description—like these are the lazy soda drinkers, these soda drinkers tend to order and drink six cans of cola a day seven days a week, they are between 40 to 49.
A lot of this had to be done by doing things like clustering where you couldn't or you wouldn't want to go through billions of rows of information to be able to find these similar characteristics with associated rules to bucket them into different compartments.
The clustering wouldn't be able to label in natural language what's happening. It would find and it does find associations between different groups.
You and I have no clue why it's decided that. It's a lot more mathematical but it's black box to you and I when we look at the results.
It's only when you have rich enough data and enough clusters where it makes sense. A cluster means a group, it's that bucket.
Where you as the human being now look at that data and say: Okay what is common between everything that this ML algorithm said is similar to each other?
Now you have the blessing of Gen AI to layer this on top of machine learning to have Gen AI sift through the rows and then come to the conclusion on its own.
Why traditional ML still dominates:
Cost: Sometimes 1000 times cheaper than Gen AI for specific tasks.
Reliable: It has accuracy, it scales to billions of predictions per day for a pretty fixed cost. Once you have the understanding and learning from the machine learning algorithm, finding the patterns, every time you hit it it's very minimal.
Imagine you had something like an AWS Lambda function or some way to hit this understanding and you give it these inputs over and over again. You can hit a million times and pay 20 to 50 bucks a month. You can scale this to billions and maybe you have a couple hundred dollars a month at really hyper scales versus all kinds of Gen AI solutions.
Really helpful for things like:
- Fraud detection
- Credit scoring
- Algorithmic trading
- Product recommendations
When you go on Amazon right now outside of the chatbot where you can write using it, you can actually see when you have suggestions for buying one thing versus the other—these product recommendations are machine learning.
Search ranking: Still on Google, even though Google's nuking it with AI mode.
Health care: For a lot of imaging, especially radiology, very common that they don't use these image models that are cutting edge. Because again imagine you're running this at scale. Each patient has like 15 different X-ray images and then you multiply that by thousands of patients, especially for a smaller clinic—this wouldn't be affordable.
Using things like deep learning you could do really good, really proper, pretty performant image recognition as well.
Operations: Imagine factories, manufacturing companies. They have some IoT components to those factories that detect whether or not the machine is going to have some form of defect or have some form of event after a million runs on the factory line or the assembly line.
The old workflow vs now:
The workflow back in 2020:
- Manual data cleaning yourself using custom code yourself
- Hand engineering features
- Writing training code from scratch
- Manual model tuning
- Building your own deployment infrastructure
There's a lot of frameworks that have come out since 2020 when I was at the peak of applying the skill set that make this a lot easier now.
Spoiler alert: I will have an application of this specific series where I do this all in Claude Code to show you how you can go from creating the algorithm to cleaning the data to deploying the machine learning algorithm if you want to be able to deploy it and use it in production to monitoring it—doing all of this within Claude Code.
Because all of this stuff has now been abstracted away in terms of the core difficulty using Gen AI to write the code for you.
We had to not only understand this, know how to write it by hand, spend days to weeks on errors that were super simple to fix if you understand what you're doing (which many times I didn't).
The modern stack:
You have different frameworks that also let you run different types of machine learning algorithms at once.
Different types of algorithms in regression or classification:
- Decision trees (think of it like a literal decision tree)
- Random forest (the equivalent of decision trees but many trees in that forest)
- Different versions of random forest called XGBoost (a universe of many different forests, not just a single tree)
You can keep layering on different types of algorithms. Usually each one has its own syntax of how you run it, has its own syntax of how you prepare data for that specific thing.
There are now basically layers of abstraction that let you take data and then use something like AutoML. What AutoML does is you have a lot of different libraries that exist and it runs tons of different algorithms on the exact same data.
So you can see of the 10, 15, 20 different algorithms you could use, which one is probably the best for this specific situation.
That's where we live now: One click deployment and built-in monitoring versus you having to figure out how do I monitor and evaluate everything by hand.
Machine learning vs Gen AI:
Machine learning: You have clear input and you have a very predictable output.
Gen AI: It is generative. Prompt engineering can increase the likelihood that you get what you want, but because it's more flexible and it requires reasoning across different contexts, there's no fixed output format.
It's very rare that you can get the exact same output that's not a one word or one line answer over and over again very reliably.
Google Colab recommendation:
I would recommend—this is completely free to use by the way: colab.research.google.com
What's cool is back in the day you could pay to get access to a basic GPU. I had to pay let's say 20 bucks a month back in 2019, 2020 to get access to an A100.
Now you could use out of the box for a limited amount of time like a T4 GPU. As your data scales, let's say you have millions of rows and you're doing a lot of computation on them, you can use GPUs and it's not expensive to upgrade to Colab Pro where you can use even more.
Obviously as you get to more production level systems you would ditch this completely and you would run this in something like AWS or Azure or GCP.
Live example - Customer churn classification:
I used the toggle Gemini feature in Google Colab. In this case I gave it an idea for creating a customer churn prediction model.
The setup:
- It imports Pandas (basically the equivalent of an Excel table, the OG Excel table—now there's a version called Polars which is way more efficient in Python)
- NumPy (just think of it as a framework that can do math stuff in Python)
- Creates 1000 rows of synthetic business-oriented data
- Monthly spend, tenure months, support calls, contract type
- Tracking churn (yes or no to whether a customer has churned)
The preprocessing: This is really important. When you pre-process information for machine learning you have what are called training and test sets.
If I gave you a million rows of information about this customer churn data set and you wanted to create an ML algorithm, theoretically to give enough ammo for the math to find the patterns, you have to give enough information for it to go through and see all these examples.
You would let's say of that million maybe give half a million to have the AI or the machine learning algorithm train on, taking all the patterns in that data set.
Then you would maybe keep the other half, that other half a million, to test. You kind of have it study and then the exam is data it's never seen before.
Because you want to be able to understand: Has the algorithm memorized its findings from the patterns or has it actually understood, has it created some form of system of understanding or pattern recognition?
Overfitting vs underfitting:
Overfitting is memorizing the data. When I give you data you've never seen before and it's a brand new pattern, it will be a very non-performant model. Its accuracy will be awful. Its metrics will be awful.
In my native tongue we say this phrase that translates to: He has memorized, he has not understood. Meaning that person clearly doesn't know what they think they know. They just know it by repetition. Which are two different things.
You could probably imagine if you were studying back in grade seven or high school and you thought you were ready for an exam because you practiced the same 10 problems 100 times. When you go to the exam and there's a brand new problem, even if it's a slight curveball on what you've studied, you wouldn't do that well sometimes unless you're really good at on the spot abstraction.
Same thing with ML. You need to spoon feed it enough examples where it can extrapolate its understanding outwards and be able to apply it.
The train/test split:
Typically the split is up to you. In this case it's training on 80% of the 1000 rows which was created and then the unseen (the exam to see whether it memorized or understood) is the 20%.
The shape of your data has to be what it expects. If it's not the right shape, nothing else will work.
This is one example of where I would spend days at the very beginning when this stuff came out understanding why is this not working? Like why does this number have to be the same as this number?
You now have to worry about that less and you now have the ability to ask Claude to explain every single thing here step by step as you wish.
ML is why the planning and pre-processing is the hard part. Running the machine learning once everything is manicured and beautiful is much easier.
The results - Logistic regression:
This is where we run what's called logistic regression. If you don't know what that is, just math. You can look up what that looks like behind the scenes.
It trains it on our very small data set. Now this is the exam. You're predicting on X test, which is the exam of unseen data.
There's cool libraries in Python which is why the world of ML can be a lot lighter than Gen AI because there is right or wrong. There is a very specific spectrum of how good a model is performing. You can really lock that down really well. It's not very opinionated. It's pretty cut and dry in most cases until you get to natural language processing (which is very similar to Gen AI in terms of the fact that it's not as black and white, there is some gray sometimes).
The metrics:
You have things like accuracy, you have precision, you have recall. These are all out of one. If it's one then it's great but it's unlikely that it's legit.
This example was 50% accurate, 55% precise, recall is 46%—they're all in the same range.
This F1 score is basically what's called the harmonic mean. It's not the average, it's a version of the average of precision and recall over two.
You get this little matrix. This is a very common matrix in machine learning. This is what we always look at as data scientists to denote how well is this algorithm performing.
This zero alludes back to the zero in our label. We call this the label or the target. This is what makes supervised learning supervised. We care to predict this.
We want to know and be able to predict how many zeros (people that did not churn) or ones (people that did churn).
In this case:
- Zero class: We're not very accurate there
- One class: We're slightly better at understanding which customers will churn versus which customers won't churn
Which sounds counterintuitive maybe to some of you. But depending on what it is you're optimizing for, you want to be very intentional.
The cancer example - Why accuracy can be trash:
Just because there are all of these metrics doesn't mean that all of these metrics matter.
Let's take cancer. Cancer is a problem where it's much more important that you, when you say someone has cancer, that they indeed have cancer versus not.
Accuracy kind of doesn't matter. If you have 100 people and only one person had cancer, technically there's a very high odd that the algorithm would predict correctly that 99 plus don't have cancer.
But in this case the algorithm is 99% right, 100% wrong. Because the thing that we care about is the fact of actually detecting the person that had cancer correctly. That's what matters.
That's where something like accuracy in many cases is trash. In our normal day to day language, accuracy is a proxy for good or really really good.
But precision is much more important. Of the people that do have cancer—now we're taking the denominator of people from 100 to that one person. Let's say now there's four people in that 100 group that had cancer.
We now worry about: Of the four people that we know have cancer, how many did we get right? That is the metric that matters.
If that one is 50-50 you don't have a very good machine learning algorithm, especially if it's lower than that.
Accuracy could kind of be like a monkey with a dart, a dartboard, closing their eyes with a blindfold and they just throw it. Most likely they'll be right because 99% of people wouldn't have cancer in that small subsample I gave you.
That's where it's really important to be able to understand. Now you have the luxury and the blessing of having generative AI to lead you through this.
Regression example - Predicting continuous values:
Let's say we don't want a binary will they churn or not churn. We want to make this into a regression task meaning it's continuous.
Now we want to ask a different question. We load the same data. Now for regression we re-prep the data again.
Now we're not doing logistic regression, we're doing linear regression. Many of you would be familiar with what that is.
We're trying to predict on a new target. This target, because we need it to be continuous meaning non-binary, we're taking monthly spend. Let's try to predict the monthly spend.
You need to make sure: If you have a training monthly spend you have to remove it out. When you go to the exam you don't want it to be looking at the exam with the answer to the questions right beside it.
You have the same data set but you have to make sure to drop the monthly spend you're trying to predict on because then it's redundant if you are predicting but you have the answer right beside you.
Regression metrics:
When you deal with regression you have different metrics to estimate how good a model is versus classification.
We have things here called:
- Mean absolute error
- Mean squared error
- Root mean squared error (this is the one I care about a lot)
There'll be a separate lecture on this if you care to understand what these are. No I'm not gonna put equations on screen even though some of you might be chomping at the bit for that.
This is the visualization of the actual versus the predicted spend of the churned customers.
Clustering example - Unsupervised learning:
Clustering is when you take a series of data, there's no target. The only target is we want the algorithm to create groups and understanding from in this case the churned customer data.
It pre-processes the data. We see all these little negatives. This is basically understanding of different clustering results. It's all mathematical end of the day.
Anything that is a number or numerical nature will be computed in what's called one-hot encoded categorical features. So it converts the words to equivalent numbers that math can actually process and understand.
The elbow method:
There is a way to also give some directionality because unsupervised is unsupervised—it's going to just yolo results if you don't give it some boundaries.
This thing's called the elbow method and the elbow method lets you estimate (not with 100% accuracy) probably how many different groups are there in your data that way you could probably segment it.
Usually you'd want to read this and say: Based on where this is going down in terms of the metric called inertia, maybe we probably have five good groups. It's really where the graph really takes its first bend.
It could be four, it could be five, and usually you'd run it with four, you'd run it with five, maybe even run it with six groups and see how good the results are.
The first time this bends at all even slightly is three. So math-wise it would be three but I always would be nosey enough to try all these different methods.
K-means clustering:
This is doing a very basic version of clustering. Even within clustering you have many versions of a clustering algorithm. It's using a very common one called k-means. It's imperfect. There are many that are much more comprehensive.
In this case we come up with saying that there are three main groups and we ask the AI to go and tag everyone that it thinks fits into a group of its own.
Very confusingly, zero is a group. So it starts at 0, 1, 2. So those are the three groups: Group 1, group 2, group 3.
Then we have the data tagged with the cluster and it would be your job as a data scientist to be able to go and see what are the trends between the different clusters.
Now you have the blessing of Gen AI again to feed this data and say: Can you come up with a full report as to explaining what are the characteristics of people in cluster 1, cluster 2, cluster 0?
Obviously you have AI here giving you a good breakdown and you can visually explore what these clusters look like.
Reading clustering plots:
When you look at a clustering plot, you want to see as little overlap as possible. The fact that we have like 1 and 2 here on top of each other, we'd have to inspect the data to see: Are they actually that different?
The perfect data sets is where you can find literally circles that are mutually exclusive.
Deep learning preview - Neural networks:
If you ever wanted to see what deep learning looks like, like a neural network, taking the same data set you would process it a little bit differently.
You'd have to import all kinds of libraries. There's a very common one that's called TensorFlow.
If I were to try to even explain this mathematical matter basically I would lose you. But essentially with neural networks you have different layers.
These are the different layers we're abstracting. You have some form of inputs. The way it gets processed is very different from the other algorithms I've showed you thus far.
You have what are called hidden layers. This is meant to mimic the way the human brain works. You see something and you get stimuli. That stimuli is propagated to different parts of your mind.
What happens is there's a whole thing called back propagation or forward propagation where the same inputs are not just taken linearly in the system. They go to some output and then they get abstracted back. That's what it's called: Back propagation, where it tries to fully understand and comprehend what's happening.
The reasoning model analogy:
If I were to give you an awful analogy (this is awful, I'm really diluting the concept): When you go from a non-reasoning model to a reasoning model (we're talking about generative AI by the way), the reasoning model has a chance to look at your prompt and go back and reevaluate a couple times before the final answer.
It runs the prediction on your prompt, has an output, takes output, re-evaluates it according to the prompt. Is it still good? Eh let's fix a couple things. Then it goes back.
That process is very similar to what's happening here. It's really what's inspired by this. It's our ability as humans to take input or stimuli and run it through our brain to come up with the result of how we should interpret what to do next, what it is depending on the problem we're trying to solve.
That's what's happening here with these different layers.
Epochs explained:
When you create the neural network, eventually this is how you compile a network. You would run and you'd try to fit the data and you'd run it over epochs.
Epoch is very similar to like an epoch—it's a fixed period of time where the data is being inputted into the neural net. During the neural net it has a certain number of samples. Every single time it's going through these epochs.
It's going through your test data in this case and then it's evaluating. Every single step that you take in this network it's trying to create some distillation of its understanding of what it is you're trying to predict.
You have different metrics like validation accuracy, you have validation loss. You'll notice as you go from number one to number 20, the numbers are very similar. The loss is pretty stable.
You want this ideally to be as close to zero as possible. 0.7 out of one is pretty trash because this is a proxy for mistakes that it's making which is also a proxy for understanding.
If this accuracy is pretty trash (it's almost 50-50) and this validation loss is also very high, this tells you that this deep learning network hasn't really digested or understood any concept whatsoever. It has not abstracted any of the data that you've provided it.
Which is why you'd go to evaluate the model and this should be all garbage. Yes it is trash across the board. This would be an unusable deep learning model.
But there's a very different structure and a different way of training it versus what I showed you before.
Training vs real world performance:
This is a good example. In training we got like 60% accuracy. When we went in the real world here on the exam, our accuracy was much lower. Much lower.
That shows you between memorizing versus in production what's happening.
Model loss in training almost went to zero which is what we want—we want as little error as possible with all the evaluations it's going through. But what we actually got in the real world is tons of error.
That is no bueno.
Why deep learning is last resort:
If you had a tool set of things that you should do, deep learning is almost always the absolute last thing you should do.
It's the most black box meaning I can explain a lot of things of how this is working but there is an element where I can't tell you exactly why it's doing well versus not well.
A lot of times it's just a lack of data. You need way more data, way more options, maybe many more layers of thinking.
Maybe it shouldn't be just like two layers. I've had deep learning algorithms we've built that go like 50 layers and different versions of those layers and different iterations of those layers to make it work.
But at the end of the day you can't fully explain deep learning.
The Deloitte strategy:
Back in the day what would happen is you'd have a company like Deloitte that would go build machine learning models for enterprises.
Deep learning typically can perform the best once you get it perfectly. They would push it through its limits. They would use deep learning to show what is mathematically possible for accuracy.
Let's say they're doing image recognition for radiology cancer, lung disease that you're trying to detect. They would use deep learning to see: How close can we get for the certain algorithm to perform very well?
Once they understood the ceiling of what performance looked like, they would try every single way to use much more explainable algorithms to get the same outcome.
So sometimes you use deep learning to show you what's possible from a performance standpoint but you do your best to not use deep learning because it's:
1. Not very explainable
2. There's a portion that's black box
Which is why now that this is evolved into our current world today of Gen AI, there's times where you can show me a prompt, I can run the same prompt as you, and it can work two different ways sometimes very different randomly (which is called the hallucination).
We can't fully explain hallucinations. We can guess, we can pattern match, but that's a part of the reason why.
Deep learning has always been a weapon that is the place of last resort unless you were doing something that was like image recognition where it does make sense to use more deep learning versus everything else, at least back in the day.
Reinforcement learning example - The maze:
Let's get to the most convoluted one but the coolest one. Visualize a maze here where:
- Zero represents a path
- One represents a wall
- Two denotes the starting position of an agent
- Three denotes the exit or the goal
In reinforcement learning you have this objective function. An objective function is literally: What is the objective of the agent?
This is where the word agent comes from and where it was legit. Now we use agents for everything from a chatbot to an n8n workflow.
The maze setup:
All the ones are walls. Zero is the path. Three is the goal—this is Valhalla, this is where we want to end up, this is where the agent should end up.
Two is the starting point of the agent. This is where we're starting or where it's starting and this is where it wants to end up. The ones are walls and we need to find a way to get it to the promised land.
I already lost track of how to get there but this is the path. You have different options. You can go this way or this way. You have a luxury there. But this is where you start, this is where you end. You have to stop hitting the walls.
Q-learning (the OG agent):
This thing is called Q-learning. This is essentially what's happening when you create—this is the OG of an agent.
You know how agent mode can go on a webpage and look for something and take the next steps? This is the OG version of agent mode. But it's mathematical agent mode.
It trains the agent on what's a wall, what's not a wall. It shows you and visualizes the performance, tests it, and just cleans everything up.
This is now all abstracted away from you. This is completely free to use by the way: colab.research.google.com
The reward system:
This is the example of the reward system:
- When it hits the wall: Bad bad bot or bad agent (sometimes you can make a reward not just one or zero, you can make it super negative)
- Wrong path: Maybe we give a negative reward of negative one
- Wall or invalid: Negative 10
- Exit: Golf clap, you get 100 (maybe we make it even 1000 positive points)
To go back to my Tesla example: If a Tesla on autopilot hits a dog it should be like negative 10 billion. It should really stand out to the algorithm that you really mess things up. This is awful.
It's as simple as that at least conceptually. The agent will go through the environment and then tell us exactly what it's doing. Start position and then it'll go up, down, right and we'll be able to see what it looks like.
These are parameters that you would set. Like deep learning there's a mathematical way to explain what parameters are most commonly set but there's no for sure—if you use these parameters it'll work every single time.
It has to go through training. Like a child it has to hit its head, understand from what's happening.
The training process:
It tells you: How many episodes it went through. How many times did the agent try to escape the maze? Many times over.
In the span of 10 minutes it could be 10,000 times. Which is why if you've ever seen AlphaGo or any of the revolutionary reinforcement learning applications, the value is the fact that you and I, when we build something, it takes us half an hour.
But this can attempt something millions of times in that same scope or same allocation of time.
Reading the results:
This is a plot that if you knew what you're looking for you'd be able to read to see how many good rewards to negative rewards did it get in each episode from zero to a thousand.
One thing you'll notice is that it did some level of learning. Eventually at the beginning it sucked. You can see it accumulated hundreds of negative rewards meaning it slapped its head around the wall multiple multiple times or took the wrong path multiple times.
Eventually it got a lot better. This is where we go from zero to non-zero:
- Anything above the line is good
- Anything below is no good
This took around 200 iterations to stop sucking as hard in terms of getting out of the maze.
That's a little preview of reinforcement learning.
The reality check:
Can we go deeper? Can we—we can always go deeper. I could dedicate this whole community, I could make a new community about machine learning.
But one, I care about making this actionable for you. It's not about mentally being aware of this stuff. It's knowing that depending on the problem, 90% of the time you'll never talk about deep learning and you'll never talk about reinforcement learning.
It might be cool for you to know about it but anything at the beginning (supervised and unsupervised) are the main things that you would default to.
What's coming next:
Next day we're gonna talk about how to evaluate things. We'll talk about those metrics a lot more deeply. And then we'll go from there.
Eventually: Full Claude Code implementation showing you how to go from creating the algorithm to cleaning data to deploying to monitoring—all within Claude Code.
Next episode: Evaluation metrics deep dive - Why accuracy lies and precision matters (the cancer detection problem).

lessons.description:
with Vercel's Agent Browser 🚀Vercel's Agent Browser - Best UI Testing Tool Yet (way less tokens)
🧪 New UI Testing Method: A new agentic browser approach enables automated, hands-on UI testing for apps and interfaces, improving how agent-driven browser validation is performed.📉 Token Efficiency Evolution: Compared to earlier tools like Playwright, Chrome MCP, and Claude-in-Chrome, each iteration has reduced token usage, with the new Agent Browser by Vercel being the most efficient so far.⚙️ How Agent Browser Works: The tool captures a single high-level screenshot, reflects on what actions to take, and then interacts with the UI—avoiding excessive screenshot spam and unnecessary token consumption.🧩 Seamless Cloud Code Integration: Agent Browser can be cloned from GitHub, installs dependencies automatically, and includes a reusable skill stored in the .cloud root folder for persistent, repeatable use.🚀 Real-World Validation Power: In live testing, the agent autonomously interacted with UI elements, verified physics-based behaviors, and provided observations—delivering the most advanced and practical UI verification workflow currently available.

Prompt-to-HTML Tailwind Renderer
lessons.description: This n8n workflow turns a user’s ?query= prompt into a fully rendered, Tailwind-styled HTML page and returns it immediately via the webhook. It first calls OpenAI Chat Completions with a strict JSON Schema (Structured Output) to produce a UI component tree, then a second OpenAI step converts that JSON into {title, html}. Finally, it wraps the result in an HTML template (injecting the Tailwind CDN) and responds with Content-Type: text/html—perfect for rapid prototypes, forms, and simple landing pages.

lessons.title: Voice Cloning Tools Comparison
lessons.description:
We had DeepSeek for Text, and now we have Minimax for Voice 🗣️
Minimax offers fast voice cloning (under 10 seconds) and high-quality text-to-speech capabilities, mirroring ElevenLabs in both functionality and UI design.Voice model options like Speech02HD, O2 Turbo, and O1HD provide users with customizable controls for speed, pitch, and volume, delivering impressive vocal output.Test sentences were generated to compare tonality and vocal clarity, with results comparable to ElevenLabs but at a much lower cost.Minimax provides a robust developer experience, including an API, WebSocket support, and an MCP server for real-time applications—matching or exceeding ElevenLabs’ backend offerings.It poses a serious challenge to ElevenLabs and OpenAI, especially if those companies don’t evolve beyond their current (often beta-stuck) product ecosystems.
